# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 — Definition
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

> **Q1.** Define *Artificial Intelligence*. List any **two** characteristics of an intelligent agent.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Artificial Intelligence is the branch of computer science that deals with developing systems capable of performing tasks that typically require human intelligence, such as perception, reasoning, learning, and decision-making. As per **Russell and Norvig (2020)**, AI is the study of *rational agents* that select actions maximising a performance measure based on percepts.
>
> **Two Characteristics (1 mark):**
> 1. **Reactivity** — The agent responds in a timely fashion to changes in its environment.
> 2. **Pro-activeness / Goal-Directedness** — The agent does not merely react but takes the initiative to achieve its goals.

---

### Question 2 — Short Conceptual
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

> **Q2.** What is the **Turing Test**? Mention its major limitation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Turing Test (2 marks):** Proposed by *Alan Turing* in 1950 in his paper *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"*, the Turing Test (also called the *Imitation Game*) is a method of inquiry to determine whether a machine can exhibit intelligent behaviour equivalent to, or indistinguishable from, that of a human. A human judge engages in a text-based conversation with both a human and a machine; if the judge cannot reliably identify the machine, the machine is said to have passed the test.
>
> **Major Limitation (1 mark):** The test only evaluates linguistic behaviour, ignoring *embodiment*, *physical intelligence*, and *understanding* — the *Chinese Room argument* by John Searle (1980) showed that mere symbol manipulation does not imply true comprehension.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse *Turing Test* with *CAPTCHA* — CAPTCHA is a *reverse* test designed to tell humans from bots.
> 2. Do **not** write "AI = robots" in definition questions; AI is a *cognitive* discipline; robotics is one of its *applications*.
> 3. In historical questions, always **date** milestones (e.g., "Dartmouth 1956" not just "Dartmouth").
> 4. When asked for *types of AI*, mention **all four** classification axes (capability, functionality, learning, reasoning) for full marks.
> 5. Always include the **formula** in formula-based questions, not just the explanation.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Question A]**

> **Q.A (a)** Define the **Turing Test** in detail. Describe the rules of the *Imitation Game* and explain the **Chinese Room argument** as a critique of the Turing Test. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.A (b)** Discuss the **historical evolution** of Artificial Intelligence from 1943 to 2024, highlighting at least **six milestones** and the causes of the **AI winters**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 marks

**Definition of the Turing Test (2 marks):**
The Turing Test, proposed by **Alan Turing in 1950** in his paper *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"* (published in the philosophical journal *Mind*), is an empirical, behaviourist method to evaluate machine intelligence. It is also known as the **Imitation Game**.

**Rules of the Imitation Game (2 marks):**
- Three players: a **Human (H)**, a **Machine (M)**, and a **Judge (J)**.
- All communication is **text-only** (no voice, video, or physical presence) to remove irrelevant cues.
- The judge interrogates both H and M via typed questions.
- The judge's task is to identify which is the human and which is the machine.
- The machine passes the test if the judge is **fooled at least 30% of the time** (Turing's threshold).

**The Chinese Room Argument — John Searle, 1980 (3 marks):**
- Imagine a person locked in a room, receiving Chinese symbols as input.
- The person uses a rule-book to map input symbols to output symbols, producing a *convincing* Chinese response.
- An outside observer believes the room "understands" Chinese.
- However, the person inside does *not* understand Chinese — they merely manipulate symbols.
- **Conclusion:** Purely formal symbol manipulation does **not** constitute *understanding* or *intelligence*. Therefore, even if a machine passes the Turing Test by behaviour, it may not truly be intelligent.
- This argument challenges the *behaviourist* foundation of the Turing Test and is one of the most cited philosophical rebuttals in AI.

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 marks

**Historical Evolution (6 marks — 0.5 per milestone, plus linking sentence):**

1. **1943 — McCulloch-Pitts Neuron:** First mathematical model of an artificial neuron; foundation of neural networks.
2. **1950 — Turing's Paper:** Proposed the Imitation Game and asked *"Can machines think?"*
3. **1956 — Dartmouth Conference:** *John McCarthy* coined the term *"Artificial Intelligence"*. Considered the birth of AI as a formal discipline.
4. **1969 — Shakey the Robot:** First mobile robot to combine perception, reasoning, and action (developed at SRI).
5. **1997 — Deep Blue:** IBM's chess engine defeats world champion *Garry Kasparov* — first major public AI victory.
6. **2012 — AlexNet:** *Geoffrey Hinton's* team wins ImageNet using deep CNNs, igniting the deep-learning revolution.
7. **2016 — AlphaGo:** *DeepMind's* program defeats Go champion *Lee Sedol* using deep RL + Monte Carlo tree search.
8. **2022 — Generative AI Era:** *ChatGPT* and *GPT-4* bring LLMs to the mainstream; foundation models enter robotics for planning.

**AI Winters — Causes (1 mark):**
- **First AI Winter (1973–1980):** Triggered by the *Lighthill Report* in UK and DARPA funding cuts. Cause: *over-promised* symbolic AI failed to scale; computational limits prevented neural nets from training.
- **Second AI Winter (1987–1993):** Expert systems market collapsed; they were *brittle*, *expensive to maintain*, and could not handle uncertainty. Funding evaporated.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Question B]**

> **Q.B (a)** Explain the **four classification axes of AI** with examples. Why is *Hybrid AI* the most relevant approach for modern robotics? **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.B (b)** Describe the **rational agent model** in detail. With the help of a block diagram, explain how a rational robot agent operates in an environment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 marks

**The Four Classification Axes (6 marks — 1.5 each):**

1. **Based on Capability:**
   - **Narrow AI (Weak AI):** Performs a single specific task. Example: *Siri*, *chess engine*, *industrial welding robot*.
   - **General AI (AGI):** Human-level intelligence across any task. *Theoretical; not yet achieved.*
   - **Super AI:** Surpasses human intelligence in all domains. *Hypothetical.*

2. **Based on Functionality:**
   - **Reactive Machines:** No memory; respond only to current input. Example: *IBM Deep Blue*.
   - **Limited Memory:** Uses recent history. Example: *self-driving cars* (e.g., *Tesla Autopilot*).
   - **Theory of Mind:** Understands human emotions, beliefs, intentions. *Research stage.*
   - **Self-Aware AI:** Conscious of self. *Hypothetical.*

3. **Based on Learning Approach:**
   - **Supervised Learning:** Learns from labelled $(x, y)$ data. Example: *image classification*.
   - **Unsupervised Learning:** Finds patterns in unlabelled data. Example: *clustering robot trajectories*.
   - **Reinforcement Learning:** Learns by reward/penalty. Example: *robot gait learning*.
   - **Transfer Learning:** Reuses pre-trained models. Example: *fine-tuning BERT for HRI*.

4. **Based on Reasoning Style:**
   - **Symbolic (GOFAI):** Uses explicit logic rules. Example: *STRIPS planner*.
   - **Connectionist (Neural):** Learns from data via networks. Example: *CNN for object detection*.
   - **Hybrid:** Combines both. Example: *neuro-symbolic reasoning in autonomous vehicles*.

**Why Hybrid AI for Robotics (1 mark):**
Modern robots require both **interpretable high-level reasoning** (symbolic planning for tasks, safety rules) and **robust low-level perception** (neural networks for vision, speech). Hybrid AI provides *both*, which is why it is the dominant paradigm in 2024 robotics research (e.g., *PaLM-E*, *RT-2*).

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 marks

**Rational Agent Model (3 marks):**
A *rational agent* (Russell & Norvig definition) is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through sensors and acting upon it through actuators. It selects the action that **maximises its expected performance measure** given its percept sequence.

**Mathematical Formulation (1 mark):**

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} \; E\left[\, PM \mid percept_{t}, memory_{t-1}, KB_{t-1} \,\right]$$

where:
- $a^*$ = optimal action
- $A$ = action space
- $PM$ = performance measure
- $KB$ = knowledge base
- $E$ = expectation operator

**Block Diagram of Rational Robot Agent (3 marks):**

The agent cycle is: **Percept → Interpret → Reason → Act → Learn → Update KB → repeat**.

Key components:
- **Sensors:** Cameras, LIDAR, IMU, microphones.
- **Percept Interpreter:** Converts raw sensor data to symbolic/variable form.
- **Knowledge Base:** Stores world model, rules, learned policies.
- **Reasoning / Inference Engine:** Uses logic, search, or neural inference to decide next action.
- **Actuator Interface:** Translates action decisions to low-level motor commands.
- **Learning Module:** Updates the KB based on feedback / reward signals.
- **Performance Measure:** Externally evaluates the agent's success.

**Real-world example (1 mark):** A *vacuum-cleaning robot* perceives room layout via LIDAR, reasons about which regions are dirty, plans an optimal path, and learns to avoid obstacles over time — fulfilling the rational agent cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — AI Introduction (Module 1):**

- **AI Definition** — AI = science of making machines perform tasks requiring human-like intelligence (perception, reasoning, learning, action).
- **Dartmouth 1956** — Official birth year; term coined by *John McCarthy*.
- **Turing 1950** — Proposed the *Imitation Game*; asked *"Can machines think?"*
- **Turing Test Pass Condition** — Judge fooled > 30% of the time (Turing's 1950 prediction).
- **CAPTCHA** — *Reverse* Turing test; distinguishes humans from bots.
- **Chinese Room (Searle, 1980)** — Strongest critique: symbol manipulation ≠ understanding.
- **AI Winter 1 (1973)** — Lighthill Report; over-hyped symbolic AI failed.
- **AI Winter 2 (1987)** — Expert systems collapsed; brittle and unscalable.
- **Deep Blue (1997)** — IBM beats Kasparov; first public major AI victory.
- **AlexNet (2012)** — Deep learning revolution begins.
- **AlphaGo (2016)** — Reinforcement learning masters Go.
- **ChatGPT (2022)** — Generative AI enters mainstream; LLMs become robot planners.
- **Rational Agent Formula** — $a^* = \arg\max_a E[PM \mid percept]$.
- **Bayes' Theorem** — $P(h \mid e) = P(e \mid h) P(h) / P(e)$ — foundation of robot localisation.
- **Shannon Entropy** — $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ — measures uncertainty.
- **Four AI Classification Axes** — Capability / Functionality / Learning / Reasoning.
- **Hybrid AI** — Symbolic + Neural; dominant paradigm in modern robotics.
- **Foundations of AI** — Philosophy, Math, Economics, Neuroscience, Psychology, Linguistics, Control Theory, Computer Engineering.
- **Intelligent Agent Criteria** — Percept, Reason, Learn, Plan, NLU, Problem Solve, Generalise.
- **Robot AI Stack** — Perception → Reasoning → Planning → Action → Learning (closed loop).
- **KTU Must-Memorise Names** — Turing, McCarthy, Minsky, Newell, Simon, Hinton, Bengio, LeCun, Russell, Norvig, Pearl, Searle, Shannon, Samuel.
- **KTU Must-Memorise Dates** — 1943, 1950, 1956, 1969, 1973, 1980, 1987, 1997, 2012, 2016, 2022.
- **Avoid in Exams** — Confusing AI with robotics; calling *narrow AI* as *strong AI*; missing dates.
- **Engineering Connection** — Every modern robot (autonomous vehicles, surgical bots, warehouse robots, humanoids) uses *all seven* AI pillars, not just one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Areas of AI - Natural Language Processing, Vision and Speech Processing, Robotics, and Expert Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Application Areas of AI - Natural Language Processing, Vision and Speech Processing, Robotics, and Expert Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Application Areas of AI** refer to the broad, interdisciplinary domains in which Artificial Intelligence techniques are deployed to build systems that can perceive, reason, learn, and act. In the KTU 2024 scheme for *AI & Machine Learning for Robots*, these areas are classified into five core pillars: **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, **Computer Vision**, **Speech Processing**, **Robotics**, and **Expert Systems**. Each of these pillars represents a *modality* through which an intelligent system (often an autonomous robot) interacts with the world. The unifying goal is to transform raw, unstructured input — text, pixels, sound waves, sensor readings, or domain knowledge — into structured, actionable decisions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "AI Humanoid" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The AI as a Human Intern** > > Imagine hiring a highly capable intern who can read (NLP), see (Vision), listen (Speech Processing), move and manipulate the physical world (Robotics), and reason like a senior consultant (Expert Systems). Each of the five application areas is essentially a *specialized skill set* of this intern. A real AI system may not have all five, but the most powerful autonomous systems (e.g., a humanoid robot like *Pepper* or *Sophia*) integrate several of them in parallel. ## 1.3 The Five Pillars — At-a-Glance Definitions | # | Application Area | Core Capability | Typical Input | Typical Output | |---|------------------|------------------|----------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** | Understanding & generating human language | Text, sentences, documents | Intent, sentiment, translation, summary | | 2 | **Computer Vision (CV)** | Interpreting visual imagery | Pixels, images, video | Object labels, depth maps, pose | | 3 | **Speech Processing** | Decoding & synthesizing spoken audio | Audio waveforms | Text, speaker ID, synthesized voice | | 4 | **Robotics** | Acting in the physical world | Sensor data (LIDAR, IMU, cameras) | Motor commands, trajectories | | 5 | **Expert Systems** | Emulating human expert reasoning | Facts, rules, queries | Diagnoses, recommendations, decisions | ## 1.4 Why These Five Areas Matter for Robotics > [!IMPORTANT] > In a robotic system, these five pillars map directly onto the classical **Sense → Plan → Act** paradigm: > - **Sense** ← Vision + Speech + NLP (perception stack) > - **Plan** ← Expert Systems + ML reasoning > - **Act** ← Robotics control systems > > This integration is the foundation of modern **embodied AI** — the subject of this entire course (PEBRT755). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Natural Language Processing (NLP) **Definition:** NLP is the branch of AI concerned with the interaction between computers and human (natural) languages. It combines computational linguistics with statistical and deep learning models. ### Sub-tasks of NLP - **Morphological Analysis** — breaking words into roots/affixes. - **Syntactic Analysis (Parsing)** — constructing grammatical parse trees. - **Semantic Analysis** — extracting meaning. - **Pragmatic / Discourse Analysis** — understanding context across sentences. - **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — identifying people, places, dates. - **Sentiment Analysis** — classifying polarity (positive/negative/neutral). - **Machine Translation** — e.g., English → Malayalam. - **Question Answering (QA)** — chatbots, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). - **Text Summarization** — extractive vs. abstractive. > [!NOTE] > **Robotics Link:** Service robots use NLP to receive voice/text commands such as *"Bring me a glass of water from the kitchen."* The robot parses this into a sequence of sub-tasks (locate, grasp, navigate, deliver). ### Key Metric in NLP - **Perplexity (PPL)** — measures how well a probability model predicts a sample. $$ \text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^{-1/N} = \sqrt[N]{\prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1})}} $$ Lower perplexity = better language model. ## 2.2 Computer Vision (CV) **Definition:** CV enables machines to derive meaningful information from digital images, videos, and other visual inputs. It automates tasks that the human visual system can do. ### Sub-tasks of CV - **Image Classification** — assigning a label to an image. - **Object Detection** — locating objects with bounding boxes. - **Image Segmentation**: - *Semantic Segmentation* — class per pixel. - *Instance Segmentation* — class + instance per pixel. - **Pose Estimation** — detecting human/robot joint positions. - **Optical Flow** — estimating motion between frames. - **3D Reconstruction / Depth Estimation** — from 2D images or stereo. - **Face Recognition & Tracking**. ### Key Metrics in CV - **IoU (Intersection over Union):** $$ \text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} $$ where $A$ = predicted bounding box, $B$ = ground-truth bounding box. Value range: $[0, 1]$, higher is better. - **mAP (mean Average Precision)** — standard metric for object detection (e.g., COCO benchmark). ## 2.3 Speech Processing **Definition:** Speech processing deals with the analysis, interpretation, and synthesis of spoken language. It sits at the intersection of signal processing, acoustics, and AI. ### Sub-tasks - **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)** — speech → text. - **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** — text → speech waveform. - **Speaker Identification / Verification** — *"who is speaking?"* - **Speech Emotion Recognition** — detecting affective state. - **Keyword Spotting / Wake-Word Detection** — *"Hey Robot"*. - **Noise Robustness & Beamforming** — for far-field robot microphones. ### Key Metric - **Word Error Rate (WER):** $$ \text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N} $$ where $S$ = substitutions, $D$ = deletions, $I$ = insertions, $N$ = total words in reference. Lower is better. ## 2.4 Robotics **Definition:** Robotics is the branch of AI and engineering concerned with the design, construction, operation, and application of autonomous or semi-autonomous mechanical systems that can perform tasks in the physical world. ### Sub-branches - **Industrial Robotics** — fixed manipulators (welding, painting, pick-and-place). - **Mobile Robotics** — AGVs, AMRs, drones (UAVs). - **Humanoid Robotics** — bipedal anthropomorphic platforms. - **Medical Robotics** — surgical assistants (e.g., Da Vinci). - **Swarm Robotics** — coordinated multi-agent systems. - **Soft Robotics** — compliant, flexible-bodied robots. - **Autonomous Vehicles** — self-driving cars. ### Key AI Techniques Used in Robotics - **SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping). - **Path Planning** — A*, Dijkstra, RRT, PRM. - **Reinforcement Learning** — for locomotion and manipulation. - **Imitation Learning** — learning from human demonstrations. - **Computer Vision** — for visual servoing and object grasping. ### Key Metric - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** $$ \text{DOF} = \text{Number of independent movements a robot can perform} $$ A 6-DOF arm can position its end-effector at any (x, y, z) location with any orientation (roll, pitch, yaw). ## 2.5 Expert Systems **Definition:** An expert system is an AI program that emulates the decision-making ability of a human expert in a specific, narrow domain. It was the dominant paradigm of AI in the 1970s–1990s and remains important in industrial diagnostics. ### Architecture 1. **Knowledge Base** — stores domain facts and rules. 2. **Inference Engine** — applies logical rules to the knowledge base. 3. **Knowledge Acquisition Module** — interface for experts to add knowledge. 4. **User Interface / Explanation Facility** — explains *why* a conclusion was reached. 5. **Working Memory** — stores current problem facts. ### Representation Schemes - **Rule-based (Production Rules):** `IF condition THEN conclusion` - **Frames & Objects** - **Semantic Networks** - **Fuzzy Logic Systems** — handle uncertainty. > [!NOTE] > **Robotics Link:** Expert systems are used in robot *fault diagnosis* (e.g., NASA Deep Space Network), *mission planning*, and *high-level task scheduling* in industrial robotic cells. ### Key Property — The **Explanation Facility** The hallmark of an expert system is its ability to *trace the chain of reasoning*. This is absent in pure black-box neural networks, which is a key conceptual difference examiners love to test. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet | Area | Core Input | Core Output | Defining Metric | Real-World Robot Example | |------|------------|-------------|------------------|---------------------------| | NLP | Text | Intent / Translation | Perplexity ↓ | Chatbot-controlled service robot | | Computer Vision | Image / Video | Labels / Depth / Pose | IoU, mAP ↑ | Autonomous car obstacle detection | | Speech Processing | Audio waveform | Text / Voice | WER ↓ | Voice-commanded home assistant robot | | Robotics | Sensor streams | Motor commands | DOF, accuracy | Industrial arm, humanoid | | Expert Systems | Facts / Rules | Diagnosis / Plan | Rule coverage | Robot cell fault-diagnosis system | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These five areas rarely operate in isolation. Modern humanoid robots (e.g., *Tesla Optimus*, *Figure 01*, *Boston Dynamics Atlas*) fuse CV (eyes) + Speech (ears) + NLP (language understanding) + Robotics (body) + Expert Systems (planning) into a single *multimodal embodied agent*. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — The Multimodal Robot Perception Pipeline Since this is a conceptual/introductory topic, the "derivations" here are systematic breakdowns of how a robot fuses the five application areas in a real task: **"A service robot receives a spoken command, navigates to an object, and grasps it."** ## 3.1 The Integrated Pipeline — Step-by-Step We model the robot's operation as a sequential chain of AI modules. ### Step 1 — Speech Acquisition (Speech Processing) The robot's microphone array captures an analog audio signal. $$ x(t) = s(t) + n(t) $$ where $s(t)$ is the clean speech signal and $n(t)$ is ambient noise. **Logic:** We first separate speech from background noise using beamforming. ### Step 2 — Speech-to-Text (ASR) The cleaned audio is fed to an ASR model that produces text. $$ \text{ASR}: \quad x_{\text{clean}}(t) \;\longrightarrow\; W = \{w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N\} $$ **Logic:** Acoustic model + language model together output the most probable word sequence. ### Step 3 — Natural Language Understanding (NLP) The text is parsed into a structured intent. $$ \text{NLU}: \quad W \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Intent}(I) + \text{Slots}(S) $$ **Example:** *"Bring me a glass of water"* → - Intent $I$ = `DELIVER_OBJECT` - Slots $S$ = {`object: glass`, `liquid: water`, `recipient: speaker`} **Logic:** Semantic parsing + slot-filling extract the *who/what/where*. ### Step 4 — Expert System Planning The structured intent is converted to a task plan. $$ \text{Plan} = \text{Plan}(I, S) = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4\} $$ where $T_1$: locate kitchen, $T_2$: find glass, $T_3$: pick glass, $T_4$: navigate to user. **Logic:** The inference engine applies *production rules* such as: > `IF Intent = DELIVER_OBJECT AND object ∈ {kitchen_items} THEN subtask = NAVIGATE(kitchen)`. ### Step 5 — Vision-Based Perception (Computer Vision) As the robot moves, its RGB-D camera streams frames. $$ I_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3} $$ An object-detection network (e.g., YOLO) outputs bounding boxes: $$ \text{CV}: \quad I_t \;\longrightarrow\; \{(b_i, c_i, s_i)\}_{i=1}^{K} $$ where $b_i$ is the bounding box, $c_i$ the class label, $s_i$ the confidence score. ### Step 6 — Robotics Action The selected bounding box's centroid is transformed into robot coordinates, and an inverse-kinematics solver computes joint angles. $$ \theta = f^{-1}(x_{\text{desired}}, y_{\text{desired}}, z_{\text{desired}}) $$ The robot's motors execute: $$ \tau = M(\theta)\ddot{\theta} + C(\theta, \dot{\theta})\dot{\theta} + G(\theta) $$ (standard manipulator dynamics equation, where $\tau$ is torque, $M$ the inertia matrix, $C$ Coriolis terms, and $G$ gravity). ### Step 7 — Feedback Loop A success signal (e.g., weight sensor detects the glass) is sent back to update the expert system's working memory. $$ \text{Status} = \text{EXECUTE}(T_i) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{update}(WorkingMemory) $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Pipeline Steps to AI Areas | Pipeline Step | Primary AI Area | Key Algorithm | Key Metric | |---------------|------------------|----------------|-------------| | Audio capture | Speech Processing | Beamforming | SNR (dB) | | Speech → Text | Speech Processing | ASR (CTC/Transformer) | WER | | Text → Intent | NLP | Intent classifier, slot filling | Intent accuracy | | Intent → Plan | Expert Systems | Inference engine, planning | Plan success rate | | Image → Object | Computer Vision | CNN / YOLO / DETR | mAP, IoU | | Plan → Motion | Robotics | IK, SLAM, RL | Pose error (m) | | Status update | Expert Systems | Rule firing | Cycle time (s) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** When examiners ask *"How do the application areas of AI integrate in a robot?"*, this exact **Sense (Speech+CV+NLP) → Plan (Expert System) → Act (Robotics)** narrative is the highest-scoring answer. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Interconnection of AI Application Areas ```mermaid graph TD A1[Human Language]:::input A2[Visual World]:::input A3[Spoken Audio]:::input A4[Sensor Data]:::input A5[Domain Knowledge]:::input B1[NLP Module]:::proc B2[Computer Vision Module]:::proc B3[Speech Processing Module]:::proc B4[Expert System Engine]:::proc B5[Robotics Controller]:::proc C1[Intent and Text Output]:::out C2[Object Labels and Depth]:::out C3[Transcribed Text]:::out C4[Diagnosis and Plan]:::out C5[Motor Commands]:::out D1[Embodied Autonomous Robot]:::robot A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> D1 A2 --> B2 --> C2 --> D1 A3 --> B3 --> C3 --> D1 A5 --> B4 --> C4 --> D1 A4 --> B5 --> C5 --> D1 B1 -. informs .-> B4 B2 -. informs .-> B4 B3 -. informs .-> B1 B4 -. commands .-> B5 classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef robot fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Expert System Architecture (Block Flow) ```mermaid graph LR F1[Human Expert]:::expert F2[Knowledge Engineer]:::expert K1[Knowledge Base
Facts and Rules]:::kb F3[End User]:::user KA[Knowledge Acquisition Module]:::mod IE[Inference Engine]:::mod WM[Working Memory]:::mod UI[User Interface]:::mod EX[Explanation Facility]:::mod F1 --> K1 F2 --> KA --> K1 K1 --> IE WM --> IE IE --> EX IE --> UI F3 --> UI UI --> WM classDef expert fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef kb fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef mod fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef user fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Sense-Plan-Act Paradigm for a Robot ```mermaid graph LR SP1[Microphone]:::sense SP2[Camera]:::sense SP3[LIDAR]:::sense SP4[IMU]:::sense P1[Speech Recognizer]:::plan P2[Object Detector]:::plan P3[SLAM]:::plan P4[Expert Planner]:::plan A1[Wheel Driver]:::act A2[Arm Controller]:::act A3[Gripper]:::act SP1 --> P1 --> P4 SP2 --> P2 --> P4 SP3 --> P3 --> P4 SP4 --> P3 --> P4 P4 --> A1 P4 --> A2 P4 --> A3 classDef sense fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef plan fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef act fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** > - **Diagram 1** shows how all five application areas converge into the robot. > - **Diagram 2** shows the *internal* structure of an expert system (a frequent KTU short-answer topic). > - **Diagram 3** maps sensors → AI modules → actuators, i.e., the **Sense → Plan → Act** loop. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **"List any three application areas of AI and give one real-world example for each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three application areas are: 1. **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** — Example: Google Translate converting English to Malayalam. 2. **Computer Vision** — Example: Tesla Autopilot detecting pedestrians and lane markings. 3. **Robotics** — Example: Amazon's warehouse robots autonomously transporting shelves. *(Award 1 mark per area + 1 mark for the example. Total 3 marks.)* ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper] **"Differentiate between an Expert System and a Neural Network."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Expert System | Neural Network | |---------|----------------|----------------| | Knowledge representation | Explicit rules (IF-THEN) | Implicit weights in connections | | Reasoning | Symbolic, traceable | Sub-symbolic, black-box | | Explanation facility | Yes (shows rule chain) | No (typically opaque) | | Data requirement | Few rules, domain expertise | Large labeled datasets | *(1 mark for the comparison axis, 2 marks for paired correct points. Total 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of an **Expert System** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each component. **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss how **Natural Language Processing** is used in modern service robots. Include in your answer the sub-tasks of NLP and at least one metric used to evaluate NLP systems. #### Model Solution — Part (a) An expert system is an AI program that emulates a human expert in a narrow domain. Its architecture consists of: 1. **Knowledge Base (2 marks):** Stores domain-specific facts and rules in the form of production rules: `IF THEN `. Example: `IF (fever = yes) AND (cough = yes) THEN (possible_disease = flu)`. 2. **Inference Engine (2 marks):** The "brain" that applies the rules to the working memory. It uses two strategies: - *Forward Chaining* — data-driven (from facts to conclusions). - *Backward Chaining* — goal-driven (from hypothesis to supporting facts). 3. **Working Memory (1 mark):** Temporary storage for the current problem's facts and intermediate conclusions. 4. **User Interface & Explanation Facility (1 mark):** Allows the user to query the system and receive not just the answer but also *why* that answer was chosen (the chain of rules fired). 5. **Knowledge Acquisition Module (1 mark):** Lets domain experts (e.g., doctors, engineers) add or modify rules without writing code. *(A neat labelled block diagram fetches an additional 1 mark, but here we describe it textually. Total = 7 marks.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) NLP enables service robots to understand and respond to human language. The sub-tasks are: 1. **Tokenization & Morphological Analysis** (1 mark) — splitting input into words and identifying roots. Example: *"Bring me water"* → `[Bring, me, water]`. 2. **Syntactic Parsing** (1 mark) — building a parse tree to identify the grammatical subject, verb, and object. This determines that *the robot* is the actor and *water* is the object. 3. **Semantic Analysis** (1 mark) — extracting meaning. *"Water"* is mapped to the entity `liquid:beverage` in the robot's ontology. 4. **Intent Classification & Slot Filling** (2 marks) — mapping the utterance to an action template: - Intent: `DELIVER` - Slot: `object = water, destination = user_location` 5. **Dialogue Management & Response Generation** (1 mark) — the robot replies: *"Sure, fetching water for you."* **Evaluation Metric — Perplexity (1 mark):** $$ \text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^{-1/N} $$ A perplexity of 20 on a held-out test set indicates a strong language model. Lower is better. **Real-world example (additional 0.5 mark):** Amazon's voice-ordering system, SoftBank's Pepper robot, Apple Siri. --- ### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the major **sub-tasks of Computer Vision** used in robotic perception. Explain the **IoU metric** with its formula. **(b) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast **Speech Processing** and **Natural Language Processing** as AI application areas. State one evaluation metric for each. #### Model Solution — Part (a) Computer vision sub-tasks used in robotic perception are: 1. **Image Classification (1.5 marks):** Assigning a single label to an entire image. Robot use: identifying whether a tile is *cracked* or *intact* in a quality-control line. 2. **Object Detection (1.5 marks):** Locating multiple objects with bounding boxes and class labels. Robot use: a warehouse robot detecting boxes on a shelf (YOLO, Faster R-CNN). 3. **Semantic Segmentation (1.5 marks):** Classifying each pixel. Robot use: an autonomous car marking road pixels vs. sidewalk pixels. 4. **Pose Estimation (1.5 marks):** Detecting keypoints/joints. Robot use: a humanoid robot imitating a human's arm position for imitation learning. **IoU Metric (1 mark):** $$ \text{IoU} = \frac{\text{Area of Overlap}}{\text{Area of Union}} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} $$ If a predicted box overlaps 80% of the ground truth, with 10% extra area, then $\text{IoU} = 0.8 / (0.8 + 0.1 + 0.1) = 0.8$. IoU ≥ 0.5 is typically considered a "correct detection" in the PASCAL VOC benchmark. *(Total = 7 marks.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) | Feature | Speech Processing | Natural Language Processing | |---------|-------------------|------------------------------| | **Input modality** (1.5 marks) | Acoustic waveform (continuous, time-series) | Text (discrete symbols) | | **Core task** (1.5 marks) | Speech ↔ Text conversion (ASR / TTS), speaker ID, emotion | Understanding, generating, translating text | | **Pipeline** (1.5 marks) | Signal processing → acoustic model → language model → text | Tokenization → parsing → semantic analysis → intent | | **Dependency** (1 mark) | Often the *front-end* of an NLP pipeline (voice assistants) | Can be input or output of speech systems | | **Evaluation Metric** (1.5 marks) | **WER** = $\frac{S + D + I}{N}$ | **Perplexity** = $P(W)^{-1/N}$ | **Key insight (bonus 0.5 mark):** A voice-enabled robot uses Speech Processing to *acquire* language and NLP to *understand* it. They are sequential in a multimodal robot. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warnings > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in this topic:** > 1. **Confusing Speech Processing with NLP.** Speech = audio → text. NLP = text → meaning. They are *sequential*, not the same. > 2. **Forgetting the Explanation Facility of an Expert System.** It is the *defining* feature that distinguishes an expert system from a black-box ML model. > 3. **Listing application areas without giving a real-world example.** KTU valuators award 1 mark per example in Part A. > 4. **Writing the IoU formula with the `|` symbol inside a markdown table.** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table. > 5. **Not mapping areas to robotics.** This course is *AI for Robots* — every answer should ideally end with a robotics link. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Five AI application areas:** NLP, Computer Vision, Speech Processing, Robotics, Expert Systems. - ✅ **NLP** = text understanding; metric = **Perplexity (↓)**; robotics use = command parsing. - ✅ **Computer Vision** = image/video interpretation; metric = **IoU (↑), mAP (↑)**; robotics use = object detection, SLAM. - ✅ **Speech Processing** = audio ↔ text; metric = **WER (↓)**; robotics use = voice control. - ✅ **Robotics** = physical-world actuation; key concept = **DOF**; AI techniques = SLAM, RL, path planning. - ✅ **Expert Systems** = rule-based reasoning; key components = **Knowledge Base, Inference Engine, Working Memory, Explanation Facility, Knowledge Acquisition Module**. - ✅ **Architecture pattern:** *Sense (Speech+CV+NLP) → Plan (Expert System) → Act (Robotics)*. - ✅ **Real-world robotics fusion:** Humanoids (Pepper, Atlas, Optimus) integrate all five areas into a *multimodal embodied agent*. - ✅ **Evaluation mind-map:** NLP → Perplexity · CV → IoU/mAP · Speech → WER · Robotics → DOF, pose error · Expert Systems → rule coverage, explanation quality. - ✅ **Exam tip:** Always close your answer with a one-line robotics application to score full marks in PEBRT755.

topics.title: Learning - Forms of Learning, Supervised Learning Algorithms, Unsupervised Learning Algorithms, and Reinforcement Based Learning (Agent, Environment, Action, State, Reward)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Learning - Forms of Learning, Supervised Learning Algorithms, Unsupervised Learning Algorithms, and Reinforcement Based Learning (Agent, Environment, Action, State, Reward)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Learning in the Context of AI/ML?

**Machine Learning (ML)** is formally defined as a subfield of artificial intelligence that enables systems to automatically learn patterns, relationships, and decision-making policies from data and experience, **without being explicitly programmed** for every possible scenario. The learning process involves optimizing a mathematical objective function (loss or reward) over a dataset of observations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Learning is the process of converting experience (data or interaction) into expertise (a model or policy) that generalizes to unseen situations."

In the context of **robotics (PEBRT755)**, learning refers to a robot's ability to improve its perception, control, planning, and decision-making over time by observing sensor data, executing actions, and receiving feedback from the environment.

## 1.2 Forms of Learning — The Grand Taxonomy

Machine Learning paradigms are primarily classified into three foundational forms, each defined by the **type of feedback signal** available to the learner:

| **Form** | **Feedback Signal** | **Goal** |
|---|---|---|
| Supervised Learning | Labeled (input, output) pairs | Learn a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ |
| Unsupervised Learning | Unlabeled data only | Discover hidden structure $\mathcal{P}(X)$ |
| Reinforcement Learning | Scalar reward signal | Learn an optimal policy $\pi^*(a \vert s)$ |

A fourth emerging paradigm, **Semi-Supervised Learning**, sits between supervised and unsupervised approaches, while **Self-Supervised Learning** powers modern foundation models.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies — "Learn Like a Human"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Analogy: A Child Learning in Three Ways**

**1. Supervised Learning — Learning with a Teacher**
Imagine a child learning to identify fruits. The teacher shows an apple and says *"This is an apple"*, shows a mango and says *"This is a mango"*. After seeing thousands of (image, label) pairs, the child generalizes and can identify unseen fruits.
- **Robot Analogy:** A robotic arm is shown 10,000 images of screws (labeled "tighten") and bolts (labeled "loosen"). It learns to classify fasteners.

**2. Unsupervised Learning — Self-Discovery**
The same child is dumped into a basket of mixed fruits with **no labels**. The child naturally groups them by color, size, and shape — discovering categories without a teacher.
- **Robot Analogy:** A mobile robot maps an unknown building by clustering LiDAR points into rooms, corridors, and obstacles **without prior labels**.

**3. Reinforcement Learning — Learning by Trial and Error (like training a pet)**
A puppy is given a treat when it sits on command, and scolded when it jumps. Over time, the puppy learns the *policy*: "sit when owner says 'sit'". The puppy is the **agent**, the living room is the **environment**, "sit/jump" are **actions**, the puppy's body posture is the **state**, and the treat/scolding is the **reward**.
- **Robot Analogy:** A humanoid robot learns to walk. Each step that doesn't fall is rewarded (+1); a fall is penalized (-100). Through millions of trials, the robot learns a walking **policy**.

## 1.4 Defining the Key Terms in Reinforcement Learning

The Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework is built on five foundational entities. These terms are **non-negotiable** in KTU examinations:

> [!NOTE]
> **RL Axioms — The AEAST Mnemonic**
> 1. **Agent (𝒜):** The decision-making entity (the robot, the algorithm, the puppy). It is the *learner* and *actor*.
> 2. **Environment (𝓔):** The external world with which the agent interacts. It accepts actions and returns new states and rewards. In robotics, the environment is the **physical world** plus sensor models.
> 3. **Action ($a \in \mathcal{A}$):** The set of all possible moves the agent can make at any given time. For a mobile robot, $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{forward, backward, left, right, stop}\}$.
> 4. **State ($s \in \mathcal{S}$):** A complete description of the environment at time $t$, denoted $s_t$. The Markov property requires that $s_t$ contains all information relevant to the future.
> 5. **Reward ($r \in \mathbb{R}$):** A scalar feedback signal $r_{t+1}$ indicating how *good* the action $a_t$ was in state $s_t$. The agent's objective is to maximize the **cumulative discounted reward** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$, where $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the discount factor.

## 1.5 Why These Three Paradigms Matter for Robotics

In **PEBRT755 (AI & ML for Robots)**, the choice of learning paradigm is dictated by the *availability of supervision* and the *nature of the task*:

- **Classification/Regression tasks** (e.g., object detection, grasp prediction) → Supervised Learning.
- **Exploration and clustering** (e.g., SLAM, scene segmentation) → Unsupervised Learning.
- **Sequential decision-making** (e.g., locomotion, manipulation, autonomous driving) → Reinforcement Learning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Supervised Learning — The "Teacher" Paradigm

### 2.1.1 Formal Setup

Given a **training dataset** $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where $x^{(i)} \in \mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ is the input feature vector and $y^{(i)} \in \mathcal{Y}$ is the corresponding target label, supervised learning seeks a hypothesis function $h_{\theta}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ parameterized by $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p$ that minimizes a chosen loss function.

The two principal problem types are:
- **Classification:** $\mathcal{Y} = \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ (discrete labels).
- **Regression:** $\mathcal{Y} \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ (continuous values).

### 2.1.2 The Supervised Learning Pipeline

The operational flow consists of five stages:
1. **Data Collection:** Gather labeled samples $(x, y)$.
2. **Hypothesis Selection:** Choose a model class (linear, tree, neural net).
3. **Loss Definition:** Quantify prediction error.
4. **Optimization:** Adjust $\theta$ to minimize empirical risk.
5. **Generalization Evaluation:** Test on held-out data to estimate true risk.

### 2.1.3 Core Supervised Algorithms (KTU-Listed)

**a) Linear Regression**
- Model: $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^T x = \sum_{j=0}^{d} \theta_j x_j$
- Loss: Mean Squared Error (MSE)

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

- **Normal Equation (closed-form):**

$$\theta^* = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

- **Gradient Descent Update (iterative):**

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$$

**b) Logistic Regression**
- Model: $h_{\theta}(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}$
- Loss: Binary Cross-Entropy

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - h_{\theta}(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

**c) k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)**
- Non-parametric, instance-based. For query $x_q$, predict the majority label of its $k$ closest training points under a distance metric $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ (commonly Euclidean).

**d) Decision Trees (CART / ID3)**
- Split criterion: Information Gain (entropy-based) or Gini impurity.

$$\text{Gini} = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k^2$$

$$\text{Entropy}(S) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2 p_k$$

**e) Support Vector Machines (SVM)**
- Find maximum-margin hyperplane. Optimization problem:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y^{(i)}(w^T x^{(i)} + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i$$

- Kernel trick: $K(x, x') = \phi(x)^T \phi(x')$, with common choices being RBF and polynomial.

**f) Neural Networks (Deep Learning)**
- Universal approximators composed of layers of neurons with nonlinear activations.
- Backpropagation uses the chain rule to compute gradients of $J(\theta)$ w.r.t. each weight.

## 2.2 Unsupervised Learning — The "Self-Discovery" Paradigm

### 2.2.1 Formal Setup

Given only an unlabeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$, unsupervised learning seeks to uncover the underlying structure $\mathcal{P}(X)$. There is **no supervisory signal**; the model is judged by intrinsic criteria such as compactness, separation, or reconstruction fidelity.

### 2.2.2 Core Unsupervised Algorithms

**a) k-Means Clustering**
- Objective: Partition $N$ points into $k$ clusters $C_1, \dots, C_k$ minimizing within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS).

$$J_{\text{k-means}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

- Iterative algorithm: (1) Initialize centroids $\mu_k$; (2) Assign each point to nearest centroid; (3) Recompute centroids as cluster means; (4) Repeat until convergence.

**b) Hierarchical Clustering (Agglomerative)**
- Builds a dendrogram by iteratively merging the two closest clusters based on a linkage criterion (single, complete, average, Ward's).

**c) DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)**
- Defines clusters as dense regions separated by sparse regions. Parameters: $\varepsilon$ (neighborhood radius) and $MinPts$ (minimum points).
- Points are classified as **core**, **border**, or **noise**.

**d) Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**
- Linear dimensionality reduction via eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N} X^T X$.
- Top-$k$ principal components = top-$k$ eigenvectors of $\Sigma$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d$.
- Explained variance ratio: $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$.

**e) Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)**
- Assumes data is generated from a mixture of $K$ Gaussians; uses Expectation-Maximization (EM) to fit parameters.

**f) Association Rule Mining (Apriori Algorithm)**
- Discovers frequent itemsets and rule patterns (e.g., market basket analysis).

## 2.3 Reinforcement Learning — The "Trial and Error" Paradigm

### 2.3.1 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) Formalism

An RL problem is mathematically modeled as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$:

- $\mathcal{S}$: state space.
- $\mathcal{A}$: action space.
- $P(s' \vert s, a) = \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \vert S_t = s, A_t = a)$: transition probability.
- $R(s, a, s') = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \vert S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s']$: reward function.
- $\gamma \in [0, 1]$: discount factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Markov Property:** The future depends only on the current state, not the history. Formally,
> $$\Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \vert S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t-1}, A_{t-1}, \dots) = \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \vert S_t = s, A_t = a)$$

### 2.3.2 Core RL Quantities

**a) Policy ($\pi$):** A mapping from states to action probabilities.
- Deterministic: $\pi(s) = a$.
- Stochastic: $\pi(a \vert s) = \Pr(A_t = a \vert S_t = s)$.

**b) State-Value Function:** Expected return starting from $s$ and following $\pi$.

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ G_t \vert S_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \vert S_t = s \right]$$

**c) Action-Value Function (Q-function):** Expected return of taking action $a$ in state $s$, then following $\pi$.

$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \vert S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$

**d) The Bellman Equations (recursive decompositions):**

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \vert s') Q^{\pi}(s', a') \right]$$

**e) Optimal Value Functions:**

$$V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s), \quad Q^*(s, a) = \max_{\pi} Q^{\pi}(s, a)$$

$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s') \right]$$

### 2.3.3 Exploration vs. Exploitation Dilemma

The agent must balance:
- **Exploitation:** Choose the best-known action $\arg\max_a Q(s, a)$.
- **Exploration:** Try new actions to discover better strategies (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy, softmax/Boltzmann, UCB).

### 2.3.4 Core RL Algorithms

**a) Q-Learning (off-policy, model-free, value-based):**

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

The bracketed term is the **TD-error** (Temporal Difference error):

$$\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

**b) SARSA (on-policy variant):**

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma Q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

**c) Deep Q-Network (DQN):** Replaces the Q-table with a deep neural network $Q(s, a; \theta)$. Stabilization techniques: experience replay and target network.

**d) Policy Gradient (REINFORCE):** Directly parameterizes the policy $\pi_{\theta}(a \vert s)$ and updates via:

$$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a_t \vert s_t) G_t$$

**e) Actor-Critic Methods:** Combines policy gradient (actor) with value function estimation (critic) to reduce variance.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **definitive reference** for KTU ESE numerical and derivation questions on this topic.

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Purpose** | **Units / Range** |
|---|---|---|---|
| MSE Loss (Regression) | $J(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$ | Quantify regression error | $J \geq 0$ |
| Cross-Entropy (Classification) | $J(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_i \left[ y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$ | Quantify classification error | $J \geq 0$ |
| Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \dfrac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Iterative parameter optimization | $\alpha > 0$ (learning rate) |
| Sigmoid Function | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Squash logits to probabilities | Output $\in (0, 1)$ |
| Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_k p_k \log_2 p_k$ | Measure impurity / information | $H \geq 0$, max = $\log_2 K$ |
| Gini Impurity | $G = 1 - \sum_k p_k^2$ | Decision tree split criterion | $G \in [0, 1 - 1/K]$ |
| k-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Cluster compactness | $J \geq 0$ |
| Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative future reward | $G_t \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Bellman Optimality (V) | $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) [R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^*(s')]$ | Recursive optimal value | $V^* \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Q-Learning Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | Tabular TD control | $Q \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| TD-Error | $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$ | Learning signal in TD | $\delta \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Policy Gradient | $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta}(a \vert s) G_t]$ | Score-function gradient | $\nabla J \in \mathbb{R}^p$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Supervised + RL):** CNNs for road-sign detection (supervised), RL for lane-change policies.
- **Warehouse Robots (RL):** Amazon's Kiva robots learn optimal path planning via RL.
- **Industrial Inspection (Unsupervised):** PCA-based anomaly detection on manufacturing sensor data.
- **Medical Robotics (Supervised):** Surgical robots trained on expert surgeon demonstrations (imitation learning, a supervised variant).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **exam-board standard** and must be reproduced with full algebraic transparency in the KTU answer script.

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient Descent Update Rule for Linear Regression

**Goal:** Derive the parameter update $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ for the MSE loss.

**Step 1: Write the cost function.**

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 2: Substitute the linear model $h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = \theta^T x^{(i)} = \sum_{j=0}^{d} \theta_j x_j^{(i)}$.**

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \sum_{j=0}^{d} \theta_j x_j^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_j$.** Apply the chain rule: $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} (u^2) = 2u \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_j}$, with $u = \sum_{j'} \theta_{j'} x_{j'}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}$ and $\frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_j} = x_j^{(i)}$.

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the constant factor.**

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 5: Substitute into the gradient descent rule.**

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

This is the **batch gradient descent** update. Stochastic GD (SGD) replaces the sum over $N$ with a single sample, dramatically reducing per-step compute cost.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bellman Equation for the State-Value Function

**Goal:** Derive $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) [R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$.

**Step 1: Start with the definition of the value function.**

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ G_t \vert S_t = s \right] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \vert S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 2: Split the sum into the immediate reward and the discounted future.**

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+2} \vert S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 3: Recognize that the second term equals $\gamma$ times $V^{\pi}(S_{t+1})$.**

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V^{\pi}(S_{t+1}) \vert S_t = s \right]$$

**Step 4: Apply the law of total expectation, conditioning first on $A_t$ and then on $S_{t+1}$.**

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

This recursive decomposition is the **Bellman expectation equation** — the cornerstone of dynamic programming in RL.

## 3.3 Derivation: Q-Learning Bellman Optimality Update

**Goal:** Derive the Q-learning update $Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \delta_t$.

**Step 1: Start from the Bellman optimality equation for $Q^*$.**

$$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$

**Step 2: Since $P$ and $R$ are unknown in model-free RL, we use sample estimates.** A single transition $(s_t, a_t, r_{t+1}, s_{t+1})$ gives the **sample target**:

$$y_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a')$$

**Step 3: Define the TD-error as the gap between the sample target and the current estimate.**

$$\delta_t = y_t - Q(s_t, a_t) = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

**Step 4: Apply a learning rate $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ to interpolate between old and new estimates** (sample-average update rule).

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \delta_t$$

Expanding:

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow (1 - \alpha) Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') \right]$$

This is the canonical **off-policy TD control** algorithm, proven to converge to $Q^*$ under standard stochastic approximation conditions (Robbins-Monro, all state-action pairs visited infinitely often).

## 3.4 Derivation: k-Means Convergence Bound

**Goal:** Show that the k-means objective $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$ is monotonically non-increasing.

**Step 1: Assignment step.** Fix centroids $\{\mu_k\}$. Each point is assigned to the nearest centroid, which by definition minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares for that point. Thus $J$ does not increase.

$$x \in C_k^* \iff k = \arg\min_{j} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

**Step 2: Update step.** Fix assignments $\{C_k\}$. The optimal centroid minimizing $\sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$ is the **mean** of points in the cluster (setting $\nabla_{\mu_k} J = 0$).

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_k} = -2 \sum_{x \in C_k} (x - \mu_k) = 0 \implies \mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x \in C_k} x$$

**Step 3: Monotonicity.** Since $J$ is bounded below by 0 and non-increasing at each step, the algorithm converges to a (local) minimum in finite iterations.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Learning Paradigms

> [!NOTE]
> This comparative table is **frequently asked** in KTU module examinations as a 7-mark question.

| **Dimension** | **Supervised** | **Unsupervised** | **Reinforcement** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Form** | Labeled $(x, y)$ | Unlabeled $x$ | $(s, a, r, s')$ trajectories |
| **Feedback** | Direct (loss on prediction) | None (intrinsic metrics) | Delayed (scalar reward) |
| **Goal** | Minimize prediction error | Discover structure | Maximize cumulative reward |
| **Output** | Classifier / Regressor | Clusters / Lower-dim rep | Policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ or $Q(s,a)$ |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, Precision, F1, MSE | Silhouette, WCSS, reconstruction | Cumulative reward, success rate |
| **Robotics Use** | Object detection, grasp classification | SLAM, scene segmentation | Locomotion, manipulation, autonomous nav |
| **Example Algorithm** | SVM, CNN, Random Forest | k-Means, PCA, DBSCAN | Q-Learning, DQN, PPO |
| **Key Challenge** | Labeling cost, overfitting | Validation without labels | Sample efficiency, safety |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: One-Step Q-Learning Update

**Problem:** A robot vacuum is in state $s_1$ (battery low). It takes action $a_2$ (return to dock). The current Q-table gives $Q(s_1, a_2) = 5$. It receives reward $r = +10$ and transitions to $s_2$ (charging). The max Q-value at $s_2$ is $\max_{a'} Q(s_2, a') = 8$. Given $\alpha = 0.2$ and $\gamma = 0.9$, compute the new $Q(s_1, a_2)$.

**Step 1: Identify given values.**
- $Q_{\text{old}}(s_1, a_2) = 5$
- $r = 10$
- $\gamma = 0.9$
- $\max_{a'} Q(s_2, a') = 8$
- $\alpha = 0.2$

**Step 2: Compute the TD-target.**
$$y = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_2, a') = 10 + 0.9 \times 8 = 10 + 7.2 = 17.2$$

**Step 3: Compute the TD-error.**
$$\delta = y - Q_{\text{old}}(s_1, a_2) = 17.2 - 5 = 12.2$$

**Step 4: Apply the update.**
$$Q_{\text{new}}(s_1, a_2) = Q_{\text{old}} + \alpha \delta = 5 + 0.2 \times 12.2 = 5 + 2.44 = 7.44$$

**Answer:** $Q_{\text{new}}(s_1, a_2) = 7.44$ — the agent has learned that recharging when battery is low is highly valuable.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Forms of Learning Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Machine Learning] --> branchA1[Supervised Learning]
    rootA --> branchA2[Unsupervised Learning]

    branchA1 --> leafA1a[Classification]

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Supervised Learning Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[Labeled Training Data X and Y] --> step2[Choose Hypothesis Class H]
    step2 --> step3[Define Loss Function L theta]
    step3 --> step4[Initialize Parameters theta 0]
    step4 --> step5[Compute Gradient of J theta]
    step5 --> step6{Convergence Check}
    step6 -- No --> step7[Update theta via Gradient Descent]
    step7 --> step5
    step6 -- Yes --> step8[Trained Model h theta star]
    step8 --> step9[Predict on Test Data]
    step9 --> step10[Compute Evaluation Metrics]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Unsupervised Learning Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    ustart[Unlabeled Data X only] --> uchoice{Objective}
    uchoice --> uclust[Clustering]
    uchoice --> udimred[Dimensionality Reduction]
    uchoice --> uassoc[Association Mining]
    uclust --> ukmeans[k Means Iteration]
    ukmeans --> uassign[Assign Points to Nearest Centroid]
    uassign --> urecomp[Recompute Centroids as Means]
    urecomp --> ucheck{Converged}
    ucheck -- No --> uassign
    ucheck -- Yes --> ufinal1[Cluster Labels]

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Reinforcement Learning Agent-Environment Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    agentNode[AGENT Robot]
    envNode[ENVIRONMENT World]
    stateArrow1[State s sub t] --> agentNode
    agentNode --> actionArrow[Action a sub t]
    actionArrow --> envNode
    envNode --> stateArrow2[New State s sub t plus 1]
    stateArrow2 --> agentNode
    envNode --> rewardArrow[Reward r sub t plus 1]
    rewardArrow --> agentNode

    end
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Q-Learning Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    qlstart[Initialize Q table to zeros] --> qlhyper[Set alpha gamma epsilon]
    qlhyper --> qlouter{For each episode}
    qlouter --> qlinit[Initialize state s]
    qlinit --> qlloop{Episode not done}
    qlloop --> qlchoose[Choose action via epsilon greedy]
    qlchoose --> qltake[Take action a observe r and s prime]
    qltake --> qlupdate[Q s a Q s a plus alpha r plus gamma max Q s prime a prime minus Q s a]
    qlupdate --> qlset[Set s equals s prime]
    qlset --> qlloop
    qlloop -- Done --> qlouter
    qlouter -- All episodes --> qlend[Converged Q star]
```


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define the three primary forms of machine learning with one real-world example for each.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The three primary forms of learning are:
1. **Supervised Learning:** Learns a mapping from labeled input-output pairs. Example: A robot learning to classify defective vs. non-defective products using labeled images.
2. **Unsupervised Learning:** Discovers hidden structure in unlabeled data. Example: A mobile robot clustering LiDAR points to identify distinct rooms without prior labels.
3. **Reinforcement Learning:** Learns an optimal policy by maximizing cumulative reward through interaction. Example: A robotic arm learning to grasp objects via trial-and-error with a sparse success/failure reward.

### **Question 2: Define the five core entities of a Reinforcement Learning system (Agent, Environment, Action, State, Reward).**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
- **Agent:** The decision-maker (e.g., the robot).
- **Environment:** The external world the agent interacts with (e.g., the physical workspace).
- **Action ($a$):** The set of possible moves the agent can execute (e.g., joint torques, wheel velocities).
- **State ($s$):** A complete snapshot of the environment at time $t$ (e.g., robot pose + obstacle map).
- **Reward ($r$):** A scalar feedback signal indicating the immediate goodness of an action (e.g., +1 for successful grasp, -1 for collision).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the three forms of learning in machine learning with suitable examples. Compare them on five key dimensions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the agent-environment interaction loop in Reinforcement Learning. Define the policy, value function, and Bellman equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The three forms of ML are:

**1. Supervised Learning** uses labeled training data $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ to learn a hypothesis $h_{\theta}$. The loss function (e.g., MSE) is minimized via optimization (e.g., gradient descent).
- *Example:* Training a CNN to detect obstacles in camera images using 10,000 labeled bounding boxes.

**2. Unsupervised Learning** uses only inputs $x$ to discover structure such as clusters or low-dimensional manifolds.
- *Example:* k-Means clustering of SLAM features into distinct rooms.

**3. Reinforcement Learning** uses scalar rewards from interaction to learn a policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ that maximizes expected return.
- *Example:* A quadruped robot learning to walk by receiving +1 reward per step without falling.

**Five-Dimensional Comparison:**

| Dimension | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Direct (labels) | None | Scalar reward |
| Data | Labeled | Unlabeled | Trajectories |
| Output | Predictor | Clusters / embedding | Policy |
| Goal | Minimize loss | Find structure | Maximize return |
| Robotics use | Object detection | SLAM, segmentation | Locomotion, control |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Agent-Environment Interaction Loop (Mermaid-style description):**
At each time step $t$:
1. The agent observes the state $s_t$ from the environment.
2. Based on policy $\pi$, it selects an action $a_t$.
3. The environment transitions to a new state $s_{t+1} \sim P(\cdot \vert s_t, a_t)$.
4. The environment emits a reward $r_{t+1} = R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})$.
5. The agent updates its policy to improve future expected return.

**Policy:** A mapping $\pi: \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(\mathcal{A})$ from states to action distributions. Deterministic: $\pi(s) = a$. Stochastic: $\pi(a \vert s)$.

**Value Function:** The expected cumulative discounted reward from state $s$ under policy $\pi$:

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$

**Bellman Equation (Recursive Form):**

$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

This expresses that the value of a state equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state.

### **Question B (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail any TWO supervised learning algorithms. Derive the gradient descent update rule for linear regression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the Q-Learning algorithm. List the steps of the algorithm and explain the role of the exploration-exploitation trade-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Algorithm 1: Linear Regression**
- **Model:** $h_{\theta}(x) = \theta^T x$
- **Loss:** MSE, $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$
- **Update:** $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$

**Algorithm 2: Logistic Regression**
- **Model:** $h_{\theta}(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$ where $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$
- **Loss:** Binary cross-entropy, $J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_i [y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})]$
- **Update:** Same gradient descent form with $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$

**Derivation of Linear Regression Gradient Descent:** (See Section 3.1 for full derivation. Key steps: chain rule on squared error, sum over training examples, multiply by learning rate $\alpha$.)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Q-Learning Algorithm Diagram (see Section 4.5 for Mermaid flow).**

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize $Q(s, a) = 0$ for all $s, a$.
2. Set hyperparameters: $\alpha$ (learning rate), $\gamma$ (discount), $\varepsilon$ (exploration rate).
3. For each episode:
   - Initialize $s$.
   - Repeat until terminal:
     a. With probability $\varepsilon$, choose a random $a$; else $a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a')$.
     b. Take action $a$, observe $r$ and $s'$.
     c. $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]$.
     d. $s \leftarrow s'$.

**Exploration-Exploitation Trade-off:**
- **Exploitation** ($\varepsilon$-greedy arm): Choose the action with the highest known Q-value to maximize immediate reward.
- **Exploration** (random arm): Choose a sub-optimal action to discover potentially better strategies.
- **Balance:** A high $\varepsilon$ favors exploration (slow convergence); low $\varepsilon$ favors exploitation (risk of local optima). Decaying $\varepsilon$ schedules (e.g., $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$) are commonly used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing agent and environment:** The agent is the *learner/decision-maker*, not the world. Many students incorrectly assign reward generation to the agent.
> 2. **Omitting the discount factor in $G_t$:** Full credit requires the discounted form $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$. Writing $G_t = \sum r_{t+k+1}$ without $\gamma^k$ loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up on-policy vs. off-policy:** Q-learning is **off-policy** (uses $\max_{a'}$), SARSA is **on-policy** (uses $a_{t+1}$). Confusing these loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the Markov assumption:** In RL definitions, the Markov property must be stated explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting to add the intercept term:** In linear regression derivations, students forget $x_0 = 1$ for the bias $\theta_0$.
> 6. **Not explaining the TD-error:** Writing the Q-learning update without defining $\delta_t$ as the temporal-difference error is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE**

- ✅ **Three forms of learning:** Supervised (labeled), Unsupervised (unlabeled), Reinforcement (reward-based).
- ✅ **Agent-Environment-State-Action-Reward (AEASR):** The five pillars of RL.
- ✅ **Policy $\pi(a \vert s)$:** The agent's behavior strategy; deterministic vs. stochastic.
- ✅ **Value function $V^{\pi}(s)$:** Expected return from $s$ following $\pi$.
- ✅ **Q-function $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$:** Expected return of taking $a$ in $s$, then following $\pi$.
- ✅ **Bellman equation:** Recursive decomposition $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a)[R + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$.
- ✅ **Bellman optimality:** $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a)[R + \gamma V^*(s')]$.
- ✅ **Q-learning update:** $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- ✅ **TD-error $\delta_t$:** Difference between sample target and current estimate — the learning signal.
- ✅ **Exploration vs. Exploitation:** $\varepsilon$-greedy is the standard balance.
- ✅ **Markov property:** Future depends only on the present state, not history.
- ✅ **MSE Loss:** $J = \frac{1}{2N}\sum(h_\theta(x) - y)^2$ — used in linear regression.
- ✅ **Cross-Entropy Loss:** $J = -\frac{1}{N}\sum[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ — used in logistic regression.
- ✅ **k-Means Objective:** $J = \sum_k \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$ — minimized iteratively.
- ✅ **PCA:** Projects data onto top-$k$ eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.
- ✅ **Supervised algorithms to know:** Linear/Logistic Regression, k-NN, Decision Trees, SVM, Neural Networks.
- ✅ **Unsupervised algorithms to know:** k-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN, PCA, GMM, Apriori.
- ✅ **RL algorithms to know:** Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN, Policy Gradient (REINFORCE), Actor-Critic.
- ✅ **Discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1]$:** Closer to 1 → far-sighted; closer to 0 → myopic.
- ✅ **Convergence guarantees:** Q-learning converges to $Q^*$ if all $(s, a)$ pairs are visited infinitely often and Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha$ hold.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Forms of Learning, Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent and Challenges Motivating Deep Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stochastic Gradient Descent and Challenges Motivating Deep Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)

**Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** is an iterative optimization algorithm used to train machine learning models — particularly neural networks — by updating the model parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ using the gradient of the loss function computed over a **single training example** (or a small randomly selected subset called a *mini-batch*) at each iteration, rather than over the entire training dataset.

The KTU 2024 syllabus formal update rule is:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_k - \epsilon_k \cdot \nabla L(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k; \mathbf{x}^{(i_k)}, \mathbf{y}^{(i_k)})$$

where $\epsilon_k$ is the **learning rate** at iteration $k$, and $i_k$ is the index of the randomly sampled example.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** SGD is a *core optimization* technique underpinning nearly all modern deep learning training procedures. Mastery of its convergence conditions and the practical challenges it faces is **mandatory** for ESE and Module viva questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hiker in the Fog

Imagine you are a **hiker lost on a foggy mountain** at night, and you need to reach the valley floor (the minimum of the loss). You cannot see the full terrain.

- **Batch Gradient Descent** = You have a satellite view. You compute the *exact* slope of the entire mountain in one go and step carefully. It's accurate, but extremely slow because gathering the full map takes time.
- **Stochastic Gradient Descent** = You have a small flashlight. You sample one tiny patch of ground, estimate the local slope, and take a quick step. You wander noisily, but you cover enormous ground quickly and usually reach the valley faster in practice.
- **Mini-batch Gradient Descent** = You use a headlamp (sample 32–256 patches at a time) — balancing accuracy and speed. This is the **de facto standard** in modern robotics and deep learning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The "stochastic" in SGD refers to the **randomness** in the data sampling — not in the algorithm itself. The descent direction is *noisy* but *unbiased* in expectation.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Robotics / Deep Nets) |
|---|---|---|
| Learning Rate | $\epsilon_k$ | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
| Mini-batch Size | $m$ | 32, 64, 128, 256 |
| Epochs | — | 10 to 500 |
| Momentum | $\alpha$ | 0.9 (standard) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Variants of Gradient Descent

The total loss over a training set with $n$ examples is:

$$L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(\boldsymbol{\theta}; \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{y}^{(i)})$$

The **true gradient** of this empirical risk is:

$$\nabla L(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \nabla L(\boldsymbol{\theta}; \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{y}^{(i)})$$

KTU distinguishes three variants based on how many samples are used per update:

| Variant | Samples per Update | Update Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | All $n$ (full dataset) | $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \epsilon \nabla L(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ |
| **Mini-batch GD** | $m$ where $1 < m < n$ | $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \epsilon \nabla L_m(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | $1$ (single example) | $\boldsymbol{\theta} \leftarrow \boldsymbol{\theta} - \epsilon \nabla L^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ |

### 2.2 Why and How — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Shuffle the training data** to remove sample-order bias.
2. **Iterate** $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ until a stopping criterion is met (max epochs, loss plateau, or validation convergence).
3. **Sample** a mini-batch $B_k$ of size $m$ uniformly at random from training data.
4. **Compute** the stochastic gradient $\mathbf{g}_k = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i \in B_k} \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} L(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k; \mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{y}^{(i)})$.
5. **Update parameters** along the negative gradient direction: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_k - \epsilon_k \mathbf{g}_k$.
6. **Decay** the learning rate $\epsilon_k$ using a schedule (step decay, exponential decay, or cosine annealing).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for SGD questions. Memorize the convergence conditions — they are guaranteed to appear in KTU ESE.

| Concept | Equation / Condition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| SGD Update Rule | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_k - \epsilon_k \mathbf{g}_k$ | Foundation of all deep network training |
| Learning Rate Decay (Robbins-Monro) | $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \epsilon_k = \infty$, $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \epsilon_k^2 < \infty$ | Guarantees convergence in expectation |
| Expected Gradient (Unbiased) | $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{g}_k] = \nabla L(\boldsymbol{\theta}_k)$ | Theoretical basis for SGD correctness |
| Variance of Stochastic Gradient | $\mathrm{Var}[\mathbf{g}_k] \approx \sigma^2 / m$ | Smaller mini-batches $\Rightarrow$ noisier updates |
| Momentum Update | $\mathbf{v}_{k+1} = \alpha \mathbf{v}_k - \epsilon \mathbf{g}_k$, $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{k+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_k + \mathbf{v}_{k+1}$ | Accelerates convergence through ravines |
| Hessian-Conditioned Bound | $\Delta L \le \epsilon \mathbf{g}^\top \mathbf{H} \mathbf{g} / 2$ | Loss reduction per step (ill-conditioning effect) |
| Saddle Point Escape | $\mathrm{Prob}(\mathrm{escape}) \to 1$ as $k \to \infty$ with noise | SGD escapes saddle points automatically |

> **Robbins-Monro Conditions** are a **favorite 7-mark KTU question** — know the two inequalities by heart and be ready to **state and interpret** them.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Robotics

- **Robot Perception:** Training CNNs for object detection using LiDAR + camera data.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Policy gradient methods (REINFORCE, PPO) use SGD-style updates.
- **SLAM Back-ends:** Bundle adjustment and pose-graph optimization use variants of gradient descent.
