&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.1(1) + 0.2(0) + 0.3(1) + 0.1 \\ 0.4(1) + 0.5(0) + 0.6(1) + 0.2 \\ 0.7(1) + 0.8(0) + 0.9(1) + 0.3 \\ 0.1(1) + 0.3(0) + 0.5(1) + 0.4 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1.2 \\ 1.9 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply ReLU activation $g(z) = \max(0, z)$.**

$$
a^{[1]} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1.2 \\ 1.9 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix} \quad (\text{all positive, so unchanged})
$$

**Step 4 — Compute hidden layer 2 pre-activation $z^{[2]}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z^{[2]} &= W^{[2]} a^{[1]} + b^{[2]} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.2(0.5) + 0.1(1.2) + 0.4(1.9) + 0.3(1.0) + 0.1 \\ 0.5(0.5) + 0.6(1.2) + 0.1(1.9) + 0.2(1.0) + 0.2 \\ 0.3(0.5) + 0.4(1.2) + 0.5(1.9) + 0.6(1.0) + 0.3 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.10 + 0.12 + 0.76 + 0.30 + 0.10 \\ 0.25 + 0.72 + 0.19 + 0.20 + 0.20 \\ 0.15 + 0.48 + 0.95 + 0.60 + 0.30 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1.38 \\ 1.56 \\ 2.48 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply tanh activation.**

$$
a^{[2]} = \tanh\begin{bmatrix} 1.38 \\ 1.56 \\ 2.48 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.880 \\ 0.914 \\ 0.986 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(Tanh values computed: $\tanh(1.38) \approx 0.880$, $\tanh(1.56) \approx 0.914$, $\tanh(2.48) \approx 0.986$.)

**Step 6 — Compute output pre-activation $z^{[3]}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z^{[3]} &= W^{[3]} a^{[2]} + b^{[3]} \\
&= 0.5(0.880) + 0.3(0.914) + 0.2(0.986) + 0.1 \\
&= 0.440 + 0.274 + 0.197 + 0.100 \\
&= 1.011
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply sigmoid activation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a^{[3]} &= \sigma(z^{[3]}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.011}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + 0.364} = \frac{1}{1.364} \approx 0.733
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Output:** $\hat{y} = a^{[3]} \approx 0.733$, representing a 73.3% confidence (e.g., probability the robot detects a "graspable" object).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Gradient Vanishing in Deep Sigmoid Networks

**Problem:** Show that in a deep network with $L$ hidden sigmoid layers, the gradient of the loss with respect to an early weight is bounded by a product of small terms.

**Step 1 — Chain rule from output to layer $l$.**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial W^{[l]}} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial a^{[L]}} \cdot \prod_{k=l+1}^{L} \frac{\partial a^{[k]}}{\partial z^{[k]}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{[k]}}{\partial a^{[k-1]}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{[l]}}{\partial z^{[l]}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{[l]}}{\partial W^{[l]}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute sigmoid derivative (bounded by 0.25).**

For sigmoid, $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z)) \le 0.25$.

$$
\left\vert \frac{\partial a^{[k]}}{\partial z^{[k]}} \right\vert = \sigma'(z^{[k]}) \le 0.25
$$

**Step 3 — Bound the product.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\vert \prod_{k=l+1}^{L} \sigma'(z^{[k]}) \right\vert &\le \prod_{k=l+1}^{L} 0.25 \\
&= (0.25)^{L - l}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

For $L - l = 10$ and $\vert W \vert \le 1$ (typical initialization), the gradient shrinks to $(0.25)^{10} \approx 9.1 \times 10^{-7}$, effectively zero. This is why **sigmoid hidden units fail in deep networks**, and **ReLU (derivative exactly 1 for $z>0$) is preferred**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Trade-offs

| Architecture Choice | Effect on Underfitting | Effect on Overfitting | Training Time | Inference Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Increase width** (more units/layer) | Reduces | Increases | Increases | Slower |
| **Increase depth** (more layers) | Strongly reduces | Increases (up to a point) | Strongly increases | Slower |
| **Switch to ReLU** | Mildly reduces | Mildly reduces | Faster (no exp) | Faster |
| **Add dropout ($p=0.5$)** | Mildly increases | Strongly reduces | Slower per step | Faster |
| **Add skip connections** | Reduces | Reduces | Mildly slower | Mildly slower |
| **Reduce capacity** | Increases | Reduces | Faster | Faster |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Generic Feedforward Neural Network with Hidden Units

```mermaid
graph LR
    I1((x1)) --> H1A((h1_1))
    I2((x2)) --> H1A
    I3((x3)) --> H1A
    I1((x1)) --> H1B((h1_2))
    I2((x2)) --> H1B
    I3((x3)) --> H1B
    I1((x1)) --> H1C((h1_3))
    I2((x2)) --> H1C
    I3((x3)) --> H1C
    I1((x1)) --> H1D((h1_4))
    I2((x2)) --> H1D
    I3((x3)) --> H1D
    H1A --> H2A((h2_1))
    H1A --> H2B((h2_2))
    H1A --> H2C((h2_3))
    H1B --> H2A
    H1B --> H2B
    H1B --> H2C
    H1C --> H2A
    H1C --> H2B
    H1C --> H2C
    H1D --> H2A
    H1D --> H2B
    H1D --> H2C
    H2A --> O1((y1))
    H2A --> O2((y2))
    H2B --> O1
    H2B --> O2
    H2C --> O1
    H2C --> O2
```

**Caption:** A fully connected feedforward network with 3 inputs, 4 hidden units in layer 1, 3 hidden units in layer 2, and 2 outputs. Every unit in one layer is connected to every unit in the next.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Forward Pass + Backward Pass)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Layer X] --> B[Linear Step: Z1 = W1*X + b1]
    B --> C[Activation 1: A1 = g1 of Z1]
    C --> D[Linear Step: Z2 = W2*A1 + b2]
    D --> E[Activation 2: A2 = g2 of Z2]
    E --> F[Linear Step: ZL = WL*A(L-1) + bL]
    F --> G[Output Layer: Y_hat = gL of ZL]
    G --> H[Loss Function: J of Y_hat, Y]
    H --> I[Backward Pass: Compute dJ/dW_l, dJ/db_l]
    I --> J[Optimizer: Update W and b via Gradient Descent]
    J --> K[Repeat for N Epochs]
    K --> B
```

**Caption:** The training loop for a deep network with $L$ hidden layers. The forward pass computes predictions; the backward pass propagates error gradients layer-by-layer (using the chain rule); the optimizer updates parameters to minimize loss.

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Universal Approximation Intuition

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Target Function f of x] --> Q1[Hard to represent directly]
    Q1 --> R1[Hidden Unit 1: Detects step at x = 0.2]
    Q1 --> R2[Hidden Unit 2: Detects step at x = 0.5]
    Q1 --> R3[Hidden Unit 3: Detects slope in x in 0.3, 0.7]
    R1 --> S1[Weighted Sum forms Step Approximation]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    S1 --> T1[As number of hidden units grows, approximation converges to f]
```

**Caption:** Visualization of the Universal Approximation Theorem. Each hidden unit acts as a "basis function" (e.g., a step or sigmoid bump). Linear combinations of these bases can approximate arbitrarily complex target functions as the number of hidden units increases.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
*Define a hidden unit in a feedforward neural network. Why is it called "hidden"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **hidden unit** is an artificial neuron located in a layer between the input layer and the output layer of a feedforward neural network. It computes a non-linear transformation of the outputs from the previous layer:
$$a_j^{[l]} = g\left(\sum_i w_{ij}^{[l]} a_i^{[l-1]} + b_j^{[l]}\right)$$
It is called "hidden" because its activation is **not directly observed** in the training data — there is no ground-truth label for it. Its role is to learn useful intermediate features that the output layer can use. *(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 formula + 1 explanation of "hidden")*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Universal Approximation Theorem. What is its main limitation in practice?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko, 1989)** states that a feedforward neural network with **at least one hidden layer** containing a finite number of units and a non-linear, non-polynomial activation function can approximate **any continuous function** on a compact domain to arbitrary precision. *(2 marks)*
**Main Limitation:** The theorem guarantees *existence* of such a network but says nothing about *trainability* — in practice, finding the optimal weights via gradient descent may fail due to vanishing gradients, local minima, or insufficient data. Also, the required number of hidden units may be **exponentially large**, making the shallow network impractical. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the role of hidden units in a deep neural network. Discuss why a non-linear activation function is mandatory in hidden layers, with reference to the XOR problem. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A neural network is designed for a 6-DOF robot arm to predict joint torques from a 12-dimensional state vector (6 joint angles + 6 joint velocities). The proposed architecture has:
- Input layer: 12 units
- Hidden layer 1: 64 units (ReLU)
- Hidden layer 2: 32 units (tanh)
- Output layer: 6 units (linear)

Compute:
(i) The total number of trainable parameters.
(ii) The shape of each weight matrix $W^{[1]}, W^{[2]}, W^{[3]}$ and bias vector $b^{[1]}, b^{[2]}, b^{[3]}$.
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Hidden Units & Non-linearity (7 marks):**

Hidden units act as **feature extractors**. Each hidden unit learns to detect a specific pattern in the input — for example, in a robot vision CNN, early hidden units detect edges, mid-level units detect textures, and deep units detect object parts.

**Why non-linearity is mandatory:** A network composed *only* of linear transformations collapses into a single linear map regardless of depth:
$$f(x) = W^{[L]} W^{[L-1]} \dots W^{[1]} x = W_{eff} x$$
Such a network can only learn linear mappings. The classic **XOR problem** (Minsky & Papert, 1969) demonstrated this: no linear classifier can separate XOR outputs $(0,1,1,0)$ from inputs. Hidden units with non-linear activations (e.g., sigmoid, ReLU) project the input into a higher-dimensional space where XOR becomes linearly separable.

**Activation choices:** ReLU is preferred in deep networks for hidden layers because its gradient is exactly 1 for positive inputs, avoiding the vanishing gradient problem of sigmoid/tanh. *(Full 7 marks)*

**(b) Parameter Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Total parameters:**

Weights:
- $W^{[1]}$: $64 \times 12 = 768$
- $W^{[2]}$: $32 \times 64 = 2{,}048$
- $W^{[3]}$: $6 \times 32 = 192$

Biases:
- $b^{[1]}$: $64$
- $b^{[2]}$: $32$
- $b^{[3]}$: $6$

**Total:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 768 + 2048 + 192 + 64 + 32 + 6 \\
&= 3{,}110 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Shapes:**

| Tensor | Shape | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $W^{[1]}$ | $(64, 12)$ | Maps 12-dim input to 64-dim hidden layer 1 |
| $b^{[1]}$ | $(64,)$ | Bias added to each of 64 hidden units |
| $W^{[2]}$ | $(32, 64)$ | Maps 64-dim to 32-dim hidden layer 2 |
| $b^{[2]}$ | $(32,)$ | Bias added to each of 32 hidden units |
| $W^{[3]}$ | $(6, 32)$ | Maps 32-dim to 6-dim output (6 joint torques) |
| $b^{[3]}$ | $(6,)$ | Bias added to each of 6 output units |

*(7 marks: 3 for total + 2 for shapes table + 2 for explanation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare Sigmoid, Tanh, and ReLU activation functions for hidden units. Why is ReLU the default choice in modern deep networks used for robot perception? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a feedforward neural network for a mobile robot's obstacle-avoidance policy. The robot has 8 sonar/lidar range readings (input) and must output 2 values: linear velocity and steering angle. Propose a 2-hidden-layer architecture. Justify:
(i) The number of hidden units per layer.
(ii) The activation function for each layer.
(iii) The total parameter count. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Activation Function Comparison (7 marks):**

| Property | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range | $(0,1)$ | $(-1,1)$ | $[0,\infty)$ |
| Zero-centered | No | Yes | No (sparse) |
| Derivative max | 0.25 | 1.0 | 1.0 (for $z>0$) |
| Vanishing gradient | Severe | Moderate | None (for $z>0$) |
| Computational cost | High (exp) | High (exp) | Very low (max) |

**Sigmoid** saturates for large $\vert z \vert$, causing vanishing gradients in deep networks. It is non-zero-centered, slowing convergence.

**Tanh** is zero-centered, but still saturates. Better than sigmoid for hidden layers, but not optimal.

**ReLU** $g(z) = \max(0, z)$ has derivative 1 for $z > 0$, providing strong gradient flow even in very deep networks. It is computationally cheap (just a threshold), and produces sparse activations (about 50% of units are zero), which acts as implicit regularization.

**Why ReLU for robot perception:** In CNN-based vision for robots (e.g., obstacle detection), networks are often 10–50 layers deep. Sigmoid/tanh would completely stall training. ReLU enables training of networks like ResNet-50 used in autonomous vehicles. *(7 marks)*

**(b) Network Design (7 marks):**

**Architecture:**

- **Input layer:** 8 units (lidar/sonar ranges)
- **Hidden layer 1:** 32 units, ReLU activation
- **Hidden layer 2:** 16 units, ReLU activation
- **Output layer:** 2 units (velocity, steering), Tanh activation (to bound outputs in $[-1, 1]$)

**Justifications:**

**(i) Number of hidden units (32, 16):**
Funnel/pyramid design. The input has 8 features; the output has 2 continuous values. Hidden layer 1 expands to 32 units to learn rich features (e.g., "obstacle on left", "corridor width"). Hidden layer 2 compresses to 16 units to begin synthesizing a decision. Total hidden capacity is moderate to avoid overfitting on typical mobile robot datasets ($\sim$10k samples). *(2.5 marks)*

**(ii) Activation functions:**
- Hidden layers: **ReLU** — fast, no vanishing gradients, sparsity.
- Output layer: **Tanh** — outputs are physical quantities (velocity in $[-v_{max}, v_{max}]$, steering in $[-\pi, \pi]$); Tanh naturally bounds them to $[-1, 1]$, which we then scale. *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Parameter count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{[1]} &: 32 \times 8 = 256 \\
b^{[1]} &: 32 \\
W^{[2]} &: 16 \times 32 = 512 \\
b^{[2]} &: 16 \\
W^{[3]} &: 2 \times 16 = 32 \\
b^{[3]} &: 2 \\
P_{total} &= 256 + 32 + 512 + 16 + 32 + 2 = 850 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

This compact size (under 1k parameters) is suitable for **on-board inference** on a robot's embedded CPU/GPU. *(2.5 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term** in parameter count. Many students compute only weights and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong shape of $W^{[l]}$**: It is $(n^{[l]}, n^{[l-1]})$, not $(n^{[l-1]}, n^{[l]})$. Conflating these is a frequent error.
> 3. **Confusing activation choice for hidden vs. output layer**: Hidden layers use ReLU/tanh; output layer uses sigmoid/softmax/linear depending on the task. Mixing them up loses marks.
> 4. **Ignoring vanishing gradient**: Sigmoid is fine for the output layer (binary classification) but **disastrous** in deep hidden layers. Always justify your activation choice.
> 5. **Ignoring the UAT limitations**: Writing "one hidden layer is enough" without acknowledging training difficulty and parameter explosion loses at least 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hidden Units and Architecture Design:**

- **Hidden unit** = neuron between input and output; learns intermediate features; no ground-truth label.
- **Pre-activation** $z = Wx + b$; **Activation** $a = g(z)$ — non-linearity is **mandatory**.
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** 1 hidden layer + non-linear activation ≈ any continuous function; but practically, **deeper is better** (fewer params, hierarchical features).
- **Default activation for hidden units = ReLU** (cheap, no vanishing gradient, sparse).
- **Avoid sigmoid/tanh in deep hidden layers** due to vanishing gradients.
- **Output activation depends on task:** sigmoid (binary), softmax (multi-class), linear (regression).
- **Architecture heuristics:** Pyramid width, power-of-2 sizes, skip connections for very deep nets, dropout for regularization.
- **Parameter count formula:** $P_l = n^{[l]} \times n^{[l-1]} + n^{[l]}$ per layer.
- **Trade-off triangle:** Capacity ↔ Overfitting ↔ Compute Cost. Match architecture to dataset size.
- **For robotics:** small MLPs (2–4 hidden layers) for control, large CNNs/Transformers (10+ layers) for perception.
- **Always justify** your architecture choice with reference to data size, task complexity, and hardware constraints.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Back-Propagation and Other Differentiation Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Back-Propagation and Other Differentiation Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Back-Propagation: Formal Definition

**Back-Propagation (Backprop)** is a supervised learning algorithm used to train artificial neural networks by efficiently computing the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight in the network. It works by propagating the error signal *backwards* from the output layer to the input layer using the **chain rule of calculus**, thereby enabling iterative weight updates via gradient descent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Back-propagation is a method to calculate the gradient of the loss function with respect to the weights of a neural network, by applying the chain rule iteratively from the output layer back to the input layer. It is *not* an optimizer itself — it is a **gradient computation engine** coupled with an optimizer (e.g., SGD, Adam).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: "The Blame Game"

Imagine a football team that lost a match. The coach doesn't just punish the striker; instead, he reviews the entire match tape:

1. **Forward Pass (The Match):** The ball moves from defenders → midfielders → strikers → goal.
2. **Loss (The Defeat):** The final score (loss) is computed.
3. **Backward Pass (The Blame Allocation):** The coach asks: *How much did each player contribute to the defeat?*
   - The striker missed a clear chance → **High blame**.
   - The defender made a positional error → **Moderate blame**.
   - The midfielder's bad pass → **Small blame**.
4. **Weight Update (Training):** Each player's role is adjusted so the same mistake is less likely next match.

This "blame allocation from output back to input" is exactly what back-propagation does with **weights** and **gradients**.

### 1.3 Other Differentiation Algorithms

Differentiation algorithms are methods to compute derivatives of functions. In machine learning, three major families exist:

| Algorithm | Mechanism | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbolic Differentiation** | Manipulates algebraic expressions symbolically | Exact, but expression swell |
| **Numerical Differentiation** | Uses finite differences | Simple, but suffers from round-off/truncation |
| **Automatic Differentiation (AD)** | Decomposes into elementary ops; applies chain rule | The foundation of modern deep learning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU cares:** Back-propagation is *literally* **Reverse-Mode Automatic Differentiation** applied to a neural network's computational graph. Understanding the broader AD landscape helps answer "why is backprop efficient?" questions in exams.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights)

- **Forward Pass**: Input → Output computation; activations are cached.
- **Backward Pass**: Output → Input; gradients flow back using chain rule.
- **Computational Graph**: A directed acyclic graph (DAG) of operations.
- **Local Gradient (∂z/∂x)**: Derivative of an operation's output w.r.t. its input.
- **Global Gradient (∂L/∂w)**: Derivative of the loss w.r.t. a parameter.
- **Learning Rate (η)**: Step size for weight updates; **typical value: 0.1 to 1e-4**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Phases of Back-Propagation

**Phase 1 — Forward Propagation:**
- Input vector $\mathbf{x}$ is fed into the network.
- Each layer $l$ computes a pre-activation $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ and applies an activation $a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)})$.
- The final output $\hat{y} = a^{(L)}$ is compared with the true label $y$ to compute the loss $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$.
- **All intermediate values are cached** in memory for the backward pass.

**Phase 2 — Backward Propagation:**
- Start with $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial a^{(L)}$ at the output.
- For each layer, compute:
  - Gradient w.r.t. pre-activation: $\delta^{(l)} = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial z^{(l)}$
  - Gradient w.r.t. weights: $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial W^{(l)}$
  - Gradient w.r.t. biases: $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial b^{(l)}$
  - Gradient w.r.t. previous activations: $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial a^{(l-1)}$
- Propagate $\delta$ backwards to earlier layers using the chain rule.

### 2.2 The Chain Rule — The Mathematical Heart

For a composite function $f(g(x))$, the derivative is:

$$\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{df}{dg} \cdot \frac{dg}{dx}$$

For a multi-variable path through a computational graph:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_j^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_j^{(l)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}} = \delta_j^{(l)} \cdot a_i^{(l-1)}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Expression** |
|---|---|---|
| $z^{(l)}$ | Pre-activation at layer $l$ | $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ |
| $a^{(l)}$ | Post-activation (output) of layer $l$ | $a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)})$ |
| $\mathcal{L}$ | Loss function | e.g., MSE: $\frac{1}{2}\sum (y - \hat{y})^2$ |
| $\delta^{(L)}$ | Error at output layer | $\delta^{(L)} = (a^{(L)} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$ |
| $\delta^{(l)}$ | Error at hidden layer $l$ | $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$ |
| $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ | Weight gradient | $\delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$ |
| $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}}$ | Bias gradient | $\delta^{(l)}$ |
| $W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ | Update rule (SGD) | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | Typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $\odot$ | Element-wise (Hadamard) product | $\mathbb{R}^{n} \times \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation:** $\delta^{(l)}$ is the *backpropagated error signal*, NOT the Kronecker delta. Confusion here costs 1-2 marks per KTU exam attempt.

### 2.4 Other Differentiation Algorithms — Engineering Comparison

**Symbolic Differentiation:**
- Treats expressions as algebraic objects; applies differentiation rules.
- **Pro:** Exact. **Con:** "Expression swell" — derivatives of complex nested functions grow exponentially in size.

**Numerical Differentiation (Finite Differences):**
- Approximates derivative as:

$$f'(x) \approx \frac{f(x + h) - f(x - h)}{2h}$$

- **Pro:** Trivial to implement. **Con:** Suffers from floating-point round-off; $O(n)$ evaluations needed for $n$ parameters — **computationally infeasible for millions of weights**.

**Automatic Differentiation (AD):**
- Decomposes the function into elementary operations (add, mul, sin, etc.).
- Two modes:
  - **Forward Mode (Tangent Linear):** Computes derivatives alongside the function evaluation. Cost grows with the number of inputs.
  - **Reverse Mode (Cotangent Linear):** Computes derivatives in a backward sweep. Cost grows with the number of outputs. **This is back-propagation.**
- **Pro:** Machine-precision, efficient. **Con:** Requires storing intermediate values (memory cost).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Robotics

In robotics (the PEBRT755 context), back-propagation is the engine behind:
- **Deep Reinforcement Learning** (e.g., training a robot arm to grasp objects).
- **Visual Servoing Networks** (pixel → motor command mappings).
- **Inverse Dynamics Models** for legged locomotion.
- **Imitation Learning** from human demonstrations.

A humanoid robot with **~10 million policy parameters** cannot be trained with numerical differentiation (would require **10 million forward passes per gradient step**). Reverse-mode AD makes this tractable in a single backward pass.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Back-Propagation Equations

**Step 1 — Define the loss for a single training example.**

For a regression task, the Mean Squared Error loss is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} (a^{(L)} - y)^2$$

For a binary classification task, Binary Cross-Entropy is commonly used:

$$\mathcal{L} = -[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y})]$$

**Step 2 — Initialize the gradient at the output layer $L$.**

For MSE with sigmoid output, using the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} = (a^{(L)} - y)$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}} = (a^{(L)} - y) \cdot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$$

Define $\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}}$, so:

$$\delta^{(L)} = (a^{(L)} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$$

**Step 3 — Backpropagate the error to layer $l-1$.**

By the chain rule:

$$\delta^{(l)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(l+1)}}{\partial z^{(l)}}$$

Since $z^{(l+1)} = W^{(l+1)} a^{(l)} + b^{(l+1)} = W^{(l+1)} \sigma(z^{(l)}) + b^{(l+1)}$:

$$\frac{\partial z^{(l+1)}}{\partial z^{(l)}} = W^{(l+1)} \cdot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$$

Substituting:

$$\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$$

**Step 4 — Compute weight and bias gradients.**

The pre-activation is $z_j^{(l)} = \sum_i w_{ij}^{(l)} a_i^{(l-1)} + b_j^{(l)}$, so $\partial z_j^{(l)} / \partial w_{ij}^{(l)} = a_i^{(l-1)}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}} = \delta_j^{(l)} \cdot a_i^{(l-1)}$$

In matrix form:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$$

Similarly, $\partial z_j^{(l)} / \partial b_j^{(l)} = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the update rule (SGD):**

$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

$$b^{(l)} \leftarrow b^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Hand-Calculable Network)

**Network Architecture:**
- Inputs: $a^{(0)} = [1.0,\ 0.5]^T$
- Hidden layer (1 neuron): $W^{(1)} = [0.8,\ 0.2]$, $b^{(1)} = 0.1$
- Output layer (1 neuron): $W^{(2)} = [0.5]$, $b^{(2)} = 0.05$
- Target: $y = 1.0$
- Activation: Sigmoid $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$
- Learning rate: $\eta = 0.5$
- Loss: $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(a^{(2)} - y)^2$

**Forward Pass:**

$$z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} \cdot a^{(0)} + b^{(1)} = 0.8(1.0) + 0.2(0.5) + 0.1 = 1.00$$

$$a^{(1)} = \sigma(1.00) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.3679} = 0.7311$$

$$z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} \cdot a^{(1)} + b^{(2)} = 0.5(0.7311) + 0.05 = 0.4155$$

$$a^{(2)} = \sigma(0.4155) = 0.6024$$

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(0.6024 - 1.0)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(-0.3976)^2 = 0.0791$$

**Backward Pass:**

Sigmoid derivative: $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$.

$$\sigma'(z^{(2)}) = 0.6024 \cdot (1 - 0.6024) = 0.2396$$

$$\delta^{(2)} = (a^{(2)} - y) \cdot \sigma'(z^{(2)}) = (-0.3976)(0.2396) = -0.0953$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot a^{(1)} = -0.0953 \times 0.7311 = -0.0696$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} = -0.0953$$

Now propagate to the hidden layer:

$$\sigma'(z^{(1)}) = 0.7311 \cdot (1 - 0.7311) = 0.1966$$

$$\delta^{(1)} = (W^{(2)})^T \cdot \delta^{(2)} \cdot \sigma'(z^{(1)}) = 0.5 \cdot (-0.0953) \cdot 0.1966 = -0.00937$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)} \cdot (a^{(0)})^T = -0.00937 \cdot [1.0,\ 0.5] = [-0.00937,\ -0.00468]$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)} = -0.00937$$

**Weight Update:**

$$W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = 0.5 - 0.5(-0.0696) = 0.5 + 0.0348 = 0.5348$$

$$b^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = 0.05 - 0.5(-0.0953) = 0.05 + 0.0476 = 0.0976$$

$$W^{(1)}_{\text{new}} = [0.8,\ 0.2] - 0.5 \cdot [-0.00937,\ -0.00468] = [0.80468,\ 0.20234]$$

$$b^{(1)}_{\text{new}} = 0.1 - 0.5(-0.00937) = 0.10468$$

**Verification of Update (Conceptual):** Since $a^{(2)} = 0.6024 < y = 1.0$, the model should *increase* its output. Indeed, $W^{(2)}$ went up from 0.5 → 0.5348, and $b^{(2)}$ went up from 0.05 → 0.0976 — both correct directions.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Differentiation Methods

| **Method** | **Accuracy** | **Cost per gradient** | **Memory** | **Symbolic Stability** | **Use in ML** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Manual / Symbolic | Exact | Expression grows exponentially | High | Brittle (expression swell) | Rare |
| Numerical (Finite Diff.) | Approximate (round-off) | $O(n)$ forward passes | Low | Robust | Sanity checks only |
| Forward-Mode AD | Machine precision | $O(n)$ tangent sweeps | Low | Stable | Few inputs, many outputs |
| **Reverse-Mode AD (Backprop)** | **Machine precision** | **$O(1)$ backward pass** | **High (cache intermediates)** | **Stable** | **All deep learning** |

Where $n$ = number of input parameters.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Back-Propagation Computational Graph

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **flow of information** during a single training step, from input ingestion to weight update.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start of Training Step]) --> inputA[Input Vector x]
    inputA --> forward1[Layer 1: z1 = W1 x + b1]
    forward1 --> act1[Activation: a1 = sigma z1]
    act1 --> forward2[Layer 2: z2 = W2 a1 + b2]
    forward2 --> act2[Activation: a2 = sigma z2]
    act2 --> loss[Loss Computation: L = 0.5 y minus a2 squared]
    loss --> cache[(Cache All Activations)]
    cache --> back1[Compute delta2: L w.r.t. z2]
    back1 --> gradW2[Gradient: dL dW2 = delta2 a1 transpose]
    gradW2 --> gradb2[Gradient: dL db2 = delta2]
    back1 --> back2[Backpropagate: delta1 = W2 transpose delta2 elementwise sigma prime z1]
    back2 --> gradW1[Gradient: dL dW1 = delta1 x transpose]
    gradW1 --> gradb1[Gradient: dL db1 = delta1]
    gradW2 --> update[Update Weights: W new = W minus eta dL dW]
    gradW1 --> update
    gradb2 --> update
    gradb1 --> update
    update --> end1([End of Step: Weights Adjusted])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Differentiation Engine

This block diagram shows how a generic **Automatic Differentiation (AD) Engine** — of which back-propagation is an instance — operates on a robot's neural network controller.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORWARD_PASS["FORWARD PASS BLOCK"]
        X[Input Tensor X] --> OP1[Elementary Op 1: Multiply]
        OP1 --> OP2[Elementary Op 2: Add Bias]
        OP2 --> OP3[Elementary Op 3: Sigmoid]
        OP3 --> OP4[Elementary Op 4: Loss]
        OP4 --> YP[Predicted Output Yhat]
        OP4 --> TAPE[(Tape: Stores all intermediate values)]
    end
    subgraph BACKWARD_PASS["BACKWARD PASS BLOCK"]
        TAPE --> REV1[Reverse Op 4: dL dYhat]
        REV1 --> REV2[Reverse Op 3: dL dz via sigma prime]
        REV2 --> REV3[Reverse Op 2: dL dW and dL db]
        REV3 --> REV4[Reverse Op 1: dL dX for earlier layers]
        REV4 --> GRAD[Gradient Tensors]
    end
    subgraph OPTIMIZER_BLOCK["OPTIMIZER BLOCK"]
        GRAD --> SGD[SGD or Adam Step]
        SGD --> NEWPARAMS[Updated Weights and Biases]
    end
    YP --> REV1
    NEWPARAMS --> X
```

### 4.3 Forward-Mode vs Reverse-Mode AD Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FMODE["FORWARD MODE AD"]
        F1[x1] -->|seed1| FN1[Node 1]
        F1 -->|seed0| FN1B[Node 1b]
        FN1 --> FN2[Node 2]
        FN1B --> FN2B[Node 2b]
        FN2 --> FN3[Output y1]
        FN2B --> FN3B[Output y2]
    end
    subgraph RMODE["REVERSE MODE AD BACKPROP"]
        R1[Loss L] --> RN3[Node 3 backwards]
        RN3 -->|dy1 dw| RN2[Node 2 backwards]
        RN3 -->|dy2 dw| RN2
        RN2 --> RN1[Node 1 backwards]
        RN1 --> RGRAD[Gradient dL dW]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how **Forward Mode** requires $n$ sweeps for $n$ inputs, while **Reverse Mode** requires only $1$ sweep (regardless of $n$) but $1$ sweep per output. Since neural networks have **millions of weights** but only **1 loss scalar**, reverse mode is exponentially cheaper — this is the deep mathematical reason back-propagation dominates deep learning.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the back-propagation algorithm. State the chain rule of calculus and explain its role in back-propagation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Back-propagation is an algorithm used to compute the gradient of the loss function $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to the weights $W$ of a neural network by propagating the error signal backwards from the output layer to the input layer.

**Chain Rule (1 mark):** For a composite function $f(g(x))$, the chain rule states:

$$\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{df}{dg} \cdot \frac{dg}{dx}$$

**Role in Back-propagation (1 mark):** The chain rule allows the gradient of the loss with respect to any weight in an early layer to be expressed as a product of local gradients along the path from that weight to the output. This enables efficient recursive computation of all gradients in a single backward sweep.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Compare symbolic, numerical, and automatic differentiation. Which one does back-propagation use?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Method | Mechanism | Drawback |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbolic** (1 mark) | Algebraic manipulation of expressions | Expression swell |
| **Numerical** (1 mark) | Finite-difference approximations | Round-off errors; expensive $O(n)$ |
| **Automatic** (1 mark) | Decomposes into elementary ops + chain rule | Memory cost |

**Back-propagation uses Reverse-Mode Automatic Differentiation.**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Computational Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the back-propagation equations for a fully-connected neural network with $L$ layers, sigmoid activation, and Mean Squared Error loss. Clearly show the recursive formula for $\delta^{(l)}$ and the expressions for $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ and $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-input, 1-hidden-neuron, 1-output network with weights $W^{(1)} = [0.6,\ 0.4]$, $b^{(1)} = 0$, $W^{(2)} = [0.7]$, $b^{(2)} = 0.1$, input $a^{(0)} = [0.5,\ 0.3]^T$, target $y = 0.8$, and learning rate $\eta = 0.1$. Use sigmoid activation and MSE loss. Perform **one complete forward pass, one backward pass, and the weight update**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Loss definition (1 mark):**

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(a^{(L)} - y)^2$$

**Step 2 — Output error (1 mark):**

$$\delta^{(L)} = (a^{(L)} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$$

**Step 3 — Recursive backprop (2 marks):**

$$\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$$

**Step 4 — Gradients (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

**Step 5 — Update rule (1 mark):**

$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**Forward Pass (3 marks):**

$$z^{(1)} = 0.6(0.5) + 0.4(0.3) + 0 = 0.42$$

$$a^{(1)} = \sigma(0.42) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.42}} = 0.6035$$

$$z^{(2)} = 0.7(0.6035) + 0.1 = 0.5225$$

$$a^{(2)} = \sigma(0.5225) = 0.6277$$

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(0.6277 - 0.8)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(-0.1723)^2 = 0.01485$$

**Backward Pass (3 marks):**

$$\sigma'(0.5225) = 0.6277(1 - 0.6277) = 0.2337$$

$$\delta^{(2)} = (0.6277 - 0.8)(0.2337) = (-0.1723)(0.2337) = -0.04027$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = -0.04027 \times 0.6035 = -0.02430$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}} = -0.04027$$

$$\sigma'(0.42) = 0.6035(1 - 0.6035) = 0.2393$$

$$\delta^{(1)} = 0.7 \times (-0.04027) \times 0.2393 = -0.006745$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = -0.006745 \times [0.5,\ 0.3]^T = [-0.003373,\ -0.002024]^T$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(1)}} = -0.006745$$

**Weight Update (1 mark):**

$$W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = 0.7 - 0.1(-0.02430) = 0.70243$$

$$b^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = 0.1 - 0.1(-0.04027) = 0.10403$$

$$W^{(1)}_{\text{new}} = [0.6,\ 0.4] - 0.1 \times [-0.003373,\ -0.002024] = [0.60034,\ 0.40020]$$

$$b^{(1)}_{\text{new}} = 0 - 0.1(-0.006745) = 0.0006745$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Conceptual + AD Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Sessional Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the three main approaches to computing derivatives in machine learning: symbolic, numerical, and automatic differentiation. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each and justify why reverse-mode AD (back-propagation) is preferred for training deep neural networks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the computational flow of back-propagation through a multi-layer network. Explain what is stored during the forward pass and how it is used during the backward pass. Why is reverse-mode AD more efficient than forward-mode AD for networks with millions of parameters? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**Symbolic Differentiation (2 marks):**
- Manipulates expressions symbolically (e.g., $d/dx[x^2 \sin(x)] = 2x \sin(x) + x^2 \cos(x)$).
- **Advantage:** Exact closed-form derivatives.
- **Disadvantage:** Suffers from *expression swell* — derivatives of deeply nested functions become exponentially large and unmanageable.

**Numerical Differentiation (2 marks):**
- Uses finite-difference formula $f'(x) \approx [f(x+h) - f(x-h)] / (2h)$.
- **Advantage:** Trivial to implement.
- **Disadvantage:** Suffers from floating-point round-off; requires $O(n)$ forward evaluations for $n$ parameters — infeasible for neural networks with millions of weights.

**Automatic Differentiation (AD) (2 marks):**
- Decomposes the function into elementary operations and applies the chain rule at each step.
- Two modes: **Forward mode** (tangent) and **Reverse mode** (cotangent / adjoint).
- **Advantage:** Machine-precision accuracy; reverse mode computes gradients of a scalar loss w.r.t. millions of parameters in a single backward pass at a cost roughly equivalent to one forward pass.
- **Disadvantage:** Memory cost — must store all intermediate values.

**Justification (1 mark):** For deep networks with millions of parameters $n$ and a single scalar loss, reverse-mode AD has complexity $O(\text{cost of forward pass})$, regardless of $n$. Forward-mode and numerical methods scale as $O(n)$ and $O(n)$ respectively. Hence, reverse-mode is exponentially more efficient.

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**Computational Flow (3 marks):**
The forward pass computes $a^{(l)} = \sigma(W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)})$ layer by layer, terminating in the loss $\mathcal{L}$. The backward pass then computes $\delta^{(l)}$ recursively from the output back to the input, using cached values.

**What is Stored (2 marks):**
During the forward pass, all pre-activations $z^{(l)}$ and post-activations $a^{(l)}$ are cached (stored). These are needed in the backward pass to compute:

$$\sigma'(z^{(l)}) = \sigma(z^{(l)})(1 - \sigma(z^{(l)}))$$

and to evaluate $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$.

**Why Reverse is More Efficient (2 marks):**
- **Forward-mode AD** computes one *column* of the Jacobian per pass, so it needs $n$ passes for $n$ input parameters.
- **Reverse-mode AD** computes one *row* of the Jacobian per pass, so it needs $m$ passes for $m$ output scalars.
- For deep learning, $n$ (parameters) is in the millions, $m$ (loss components) is $1$. Hence, $1$ reverse pass $\ll$ millions of forward passes.

**Block Diagram Description (already covered in Section 4.2 — refer to the Mermaid flow).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\delta^{(l)}$ with the Kronecker delta** — the backprop error is a vector of partial derivatives, not a discrete symbol.
> 2. **Forgetting the Hadamard product $\odot$** when multiplying with $\sigma'(z^{(l)})$. Element-wise multiplication is mandatory because the dimensions must match — a matrix product will give the wrong result.
> 3. **Forgetting the transpose $(a^{(l-1)})^T$** in the weight gradient. The outer product structure $\delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$ is needed to match the shape of $W^{(l)}$.
> 4. **Not caching activations** — without them, $\sigma'(z^{(l)})$ cannot be computed and the backward pass stalls.
> 5. **Mixing up forward and reverse mode AD** — remember: forward = tangent (1 input, many outputs), reverse = adjoint (many inputs, 1 output).
> 6. **Overshooting with $\eta$** — if the loss increases after an update, the learning rate is too high. KTU exam questions may include this as a troubleshooting sub-part.
> 7. **Vanishing / Exploding Gradient Problem** — in deep networks, repeated multiplication by $\sigma'(\cdot) < 0.25$ causes gradients to vanish; large weights cause them to explode. Mentioning this in long-form answers often earns an extra conceptual mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Back-propagation = Reverse-Mode Automatic Differentiation applied to a neural network's computational graph.**
- **Two phases:** Forward (compute & cache activations) and Backward (propagate $\delta$ using chain rule).
- **The key recursive equation:**

$$\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$$

- **Output layer error (MSE + sigmoid):**

$$\delta^{(L)} = (a^{(L)} - y) \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$$

- **Gradient w.r.t. weights:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$$

- **Update rule (SGD):**

$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

- **Why reverse-mode AD wins:** 1 backward pass regardless of parameter count → essential for deep networks.
- **Three differentiation paradigms:** Symbolic (exact but bloated), Numerical (cheap but round-off), Automatic (machine precision & efficient).
- **Practical robotics use cases:** Deep RL for manipulation, visual servoing, legged locomotion, inverse dynamics models.
- **Vanishing/Exploding gradient** is a consequence of repeated multiplication by activation derivatives — motivates ReLU and batch normalization.
- **Time complexity of back-propagation:** $O(\text{total weights})$ per training example — same order as the forward pass.
- **Memory cost:** $O(\text{total activations})$ — must be cached for the backward sweep.

> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Most 14-mark questions in Module 2 combine a *derivation* (7 marks) with a *numerical computation on a 2- or 3-layer toy network* (7 marks). Practice computing sigmoid, sigmoid derivative, $\delta$, and the gradient — by hand — until fluent. The numerical part is where **most students lose marks** due to arithmetic slips, not conceptual errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sequence Model** is a class of probabilistic machine learning model that captures the statistical structure of data arranged in temporal or ordinal order, where the prediction or generation of any element may depend on the preceding (and in some architectures, succeeding) elements of the sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A sequence model is defined as a function $f_{\theta}: \mathcal{X}^{\le \tau} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that maps a variable-length ordered input sequence $\mathbf{x}^{(1:\tau)}$ to an output (which may itself be a sequence, a class label, or a distribution), while preserving the **causality constraint**: the prediction at step $t$ may only depend on observations from steps $1, 2, \ldots, t-1$ (or up to step $t$ in non-causal settings).

Formally, a sequence is denoted as:
$$\mathbf{x} = (x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau)})$$

where $\tau$ is the variable (or fixed) sequence length and each $x^{(t)} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the observation at time step $t$. The model seeks to learn the joint distribution:

$$P(\mathbf{x}) = P(x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau)})$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (The Story-Telling Analogy):**
> Imagine you are listening to a friend narrate a story in Malayalam, English, and Tamil mixed together. To guess the *next* word, you don't just look at the current word in isolation — you remember the **context** built up over the last several sentences. Your brain is essentially running a **sequence model** in real-time, with a "hidden state" that compresses everything you've heard so far.
>
> - A **Feedforward Neural Network (FNN)** is like reading the story but only being allowed to see the *current* sentence. It forgets everything else.
> - A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is like reading with a perfect memory of every previous sentence, but summarizing that memory into a single mental note (the hidden state) that updates with every new word.
> - A **Transformer** is like being able to glance back at *any* previous word in the story whenever you need it (self-attention), instead of relying on the summary note.
>
> For a **robot**, the "story" is the stream of sensor readings, joint angles, velocities, and language instructions. To act intelligently, the robot must model these as sequences, not isolated snapshots.

## 1.3 Why Standard Feedforward Networks Fail on Sequences

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Limitation:** Standard multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and convolutional networks with fixed input size **cannot natively process variable-length sequential data** because:
> 1. They assume **input and output dimensions are fixed** at design time.
> 2. They do not **share parameters** across different positions in the sequence — so a pattern learned at time $t=1$ is *not* automatically transferred to time $t=100$.
> 3. They cannot model **long-range temporal dependencies** without an impractically large input window.

## 1.4 The Need in Robotics (Domain Context)

In the **PEBRT755 - AI & ML for Robots** context, sequence models are indispensable because robotic systems are fundamentally sequential:

| Robotic Phenomenon | Sequential Data Type | Why a Sequence Model? |
|---|---|---|
| Robot walking | Joint angles $\theta_t$ over time | Future gait depends on past posture |
| SLAM | LiDAR/IMU streams | Map building requires temporal fusion |
| Voice control | Speech spectrograms $x^{(1:T)}$ | Phonemes depend on previous phonemes |
| Manipulation | Object pose trajectories | Smooth motion is a temporal dependency |
| Human–Robot Interaction | Natural language commands | Sentence meaning requires word order |

---

## 1.5 The Four Canonical Sequence Modelling Tasks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 High-Yield Classification:** Sequence problems are conventionally categorized into four archetypes. The student **must** be able to name and recognize each:

1. **Sequence to Sequence (Seq2Seq):** Input is a sequence, output is a sequence. *Example:* robot translating a spoken command into a motion plan.
2. **Sequence to Vector (Many-to-One):** Input is a sequence, output is a fixed vector/label. *Example:* classifying a robot's action from a window of sensor data.
3. **Vector to Sequence (One-to-Many):** Input is a fixed vector, output is a sequence. *Example:* generating a trajectory from a goal pose.
4. **Sequence to Synchronous Sequence (Many-to-Many, aligned):** Output at every time step. *Example:* real-time motor command prediction from a video stream.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Central Problem — Joint Distribution Over Sequences

The fundamental goal of a sequence model is to learn the joint probability distribution over a sequence of random variables. Applying the **chain rule of probability** (also called the *product rule*) repeatedly:

$$P(x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau)}) = \prod_{t=1}^{\tau} P\!\left(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(t-1)}, x^{(t-2)}, \ldots, x^{(1)}\right)$$

- **Why this form?** It is a mathematical identity — the chain rule is always valid.
- **How is it useful?** It converts the intractable *joint* distribution into a product of *conditional* distributions, each of which we can model separately.
- **The catch:** Each conditional may depend on **all** previous steps, which is computationally expensive for long sequences.

## 2.2 The Autoregressive Decomposition

To make the problem tractable, sequence models make explicit assumptions about how far back in time a prediction can "see."

### 2.2.1 Full Autoregressive Model (Theoretical Ideal)
Each output depends on **all** previous inputs:
$$P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(t-1)}, \ldots, x^{(1)})$$

### 2.2.2 Markov / First-Order Assumption
The output depends **only** on the immediately preceding input:
$$P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(t-1)}, \ldots, x^{(1)}) \approx P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(t-1)})$$

This is the assumption underlying **Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)** and linear dynamical systems used in classical robot localization (Kalman filters).

### 2.2.3 Fixed-Window (Lag) Model
The output depends on a fixed window of $k$ previous inputs:
$$P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(t-1)}, \ldots, x^{(1)}) \approx P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(t-1)}, \ldots, x^{(t-k)})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Feedforward networks that use a sliding window of past observations are technically sequence models under this approximation, but they suffer from the curse of dimensionality as $k$ grows and **cannot generalize across different sequence positions**.

## 2.3 The Latent Variable / Hidden State Formulation

Modern sequence models (RNNs) introduce a **hidden state** $h^{(t)}$ that acts as a lossy summary of all past observations:

$$h^{(t)} = f_{\theta}\!\left(h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}\right)$$
$$\hat{y}^{(t)} = g_{\theta}\!\left(h^{(t)}\right)$$

- $f_{\theta}$ is the **recurrence** (shared across all time steps).
- $g_{\theta}$ is the **output projection**.
- $h^{(0)}$ is initialized (typically to zero) and acts as the model's initial "memory."

The predicted distribution at step $t$ is:
$$P(x^{(t+1)} \mid x^{(t)}, \ldots, x^{(1)}) \approx P(x^{(t+1)} \mid h^{(t)})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why hidden states are powerful:** They decouple the model's representational capacity from the sequence length. The hidden state $h^{(t)}$ is **fixed-dimensional**, regardless of how long the sequence is — this is what allows RNNs to handle variable-length inputs.

## 2.4 The Causality Constraint

For a *causal* (or *real-time*) sequence model — essential for online robot control — the output at time $t$ must depend only on observations up to time $t$:
$$P(x^{(t+1)} \mid x^{(t)}, x^{(t-1)}, \ldots, x^{(1)})$$

A model that also uses **future** observations $x^{(t+1)}, x^{(t+2)}, \ldots$ (e.g., bidirectional RNNs used in offline ASR) is called **acausal** and is unsuitable for real-time robotic control loops.

## 2.5 Training Objective — Maximum Likelihood

Given a training set of $N$ independent sequences $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$, the model parameters $\theta$ are learned by **minimizing the negative log-likelihood**:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{\tau^{(i)}} \log P_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(t,i)} \mid x^{(<t,i)}\right)$$

This is also called the **cross-entropy loss** when the outputs are discrete categorical variables (e.g., word tokens, robot action classes).

## 2.6 Connection to Robotics — Linear Dynamical Systems

For a continuous-state robot with linear dynamics, the classical state-space model is:
$$x^{(t)} = A\, x^{(t-1)} + B\, u^{(t-1)} + w^{(t-1)}$$
$$y^{(t)} = C\, x^{(t)} + v^{(t)}$$

where $w$ and $v$ are process and measurement noise (typically zero-mean Gaussian). This is a **closed-form sequence model** used in Kalman filters for robot localization — an important precursor to the learnable RNN.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU ESE 2024**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation Notes | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sequence notation | $\mathbf{x} = (x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau)})$ | $\tau$ = sequence length | Defining any sequence |
| 2 | Joint probability (chain rule) | $P(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{\tau} P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(<t)})$ | $x^{(<t)} = (x^{(1)},\ldots,x^{(t-1)})$ | Foundation of all seq models |
| 3 | Markov (1st order) assumption | $P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(<t)}) = P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(t-1)})$ | Conditional independence | HMMs, Kalman filters |
| 4 | Fixed-$k$ window model | $P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(<t)}) = P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(t-k)},\ldots,x^{(t-1)})$ | $k$ = window size | Sliding-window MLPs |
| 5 | Hidden state update | $h^{(t)} = f_{\theta}(h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)})$ | $f_{\theta}$ is **shared** | Core RNN equation |
| 6 | Output prediction | $\hat{y}^{(t)} = g_{\theta}(h^{(t)})$ | $g_{\theta}$ = readout | Many-to-many tasks |
| 7 | Causality constraint | $h^{(t)}$ depends only on $x^{(\le t)}$ | $f_{\theta}$ sees no future | Online robot control |
| 8 | Negative log-likelihood loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t} \log P_{\theta}(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(<t)})$ | Sum is over all steps | Training objective |
| 9 | Cross-entropy (discrete case) | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t} \sum_{k} y_k^{(t)} \log \hat{y}_k^{(t)}$ | $y_k$ = one-hot true label | Classification loss |
| 10 | Linear dynamics (robot) | $x^{(t)} = A x^{(t-1)} + B u^{(t-1)} + w$ | $A,B,C$ = system matrices | Kalman/particle filter |
| 11 | State-space observation | $y^{(t)} = C x^{(t)} + v$ | $v$ = measurement noise | Sensor model |
| 12 | Bidirectional extension | $\vec{h}^{(t)} = f(\vec{h}^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)})$, $\overleftarrow{h}^{(t)} = f(\overleftarrow{h}^{(t+1)}, x^{(t)})$ | Two opposite passes | Offline ASR, NLP tagging |
| 13 | Parameter sharing (key property) | $\theta_f$ same for all $t \in \{1,\ldots,\tau\}$ | One set of weights | Generalization across time |
| 14 | Teacher forcing (training) | Feed ground-truth $x^{(t)}$ (not $\hat{x}^{(t)}$) as next input | Speeds up training | Training RNNs |
| 15 | Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) | Gradient: $\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L} = \sum_{t} \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$ | Sum over unrolled steps | Training algorithm |

---

## 2.8 Why Recurrence is the Right Inductive Bias

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice of model architecture encodes an **inductive bias** — an assumption about the data. For sequential data, the correct bias is:
> 1. **Parameter sharing across time** (the same operation at step $t$ should also work at step $t+k$).
> 2. **Variable-length processing** (the model should handle any $\tau$).
> 3. **Causal/local dependency** (step $t$ is most strongly related to nearby steps).
>
> RNNs encode all three biases **by construction**. This is why they generalize across sequence positions in a way that fixed-window MLPs cannot.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Joint Probability → Tractable Conditional Form

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** This derivation is asked almost verbatim in KTU ESE Module 2 questions. Master the algebraic steps.

**Goal:** Show that the joint distribution over a sequence can be factored into a product of conditionals.

**Step 1 — Start with the joint distribution over $\tau$ random variables.**

$$P(x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau)})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of conditional probability.**

Recall that for any two events $A$ and $B$:
$$P(A, B) = P(A \mid B)\, P(B)$$

Applying this to the first two variables:
$$P(x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau)}) = P(x^{(\tau)} \mid x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau-1)})\; P(x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau-1)})$$

**Step 3 — Recurse on the remaining $P(x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau-1)})$.**

$$P(x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau-1)}) = P(x^{(\tau-1)} \mid x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau-2)})\; P(x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau-2)})$$

**Step 4 — Continue recursing until only $P(x^{(1)})$ remains.**

The recursion unrolls to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau)}) &=
P(x^{(\tau)} \mid x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau-1)})\; P(x^{(\tau-1)} \mid x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau-2)}) \\
&\quad \cdots\; P(x^{(2)} \mid x^{(1)})\; P(x^{(1)})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compact the product using $\prod$ notation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(\tau)}) &=
\prod_{t=1}^{\tau} P\!\left(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(t-1)}\right) \\
&= \prod_{t=1}^{\tau} P\!\left(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(<t)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where we define the shorthand $x^{(<t)} \triangleq (x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(t-1)})$ and adopt the convention that the empty product at $t=1$ is $P(x^{(1)})$.

**Step 6 — Note the base case (sanity check).**

For $t=1$, the conditional $P(x^{(1)} \mid x^{(<1)})$ has no conditioning variables. By convention, this equals the marginal $P(x^{(1)})$, which is consistent with the formula.

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Likelihood Loss for Sequence Models

**Goal:** Derive the standard training loss for an autoregressive sequence model.

**Step 1 — Assume i.i.d. sequences in the dataset.**

Given $N$ independent training sequences $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}^{(N)}\}$, the joint likelihood is:

$$
P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \mid \theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule factorization from §3.1 to each sequence.**

$$
P(\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \mid \theta) = \prod_{t=1}^{\tau^{(i)}} P_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(t,i)} \mid x^{(<t,i)}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Combine and take the log (turn product into sum).**

$$
\log P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{\tau^{(i)}} \log P_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(t,i)} \mid x^{(<t,i)}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Negate to form a loss (we minimize, so negate log-likelihood).**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{\tau^{(i)}} \log P_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(t,i)} \mid x^{(<t,i)}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize by $N$ for an average per-sequence loss.**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{\tau^{(i)}} \log P_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(t,i)} \mid x^{(<t,i)}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — For discrete outputs, the inner log-likelihood is a categorical cross-entropy.**

If $x^{(t,i)}$ takes one of $K$ classes, and $\hat{y}_k^{(t,i)} = P_{\theta}(\text{class}=k \mid x^{(<t,i)})$, then:

$$
\log P_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(t,i)} = k^* \mid x^{(<t,i)}\right) = \log \hat{y}_{k^*}^{(t,i)}
$$

which is the standard log-probability of the true class under the model's softmax distribution. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Sequence Assumption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Comparison Table — KTU Module 2**

| Assumption | Math Form | Computational Cost | Expressive Power | Used In | Suitable for Robot? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full history | $P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(<t)})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\tau \cdot d^{\tau})$ — intractable | Highest | Theoretical baseline | No (infeasible) |
| Markov (1st order) | $P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(t-1)})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\tau \cdot d^2)$ | Limited | HMMs, Kalman filter | Yes (real-time SLAM) |
| Fixed-$k$ window | $P(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(t-k:t-1)})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\tau \cdot d^{k+1})$ | Moderate | Windowed MLP | Limited |
| Latent state (RNN) | $P(x^{(t)} \mid h^{(t-1)})$, $h^{(t)} = f(h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\tau \cdot d_h^2)$ | High | RNN, LSTM, GRU | Yes (LSTM for control) |
| Self-attention (Transformer) | $P(x^{(t)} \mid \text{softmax-attn over } x^{(<t)})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\tau^2 \cdot d)$ | Very high | Transformers, GPT | Mostly offline (heavy compute) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Loss Computation

**Problem:** A tiny autoregressive language model is trained on the single sentence *"Robots Learn"* (tokens: $x^{(1)} =$ "Robots", $x^{(2)} =$ "Learn", $x^{(3)} =$ `<EOS>`). The model assigns the following probabilities:

| Step $t$ | True token $x^{(t)}$ | Model probability $P_{\theta}(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(<t)})$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | "Robots" | 0.40 |
| 2 | "Learn" | 0.10 |
| 3 | `<EOS>` | 0.50 |

**Compute the negative log-likelihood loss.**

**Step 1 — Apply the NLL formula for $N=1$.**

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{3} \log P_{\theta}\!\left(x^{(t)} \mid x^{(<t)}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the probabilities (using natural log).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L} &= -\left[ \log(0.40) + \log(0.10) + \log(0.50) \right] \\
&= -\left[ (-0.9163) + (-2.3026) + (-0.6931) \right] \\
&= -(-3.9120) \\
&= 3.9120 \text{ nats}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bits (commonly reported in ML).** Divide by $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{bits}} = \frac{3.9120}{0.6931} \approx 5.644 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** A loss of **5.644 bits** means the model is, on average, as surprised as a uniform distribution over $2^{5.644} \approx 50$ equally likely outcomes at each step. A perfectly predicting model would have $\mathcal{L} = 0$. $\blacksquare$

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Why Sequence Models Are Needed

The following diagram contrasts a feedforward network (which cannot natively process sequences) with a generic sequence model, and shows the conceptual "unrolling" through time.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        A2["x2 fixed size input"] --> N1
        A3["x3 fixed size input"] --> N1
        N1 --> O1["y single output"]
    end

    subgraph SEQ["SEQUENCE MODEL - RNN UNROLLED THROUGH TIME"]
        S1["x super 1"] --> R1["Recurrence f theta"]
        R1 --> H1["h super 1 hidden state"]
        H1 --> P1["y super 1 prediction"]
        S2["x super 2"] --> R2["Recurrence f theta SHARED"]
        R2 --> H2["h super 2 hidden state"]
        H2 --> P2["y super 2 prediction"]
        S3["x super 3"] --> R3["Recurrence f theta SHARED"]
        R3 --> H3["h super 3 hidden state"]
        H3 --> P3["y super 3 prediction"]
        H1 -.-> R2
        H2 -.-> R3

```

> [!NOTE]

These represent the **hidden state passing** — the key mechanism that gives the RNN memory of the past. Also note that $R1$, $R2$, $R3$ are the **same function** with the **same parameters** (parameter sharing).

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow — Information Flow in an RNN

This diagram illustrates the **closed-loop information flow** that distinguishes sequence models from static networks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT["Initialize h super 0 to zero vector"] --> LOOP
    LOOP["For t = 1 to tau"] --> UPDATE
    UPDATE["h super t = f sub theta of h super t minus 1 and x super t"]
    UPDATE --> OUT
    OUT["y hat super t = g sub theta of h super t"]
    OUT --> LOSE
    LOSE["Compute loss L super t against true x super t"]
    LOSE --> ACCUM
    ACCUM["Total loss L = sum of L super t"]
    ACCUM --> LOOP
    LOOP -->|"after t = tau"| BPTT
    BPTT["Backpropagation Through Time - unroll and compute gradients"]
    BPTT["Backpropagation Through Time - unroll and compute gradients"] --> UPDATE2["Update parameters theta using gradient descent"]
    UPDATE2 --> PRED["Predict next token using P of x super t plus 1 given h super t"]
    PRED --> DONE["Output sequence or final hidden state"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When drawing RNN unrollings in exams, **always show the same function block (with the same label like $f_{\theta}$ or "RNN cell") at every time step**, with **dashed arrows** carrying the hidden state. Examiners explicitly award marks for showing **parameter sharing**.

## 4.4 Diagram — Sequence Modelling in a Robot Control Loop

This shows how a sequence model is embedded in a real-time robot perception-action cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS["Robot sensors - LiDAR, IMU, camera, joint encoders"] --> BUF["Streaming buffer x super 1 to x super t"]
    BUF --> SEQ["Sequence model RNN or LSTM"]
    SEQ --> HID["Hidden state h super t - compressed history"]
    HID --> POL["Policy network pi of a given s and h"]
    POL --> ACT["Motor commands a super t"]
    ACT --> ROBOT["Physical robot executes action"]
    ROBOT --> NEW["New sensor readings at t plus 1"]
    NEW --> BUF
    SEQ --> PRED["Predicted future trajectory for planning"]
    HID --> PRED

    style SENS fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style SEQ fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style HID fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style POL fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style ACT fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style ROBOT fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Notice that the **hidden state $h^{(t)}$ serves a dual purpose** — it is both the *memory* used for the next prediction and the *state representation* consumed by the policy network. This is a recurring design pattern in modern robot learning (e.g., Dreamer, SAC-AE, LSTM-based policy gradients).
