| 1 | **NDVI** | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - RED}{NIR + RED}$ | Vegetation vigor index. Range: [−1, +1] | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **GNDVI** | $GNDVI = \dfrac{NIR - GREEN}{NIR + GREEN}$ | Green-band NDVI, more sensitive to chlorophyll concentration | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **NDRE** | $NDRE = \dfrac{NIR - RedEdge}{NIR + RedEdge}$ | Normalized Difference Red Edge — for mid-to-late season crops | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **SAVI** | $SAVI = \dfrac{(NIR - RED)(1 + L)}{NIR + RED + L}$ | Soil-Adjusted Vegetation Index, $L = 0.5$ for intermediate vegetation | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **GSD (Ground Sampling Distance)** | $GSD = \dfrac{H \times p}{f}$ | $H$ = flight altitude, $p$ = pixel pitch, $f$ = focal length | cm/pixel |
| 6 | **Swath Width** | $W = GSD_x \times N_x$ | $GSD_x$ = across-track GSD, $N_x$ = number of pixels across sensor | meters (m) |
| 7 | **Number of Flight Lines** | $N_L = \dfrac{W_{AOI}}{W \times (1 - S_{lap})}$ | $W_{AOI}$ = AOI width, $S_{lap}$ = side-lap fraction | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Ground Speed Constraint** | $v_{max} = \dfrac{H \times f}{p \times t_{exp}}$ | Max speed to avoid motion blur, $t_{exp}$ = exposure time | m/s |
| 9 | **Number of Triggered Images** | $N_I = \dfrac{L_{line} \times (1 - F_{lap})}{GSD_y \times d_y}$ | $F_{lap}$ = front-lap, $d_y$ = along-track image dimension | Count |
| 10 | **Area Coverage Rate** | $A_{dot} = v \times W$ | $v$ = ground speed | m²/s |
| 11 | **Total Flight Time** | $T_{flight} = \dfrac{A_{AOI}}{A_{dot}} + T_{turns}$ | $T_{turns}$ ≈ 15 s per turn | seconds (s) |
| 12 | **Battery Endurance Check** | $E_{req} = P_{avg} \times T_{flight} \leq C_{batt}$ | $P_{avg}$ = avg power draw, $C_{batt}$ = battery capacity | Wh |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule:** When writing absolute values in the exam, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$. Do **not** write $|x|$ because the unescaped pipe breaks markdown table rendering and, more importantly, KTU digital answer-sheet rendering in some evaluation portals.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Crop monitoring drones are not academic curiosities. They are deployed commercially across **> 50 million hectares** globally as of 2024. Specific applications include:

- **Variable Rate Application (VRA):** The AI-generated prescription map tells the tractor-mounted sprayer to apply **8 L/hectare of fungicide on stressed zones** and **0 L/hectare on healthy zones** — a 30–40 % agrochemical reduction.
- **Early Disease Outbreak Detection:** ML models trained on the **PlantVillage dataset** (54,000 images, 38 disease classes) can detect *Tomato Late Blight* up to **10 days before** it is visible to the human eye.
- **Yield Estimation:** By counting visible fruits/heads in the orthomosaic and correlating with historical NDVI time-series, farmers can predict yield **8 weeks before harvest**.
- **Insurance & Subsidy Verification:** Satellite + drone hybrid evidence is now accepted by crop insurance agencies (e.g., India's **Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana**).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The NDVI Biophysical Foundation

The **Normalized Difference Vegetation Index** is not a heuristic — it is derived from the biophysical reflectance model of a leaf.

**Step 1 — Leaf-level reflectance:** A healthy green leaf has the following characteristic reflectance spectrum (measured with a spectroradiometer):

- Blue band (475 nm): $\approx$ **5 %** reflectance
- Green band (560 nm): $\approx$ **15 %** reflectance
- Red band (668 nm): $\approx$ **5 %** reflectance
- NIR band (840 nm): $\approx$ **50 %** reflectance

The **chlorophyll molecule** strongly absorbs the red light for photosynthesis. The **spongy mesophyll** cellular structure strongly scatters the NIR light. This is the "Red-NIR dichotomy."

**Step 2 — Quantifying the contrast:** A vegetation index must *normalize* this contrast to be comparable across sensors, illumination conditions, and time of day. A simple ratio is:

$$
R = \frac{NIR}{RED}
$$

**Step 3 — Normalization to [−1, +1]:** The simple ratio $R$ is unbounded (it can range from 0 to infinity for bare soil vs. dense canopy). We bound it by subtracting 1 in numerator and using $NIR + RED$ in denominator as a normalizer:

$$
R = \frac{NIR}{RED} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R - 1 = \frac{NIR - RED}{RED}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad \frac{R - 1}{R + 1} = \frac{\dfrac{NIR - RED}{RED}}{\dfrac{NIR + RED}{RED}} = \frac{NIR - RED}{NIR + RED}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{NDVI = \frac{NIR - RED}{NIR + RED}}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- $NDVI \approx 0.8 \to +1.0$ → **Dense, healthy canopy** (forests, mature crops)
- $NDVI \approx 0.4 \to 0.7$ → **Moderate vegetation** (mid-season crops)
- $NDVI \approx 0.1 \to 0.3$ → **Sparse vegetation / shrubs**
- $NDVI \approx 0$ → **Bare soil**
- $NDVI < 0$ → **Water bodies, snow, clouds** (since $RED > NIR$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** The normalization $R - 1 \over R + 1$ is the *Möbius transformation* of the simple ratio into a bounded index. It is mathematically equivalent to projecting the 2D point $(NIR, RED)$ onto a unit line.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ground Sampling Distance (GSD)

GSD determines the spatial resolution of the orthomosaic. It is derived from the **similar triangles** principle of optical imaging.

**Step 1 — Pinhole camera geometry:** A camera is modeled as a pinhole at focal length $f$ from the sensor plane. An object on the ground of size $G$ at altitude $H$ projects to an image of size $I$ on the sensor:

$$
\frac{I}{f} = \frac{G}{H}
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting the pixel pitch:** The image size $I$ is the product of the number of pixels $N$ and the pixel pitch $p$:

$$
I = N \times p
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $G$:** For one pixel ($N = 1$), the ground footprint per pixel is:

$$
G_{1px} = \frac{H \times p}{f}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{GSD = \frac{H \times p}{f}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU standard):**

> A drone flies at $H = 120$ m altitude with a camera of focal length $f = 5.2$ mm and pixel pitch $p = 3.76 \; \mu m$. Calculate GSD.

$$
GSD = \frac{120 \times 3.76 \times 10^{-6}}{5.2 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
GSD = \frac{4.512 \times 10^{-4}}{5.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0868 \;\text{m/pixel} \approx 8.68 \;\text{cm/pixel}
$$

For a higher resolution (e.g., for weed detection), the drone must fly lower — say $H = 30$ m:

$$
GSD_{30m} = \frac{30 \times 3.76 \times 10^{-6}}{5.2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2.17 \;\text{cm/pixel}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Area Coverage Rate & Mission Time

**Step 1 — Single line coverage:** In time $T$, the drone travels a ground distance $D = v \times T$ and covers a strip of width $W$:

$$
A_{line} = D \times W = v \times T \times W
$$

**Step 2 — Coverage rate (per unit time):**

$$
\boxed{\dot{A} = v \times W \;\;[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]}
$$

**Step 3 — Mission time estimation:** For an AOI of area $A_{AOI}$ surveyed with side-lap $S$, the effective coverage per line is $W \times (1 - S)$. The total time is:

$$
\boxed{T_{mission} = \frac{A_{AOI}}{\dot{A}} + N_{turns} \times T_{turn}}
$$

where $N_{turns} = N_L - 1$ (number of turns ≈ number of flight lines minus 1), and $T_{turn} \approx 15$ s.

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:**

> Survey an AOI of **50 hectares** (1 hectare = 10,000 m²) with a drone flying at $v = 8$ m/s, swath $W = 25$ m, side-lap $S = 0.7$. The number of flight lines is computed separately, but assume $N_{turns} = 24$.

**Step (a) — Total area in m²:**

$$
A_{AOI} = 50 \times 10000 = 500{,}000 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step (b) — Effective swath (accounting for side-lap):**

$$
W_{eff} = 25 \times (1 - 0.7) = 7.5 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step (c) — Effective coverage rate:**

$$
\dot{A}_{eff} = 8 \times 7.5 = 60 \;\text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step (d) — Survey time:**

$$
T_{survey} = \frac{500{,}000}{60} = 8{,}333.3 \;\text{s}
$$

**Step (e) — Turn overhead:**

$$
T_{turns} = 24 \times 15 = 360 \;\text{s}
$$

**Step (f) — Total mission time:**

$$
T_{mission} = 8{,}333.3 + 360 = 8{,}693.3 \;\text{s} \approx 144.9 \;\text{minutes}
$$

This **exceeds the typical 30-min single-battery endurance**, meaning the drone must land, swap batteries, and resume — a critical planning consideration.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Front-Lap Required for Stereo Reconstruction

For **dense point cloud generation** via SfM, every ground point must be observed from at least **2 distinct camera positions** (stereo pair). Given the ground footprint of a single image has length $L_{img}$ along the flight track, the **baseline** between successive images must satisfy:

$$
B \leq L_{img} \times (1 - F_{lap})
$$

The along-track footprint of one image is $GSD_y \times N_y$ (where $N_y$ is the number of along-track pixels). For a 75 % front-lap:

$$
B = 0.25 \times GSD_y \times N_y
$$

The **Height-to-Base ratio (H/B)** must typically lie in $[0.5, 2.0]$ for optimal depth accuracy. Outside this range, the baseline is too short (poor depth resolution) or too long (stereo matching ambiguity).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Crop Monitoring Pipeline (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Mission Planner UI] --> A1[GeoJSON AOI Upload]
    A1 --> A2[Flight Path Generator]
    A2 --> A3[Boustrophedon Trajectory with 80 percent Front-Lap 70 percent Side-Lap]
    A3 --> B0[Autonomous Drone Mission Start]
    B0 --> B1[GPS Waypoint Navigation]
    B1 --> B2[Trigger Image Every 2 Seconds]
    B2 --> B3[Multispectral Camera 5 Bands B G R RE NIR]
    B2 --> B4[Thermal Camera LWIR 8 to 14 micron]
    B2 --> B5[RGB Camera 20 MP]
    B3 --> C0[Raw Image Storage Onboard SD]
    B4 --> C0
    B5 --> C0
    C0 --> D0[Post Processing Ground Station]
    D0 --> D1[Radiometric Calibration using Reflectance Panel]
    D1 --> D2[Structure from Motion SfM Reconstruction]
    D2 --> D3[Orthomosaic Generation]
    D2 --> D4[Digital Surface Model DSM]
    D3 --> E0[Per Pixel Band Arithmetic]
    E0 --> E1[NDVI Map]
    E0 --> E2[GNDVI Map]
    E0 --> E3[NDRE Map]
    E1 --> F0[CNN based Classifier ResNet50 or U-Net]
    E2 --> F0
    E3 --> F0
    F0 --> F1[Zone Classification Healthy Stressed Diseased]
    F1 --> F2[Prescription Map GeoTIFF]
    F2 --> G0[Decision Support System]
    G0 --> G1[Variable Rate Sprayer Activation]
    G0 --> G2[Yield Forecast Report]
    G0 --> G3[Insurance Claim Documentation]
```

## 4.2 Drone Hardware & Software Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HW[Hardware Layer]
        H1[Flight Controller Pixhawk 6X]
        H2[GPS GNSS RTK Module]
        H3[IMU 9 Axis]
        H4[ESC Motors and Propellers]
        H5[Battery 6S 22000 mAh LiPo]
        H6[Companion Computer NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano]
    end
    subgraph SP[Sensor Payload]
        S1[Multispectral Camera MicaSense]
        S2[Thermal Camera FLIR Vue Pro]
        S3[RGB Camera DJI L1]
        S4[LiDAR Unit Optional]
    end
    subgraph SW[Software Stack]
        L1[PX4 or ArduPilot Firmware]
        L2[ROS 2 Humble Middleware]
        L3[MAVLink Telemetry Protocol]
        L4[OpenCV Image Processing]
        L5[PyTorch ML Framework]
        L6[QGIS GIS Visualization]
    end
    H1 --- L1
    H2 --- L1
    H3 --- L1
    H1 --- L4
    L1 --- L3
    H6 --- L2
    L2 --- L4
    L2 --- L5
    S1 --- L4
    S2 --- L4
    S3 --- L4
    L4 --- L5
    L5 --- L6
```

## 4.3 ML Inference Pipeline for Disease Classification (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Input Orthomosaic GeoTIFF] --> P1[Tile into 256x256 Patches]
    P1 --> P2[Apply Transfer Learning with ResNet50 Pre-trained on ImageNet]
    P2 --> P3[Fine Tune on PlantVillage Dataset 38 Classes]
    P3 --> P4[Patch-wise Probability Vector 38-dim]
    P4 --> P5[Stitch Predictions into Field Map]
    P5 --> P6[Apply Morphological Opening to remove Salt-and-Pepper Noise]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Disease Severity Percentage]
    P7 --> P8[Output Confidence Score and Bounding Box]
    P8 --> P9[Farmer Dashboard Notification]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 256×256 patches?** This is the standard input size for ImageNet-pretrained backbones. Smaller patches preserve the inductive bias of the network; larger patches dilute the local texture signal that distinguishes early-stage disease lesions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define NDVI. List any four applications of NDVI maps in precision agriculture.**

**Model Answer:**

NDVI stands for **Normalized Difference Vegetation Index**. It is a dimensionless index computed from satellite or drone-based multispectral imagery using the formula:

$$
NDVI = \frac{NIR - RED}{NIR + RED}
$$

where $NIR$ and $RED$ are the reflectance values in the near-infrared (~840 nm) and red (~668 nm) bands respectively. NDVI values range from **−1.0 to +1.0**, with higher values indicating healthier, denser vegetation due to strong chlorophyll activity.

**Four applications in precision agriculture:**
1. **Crop health monitoring** — early detection of water stress, nitrogen deficiency, and pest attacks.
2. **Variable rate fertilization** — generating prescription maps for site-specific agrochemical application.
3. **Yield prediction** — correlating seasonal NDVI time series with historical harvest data.
4. **Crop classification** — distinguishing paddy, wheat, sugarcane, etc., using NDVI thresholds and ML classifiers.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the four main types of sensors used in agricultural drones. State one specific use-case for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Sensor Type | Specific Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RGB Camera** | Weed detection and plant population counting using deep-learning object detection (YOLO). |
| 2 | **Multispectral Camera** (5-band) | Computing NDVI, GNDVI, NDRE indices for chlorophyll and nitrogen stress mapping. |
| 3 | **Thermal (LWIR) Camera** | Detecting irrigation leaks and drought stress by measuring canopy temperature differential. |
| 4 | **LiDAR** | Generating 3D canopy height model (CHM) for biomass and lodging estimation. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Explain the complete architecture of a drone-based crop monitoring system. With a neat block diagram, describe the roles of the flight controller, companion computer, sensor payload, and ground station.

**Model Answer:**

A drone-based crop monitoring system is a **cyber-physical system** integrating hardware (mechanical + electronic), software (firmware + algorithms), and communication (telemetry + data links). The architecture is best described in **four interacting layers**:

**Layer 1 — Airframe & Power:**
The platform is typically a **multirotor (quadcopter/hexacopter)** for precision survey or a **fixed-wing** for large-area mapping. Multirotors offer **hover capability** (essential for high-resolution oblique imagery) and vertical takeoff/landing. Fixed-wings offer **> 60 min endurance** and **> 100 km² per flight** but require a launcher or runway. Power is supplied by a **6S Lithium Polymer (LiPo)** battery of 10,000–22,000 mAh capacity.

**Layer 2 — Flight Control Subsystem:**
The **flight controller (FC)** — e.g., Pixhawk 6X, Cube Orange — runs **PX4 or ArduPilot** open-source firmware. It executes a **500 Hz PID control loop** that fuses data from:
- **IMU** (9-axis: 3-axis accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer) for attitude estimation
- **GNSS/GPS-RTK module** for centimeter-level positioning
- **Barometer** for altitude hold
- **Optical flow / rangefinder** for low-altitude precision landing

The FC communicates with the ground station via the **MAVLink protocol** over a 2.4 GHz telemetry radio (typical range: **5–15 km**).

**Layer 3 — Companion Computer & Sensor Payload:**
A high-performance **companion computer** (NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, Raspberry Pi 5, or Qualcomm Flight Platform) runs the **ROS 2 (Robot Operating System)** middleware. It directly interfaces with the sensor stack:
- Multispectral camera (e.g., MicaSense RedEdge-P) for 5-band imagery
- RGB camera for visual inspection
- Thermal camera (FLIR Vue Pro) for canopy temperature
- LiDAR (Velodyne Puck) for 3D reconstruction

The companion computer runs **onboard preprocessing** (lens correction, geotagging) and can optionally run **real-time ML inference** using TensorRT-optimized models.

**Layer 4 — Ground Station & Cloud Backend:**
The ground station runs **Mission Planner / QGroundControl** for live mission monitoring. Post-flight, images are uploaded to a **cloud backend** (AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage) where **SfM processing** (Pix4D, Agisoft) reconstructs the orthomosaic, and **ML inference** (PyTorch + FastAPI) generates the prescription map. The result is visualized in a **GIS dashboard** (QGIS, ArcGIS Online).

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above for the full Mermaid schematic).**

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — Derive the NDVI formula from the biophysical reflectance model. With a numerical example, demonstrate that NDVI saturates in dense canopies and propose one modified index that addresses this limitation.

**Model Answer:**

*(Derivation as covered in Section 3.1 above.)*

**Numerical Demonstration of Saturation:**

Consider a wheat field at three growth stages. Let us compute NDVI using synthetic reflectance values.

| Stage | NIR Reflectance | RED Reflectance | NDVI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seedling (sparse canopy) | 0.30 | 0.10 | (0.30 − 0.10) / (0.30 + 0.10) = **0.50** |
| Tillering (medium canopy) | 0.50 | 0.05 | (0.50 − 0.05) / (0.50 + 0.05) = **0.818** |
| Heading (dense canopy) | 0.70 | 0.03 | (0.70 − 0.03) / (0.70 + 0.03) = **0.918** |
| Full Ripening (very dense) | 0.80 | 0.02 | (0.80 − 0.02) / (0.80 + 0.02) = **0.975** |

**Observation:** From the tillering stage onwards, NDVI changes are tiny (0.818 → 0.918 → 0.975) despite massive changes in actual biomass. This is the **NDVI saturation problem** in LAI > 3 canopies.

**Modified Index — EVI (Enhanced Vegetation Index):**

$$
EVI = G \times \frac{NIR - RED}{NIR + C_1 \times RED - C_2 \times BLUE + L}
$$

where $G = 2.5$, $C_1 = 6$, $C_2 = 7.5$, $L = 1$. The **BLUE band term** in the denominator compensates for atmospheric aerosol scattering, and the $L$ factor prevents division-by-zero in dense canopies, making EVI more linear against biomass up to LAI = 6.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] — A precision agriculture drone carries a camera with focal length $f = 8$ mm and a sensor pixel pitch of $p = 2.4 \; \mu m$. The sensor has 6000 pixels along-track and 4000 pixels across-track. The drone must survey a 100-hectare field at a front-lap of 80 % and side-lap of 70 % with a cruise speed of 10 m/s. Compute (i) the required flight altitude for a GSD of 5 cm/pixel, (ii) the swath width, (iii) the number of flight lines, and (iv) the total survey time (neglect turn time).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Required flight altitude:**

$$
GSD = \frac{H \times p}{f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad H = \frac{GSD \times f}{p}
$$

$$
H = \frac{0.05 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{2.4 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{2.4 \times 10^{-6}} = 166.67 \;\text{m}
$$

**Part (ii) — Swath width:**

$$
W = GSD \times N_x = 0.05 \times 4000 = 200 \;\text{m}
$$

**Part (iii) — Number of flight lines:**

Assume a square field of 100 ha → $\sqrt{1{,}000{,}000} = 1000 \;\text{m}$ per side. The number of lines needed to cover 1000 m width with 70 % side-lap:

$$
N_L = \frac{W_{AOI}}{W \times (1 - S_{lap})} = \frac{1000}{200 \times 0.3} = \frac{1000}{60} = 16.67
$$

Round up to **17 flight lines**.

**Part (iv) — Total survey time (along-track distance per line = 1000 m, total distance = 17 × 1000 = 17,000 m, neglecting turns):**

$$
T = \frac{D_{total}}{v} = \frac{17{,}000}{10} = 1700 \;\text{s} \approx 28.3 \;\text{minutes}
$$

This fits comfortably within a single 30-min battery charge.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — Explain the role of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in drone-based crop disease detection. With a flowchart, describe the transfer-learning pipeline from ImageNet pre-training to PlantVillage fine-tuning, and state three key evaluation metrics used to report classifier performance.

**Model Answer:**

**Role of CNNs:** Traditional crop disease detection relied on hand-crafted features (color histograms, GLCM texture features) coupled with classical ML classifiers (SVM, Random Forest). These pipelines suffered from poor generalization across fields, lighting conditions, and growth stages. **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** learn hierarchical feature representations **directly from pixel data**, eliminating the need for manual feature engineering. A typical CNN architecture (e.g., **ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B3, or U-Net for segmentation**) can achieve **> 95 % accuracy** on the **PlantVillage benchmark** — a 50,000+ image dataset of 38 disease classes across 14 crop species.

**Transfer Learning Pipeline** (Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram):
1. **Source Domain Pre-training:** The CNN backbone (e.g., ResNet-50) is pre-trained on **ImageNet (1.4 M images, 1000 classes)** to learn generic visual features (edges, textures, shapes).
2. **Target Domain Fine-tuning:** The final fully-connected layer is replaced with a new 38-output softmax layer. The network is then fine-tuned on **PlantVillage** with a low learning rate ($10^{-4}$) for 20–50 epochs.
3. **Drone-Specific Adaptation:** To bridge the **domain gap** (lab images vs. field drone images), an additional fine-tuning stage is performed on a small **field-collected dataset** (typically 500–2000 images) captured by the actual drone sensor at multiple altitudes and lighting conditions.
4. **Patch-wise Inference:** The drone orthomosaic is tiled into **256×256 patches**, each classified independently.
5. **Spatial Smoothing:** Predictions are post-processed with a **Markov Random Field (MRF)** or **Conditional Random Field (CRF)** to enforce spatial coherence.

**Three Evaluation Metrics:**

1. **Overall Accuracy (OA):** $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ — the fraction of correctly classified pixels/patches. A high OA is necessary but not sufficient for imbalanced datasets.
2. **F1-Score (per class and macro-averaged):** $F_1 = 2 \times \dfrac{Precision \times Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ — harmonic mean balancing false positives and false negatives. **Macro-F1** averages across all 38 classes equally, preventing dominant classes from masking poor performance on rare diseases.
3. **Intersection over Union (IoU) — for segmentation models (U-Net):** $IoU = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ — measures the overlap between predicted and ground-truth disease masks. A typical acceptable threshold for a publication-grade model is **IoU > 0.70**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top 3 ways students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the units of GSD** (cm/pixel) — a 1-mark deduction is almost automatic.
> 2. **Writing $|x|$ for absolute value** in a table or formula sheet — the unescaped pipe corrupts the typeset and confuses the digital evaluator. Always use $\vert x \vert$.
> 3. **Confusing NDVI range** — students often write "NDVI ranges from 0 to 1." This is wrong. The correct range is **−1 to +1**. Water and snow have negative NDVI. This single error has historically cost students **2 marks** in KTU 2022 and 2023 ESE papers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize before the exam:**

- ✅ **NDVI = (NIR − RED) / (NIR + RED)**. Range: [−1, +1]. Healthy vegetation > 0.6.
- ✅ **GNDVI uses GREEN instead of RED** — more sensitive to chlorophyll concentration.
- ✅ **NDRE uses Red Edge instead of RED** — preferred in mid-to-late season when canopy is dense (NDVI saturates).
- ✅ **SAVI** has an $L$ factor to correct for soil background reflectance.
- ✅ **GSD = (H × p) / f** — the only imaging geometry formula you need.
- ✅ **Standard overlaps:** 80 % front-lap, 70 % side-lap. Always state these values when designing a mission.
- ✅ **Multispectral bands:** B (475 nm), G (560 nm), R (668 nm), RE (717 nm), NIR (840 nm).
- ✅ **Thermal band:** 8–14 µm long-wave infrared.
- ✅ **LiDAR wavelength:** 905 nm or 1550 nm (1550 nm is eye-safe).
- ✅ **SfM pipeline:** Photos → Feature extraction (SIFT) → Sparse matching → Bundle adjustment → Dense matching → Point cloud → Mesh → Orthomosaic.
- ✅ **PlantVillage:** 38 disease classes, 14 crop species, ~54,000 images. The de-facto benchmark for crop disease classification.
- ✅ **Battery constraint:** Always check that $P_{avg} \times T_{flight} \leq C_{batt}$. If not, plan a multi-battery mission with safe auto-landing at the home point.
- ✅ **Five standard sensors** to remember: RGB, Multispectral, Hyperspectral, Thermal, LiDAR.
- ✅ **Three evaluation metrics** for ML models: Accuracy, F1-Score, IoU (for segmentation).
- ✅ **Two common CNN backbones:** ResNet-50 (classification) and U-Net (segmentation).
- ✅ **One safe index for dense canopy:** EVI, which uses the BLUE band to correct for atmospheric scattering and prevents saturation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traffic Sign Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Traffic Sign Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Traffic Sign Detection (TSD)** is a critical sub-field of computer vision and intelligent transportation systems that involves the automatic identification, localization, and classification of road traffic signs from real-world images or video streams captured by a camera mounted on a vehicle or robot. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for AI & Machine Learning for Robots, Traffic Sign Detection is positioned as a canonical *real-time object detection problem* where the robotic system must mimic the human driver's ability to recognize regulatory, warning, and informative signs under varying environmental conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Verbatim):**
> *"Traffic Sign Detection is the process of identifying the presence of a traffic sign within an image (detection) and assigning it to a predefined semantic category such as Stop, Yield, Speed Limit, or No Entry (classification), typically performed using deep convolutional neural networks operating on RGB image frames."*

The pipeline fundamentally consists of two cascaded stages:
1. **Localization (Where?)** – Drawing a bounding box around the sign.
2. **Recognition (What?)** – Assigning a class label to the cropped region.

In modern architectures (e.g., **YOLO, SSD, Faster R-CNN**), these two stages are unified into a single-shot regression framework, producing both bounding box coordinates and class probabilities in one forward pass.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Child Learning Road Safety"**
>
> Imagine teaching a child to recognize traffic signs. You do not hand the child a rulebook of geometry equations. Instead, you show them thousands of pictures of stop signs, speed-limit boards, and warning triangles. Over time, the child's brain (analogous to a **Convolutional Neural Network**) learns to associate:
> - The **octagonal red shape** with the word "STOP."
> - The **circular red border with a number** with "SPEED LIMIT."
> - The **triangular red border** with "YIELD" or "WARNING."
>
> Traffic Sign Detection works identically: a camera acts as the child's "eye," the CNN acts as the "brain," and a labelled dataset (e.g., the **GTSRB — German Traffic Sign Recognition Benchmark** containing 50,000+ images across 43 classes) acts as the "experience."

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

For every Traffic Sign Detection system, the KTU Board expects familiarity with the following evaluation metrics:

- **Precision (P):** The fraction of correctly detected signs among all detections made by the system. Target: **> 0.95** for safety-critical applications.
- **Recall (R):** The fraction of actual ground-truth signs that were successfully detected. Target: **> 0.98** (missing a sign is dangerous).
- **Intersection over Union (IoU):** The ratio of overlapping area to the union of predicted and ground-truth bounding boxes. Threshold for "correct detection": **IoU ≥ 0.5**.
- **Mean Average Precision (mAP):** The mean of Average Precision values across all sign classes. State-of-the-art benchmark: **mAP ≈ 0.99** on GTSRB.
- **Frames Per Second (FPS):** Real-time inference speed. Minimum requirement for autonomous driving: **≥ 30 FPS**.
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. Defined as **F1 = 2PR / (P + R)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Traffic Sign Detection Pipeline

The operational flow of any modern TSD system can be decomposed into **six sequential stages**. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests each of these as separate conceptual entities:

### Stage 1 — Image Acquisition
A monocular RGB camera (typically mounted behind the windshield) captures frames at a fixed rate (commonly **30 Hz** or **60 Hz**). Resolution choices include **640×480 (VGA)**, **1280×720 (HD)**, or **1920×1080 (Full HD)**.

### Stage 2 — Preprocessing
Raw frames suffer from motion blur, low-light noise, and lens distortion. Preprocessing steps include:
- **Resizing** to a fixed input dimension (e.g., 224×224 for VGG-based nets, 416×416 for YOLO).
- **Normalization** of pixel values: $x_{norm} = \frac{x}{255}$ or $x_{norm} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ (zero-mean, unit-variance).
- **Color Space Conversion:** Converting RGB → HSV or RGB → Grayscale. HSV is preferred because it decouples **hue (color identity)** from **illumination (brightness)**, which is critical for robust sign color recognition.
- **Histogram Equalization (CLAHE):** Contrast-Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization enhances local contrast, improving visibility in shadows or glare.

### Stage 3 — Region Proposal / Feature Extraction
- **Classical approach:** Uses handcrafted features such as **HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients)**, **SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)**, or **SURF (Speeded-Up Robust Features)**, combined with a sliding window search.
- **Deep Learning approach:** A **CNN backbone** (e.g., ResNet-50, MobileNet, EfficientNet) automatically learns hierarchical features — low-level edges in early layers, mid-level shapes in middle layers, high-level semantic patterns (e.g., the octagon) in deeper layers.

### Stage 4 — Detection
- **Two-stage detectors** (Faster R-CNN): First generate Region Proposals, then classify each proposal. High accuracy, lower speed (~7 FPS).
- **One-stage detectors** (YOLO, SSD, RetinaNet): Treat detection as a single regression problem. Lower latency (~45–155 FPS), suitable for real-time robotics.

### Stage 5 — Classification
Each detected bounding box is assigned to one of *N* sign classes (typically **N = 43** in GTSRB or **N ≈ 200** in extended datasets). The output is a probability vector obtained via the **Softmax function**:

$$
P(y_i \mid x) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} e^{z_j}}
$$

where $z_i$ is the logit (raw score) for class $i$, and $N$ is the total number of traffic sign categories.

### Stage 6 — Post-Processing
- **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** Eliminates redundant overlapping bounding boxes.
- **Confidence Thresholding:** Discards detections with probability below a threshold (commonly **0.5**).
- **Temporal Filtering:** Across consecutive frames, smoothing predictions to avoid flicker (e.g., Kalman filter or majority voting).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Intersection over Union (IoU)** | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert B_{pred} \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_{pred} \cup B_{gt} \vert}$ | Measures bounding-box accuracy | Dimensionless [0, 1] |
| 2 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Reliability of detections | Dimensionless [0, 1] |
| 3 | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of ground truth | Dimensionless [0, 1] |
| 4 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balanced P–R metric | Dimensionless [0, 1] |
| 5 | **Average Precision (AP)** | $AP = \int_{0}^{1} P(R) \, dR$ | Area under PR curve per class | Dimensionless [0, 1] |
| 6 | **mAP (mean Average Precision)** | $mAP = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} AP_i$ | Global detection benchmark | Dimensionless [0, 1] |
| 7 | **Softmax Classifier** | $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class probability output | Probability [0, 1] |
| 8 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L_{CE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Training objective | Nats (or bits) |
| 9 | **YOLO Bounding-Box Loss** | $L_{box} = \lambda_{coord}\sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2$ | Localization regression | Squared pixels |
| 10 | **Pixel Normalization** | $x_{norm} = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardize input | Dimensionless |
| 11 | **Color Space (HSV Hue)** | $H = \arccos\left[\dfrac{0.5[(R-G)+(R-B)]}{\sqrt{(R-G)^2+(R-B)(G-B)}}\right]$ | Robust color segmentation | Degrees [0°, 360°] |
| 12 | **CLAHE Clip Limit** | $L_{clip} = c \cdot \dfrac{N_{x} N_{y}}{N_{gray}}$ | Adaptive histogram cap | Pixel count |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Traffic Sign Detection is the *front-end perception module* of every Level-3+ autonomous vehicle (e.g., Tesla Autopilot, Waymo Driver, Mobileye EyeQ). It is also deployed in Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (**ADAS**), intelligent traffic surveillance drones, and warehouse AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) robots operating in semi-structured environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the IoU Metric (Step-by-Step)

The **Intersection over Union (IoU)** is mathematically derived from the Jaccard Index of two sets. The KTU Board frequently asks students to derive or compute IoU given predicted and ground-truth boxes.

**Given:**
- Ground-truth bounding box $B_{gt}$ with area $A_{gt}$.
- Predicted bounding box $B_{pred}$ with area $A_{pred}$.
- Intersection area $A_{int} = A_{gt} \cap A_{pred}$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define Union Area (Inclusion-Exclusion Principle)} \quad & A_{union} = A_{gt} + A_{pred} - A_{int} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Define Intersection Area (Geometric Overlap)} \quad & A_{int} = \max(0, x_{2}^{int} - x_{1}^{int}) \cdot \max(0, y_{2}^{int} - y_{1}^{int}) \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Compute the Jaccard Ratio} \quad & \text{IoU} = \frac{A_{int}}{A_{union}} = \frac{A_{int}}{A_{gt} + A_{pred} - A_{int}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Substitute and Simplify} \quad & \text{IoU} = \frac{A_{gt} \cap A_{pred}}{A_{gt} \cup A_{pred}} \in [0, 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose $B_{gt} = (10, 10, 50, 50)$ (top-left and bottom-right corners) and $B_{pred} = (30, 30, 70, 70)$.

The intersection rectangle has corners: $(\max(10,30), \max(10,30)) = (30, 30)$ and $(\min(50,70), \min(50,70)) = (50, 50)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{int} &= (50 - 30) \times (50 - 30) = 20 \times 20 = 400 \text{ pixels}^2 \\[6pt]
A_{gt} &= (50 - 10) \times (50 - 10) = 40 \times 40 = 1600 \text{ pixels}^2 \\[6pt]
A_{pred} &= (70 - 30) \times (70 - 30) = 40 \times 40 = 1600 \text{ pixels}^2 \\[6pt]
A_{union} &= 1600 + 1600 - 400 = 2800 \text{ pixels}^2 \\[6pt]
\text{IoU} &= \frac{400}{2800} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.143
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since **IoU = 0.143 < 0.5**, the prediction is considered a **False Positive** (missed detection).

## 3.2 Derivation of Softmax + Cross-Entropy Loss Gradient

The cross-entropy loss is the canonical training objective for multi-class traffic sign classification. Let $z_i$ denote the logit for class $i$, and $\hat{y}_i = \sigma(z_i)$ denote the softmax probability.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Softmax Activation} \quad & \hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} e^{z_j}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Cross-Entropy Loss} \quad & L = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) \quad \text{where } y_i \text{ is one-hot ground truth} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Combine Softmax + CE} \quad & L = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \left(z_i - \log \sum_{j=1}^{N} e^{z_j}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Derivative w.r.t. logit } z_k \quad & \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k - y_k \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5: Backpropagation Update} \quad & z_k^{new} = z_k^{old} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k} = z_k^{old} - \eta(\hat{y}_k - y_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Why Softmax + CE works so well):** The gradient simplifies to the elegant form $\hat{y}_k - y_k$, which is the *prediction error* itself. This avoids the vanishing-gradient problem of sigmoid + MSE and accelerates convergence on large traffic-sign datasets.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classical vs. Deep Learning Approaches

| Parameter | Classical CV (HOG + SVM) | Deep Learning (CNN, e.g., YOLO) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature Engineering** | Manual (handcrafted) | Automatic (learned) |
| **Training Data Required** | ~1,000 images | ~50,000+ images |
| **Inference Speed** | ~60 FPS (CPU) | ~45–155 FPS (GPU) |
| **Accuracy (GTSRB)** | ~97.5% | ~99.7% |
| **Robustness to Illumination** | Low (sensitive) | High (HSV + augmentation) |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | High (needs GPU/TPU) |
| **Interpretability** | High | Low (black-box) |
| **KTU 2024 Relevance** | Legacy (rarely asked) | **High-weightage topic** |

## 3.4 Average Precision (AP) Derivation

The **Average Precision (AP)** is the area under the Precision-Recall curve. Given sorted detections ranked by confidence score, AP is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Sort all detections by descending confidence score} \quad & c_1 \geq c_2 \geq \dots \geq c_M \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Compute Precision and Recall at each threshold} \quad & P(t), R(t) \text{ for } t = 1, 2, \dots, M \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Approximate AP via 11-point interpolation (Pascal VOC pre-2010)} \quad & AP = \frac{1}{11}\sum_{r \in \{0, 0.1, \dots, 1.0\}} P_{interp}(r) \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Or compute exact area} \quad & AP = \sum_{n=1}^{M} (R_n - R_{n-1}) \cdot P_n
\end{aligned}
$$

where $P_{interp}(r) = \max_{r' \geq r} P(r')$ ensures monotonicity of the PR curve.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — End-to-End Traffic Sign Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Camera Input Frame 640x480] --> B[Image Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Resize to 416x416]
    B --> B2[RGB to HSV Conversion]
    B --> B3[CLAHE Normalization]
    B1 --> C[CNN Backbone ResNet-50]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Feature Map 13x13x1024]
    D --> E[Detection Head YOLO v3]
    E --> F[Raw Predictions Boxes and Class Scores]
    F --> G[Non Maximum Suppression NMS]
    G --> H{Confidence greater than 0.5}
    H -->|Yes| I[Final Bounding Box and Class Label]
    H -->|No| J[Discard Detection]
    I --> K[Actuator Decision Brake Steer Alert]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each block is a functional module. The student should annotate this diagram in the examination and explain the role of every stage in 1–2 lines. The decision diamond (H) represents the **confidence threshold gate** — a critical filtering step taught in KTU Module 3.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — CNN Backbone Architecture (Layer-by-Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Input Image 224x224x3] --> L2[Conv2D 64 filters 3x3 ReLU]
    L2 --> L3[MaxPool 2x2 stride 2]
    L3 --> L4[Conv2D 128 filters 3x3 ReLU]
    L4 --> L5[MaxPool 2x2 stride 2]
    L5 --> L6[Conv2D 256 filters 3x3 ReLU]
    L6 --> L7[MaxPool 2x2 stride 2]
    L7 --> L8[Conv2D 512 filters 3x3 ReLU]
    L8 --> L9[MaxPool 2x2 stride 2]
    L9 --> L10[Flatten Layer]
    L10 --> L11[FC 4096 neurons Dropout 0.5]
    L11 --> L12[FC 4096 neurons Dropout 0.5]
    L12 --> L13[Softmax Output 43 Classes]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — YOLO Single-Shot Detection Grid Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMG[Input Image 416x416] --> GRID[Divide into SxS Grid 13x13]
    GRID --> C1[Cell predicts B boxes]
    GRID --> C2[Cell predicts class probability]
    GRID --> C3[Cell predicts confidence score]
    C1 --> OUT[Bounding Boxes xywh]
    C2 --> CLS[Class Labels Stop Yield SpeedLimit]
    C3 --> CONF[Confidence Score 0 to 1]
    OUT --> FINAL[Final Detection]
    CLS --> FINAL
    CONF --> FINAL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The 13×13 grid concept is the *intellectual core* of YOLO. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the student's ability to explain why a single CNN forward pass can replace the slow R-CNN pipeline. Mention **"grid cell responsibility"** — each cell is responsible for detecting objects whose center falls within it.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Architecture — Data Augmentation Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Raw Sign Image] --> AUG1[Rotation plus or minus 20 degrees]
    RAW --> AUG2[Translation plus or minus 10 percent]
    RAW --> AUG3[Scaling 0.8 to 1.2x]
    RAW --> AUG4[Shear 0.2 radians]
    RAW --> AUG5[Brightness Jitter plus or minus 30 percent]
    RAW --> AUG6[Gaussian Noise sigma 0.01]
    AUG1 --> POOL[Augmented Pool]
    AUG2 --> POOL
    AUG3 --> POOL
    AUG4 --> POOL
    AUG5 --> POOL
    AUG6 --> POOL
    POOL --> TRAIN[Train CNN Robustly]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Traffic Sign Detection. List the three main categories of traffic signs recognized by an autonomous vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**
Traffic Sign Detection (TSD) is the computer vision task of automatically identifying and localizing road signs within a digital image and classifying each sign into a predefined semantic category.

The three main categories are:
1. **Regulatory Signs** — Mandatory instructions (e.g., Stop, Speed Limit, No Entry).
2. **Warning Signs** — Alert to hazards (e.g., Sharp Turn, Pedestrian Crossing).
3. **Informative/Guide Signs** — Provide directional or facility information (e.g., Hospital, Parking, Highway Exit).

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark per category — total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the role of Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) in the traffic sign detection pipeline? State the IoU threshold commonly used.**

**Model Answer:**
NMS is a post-processing algorithm that eliminates redundant overlapping bounding boxes produced by the detector, retaining only the box with the **highest confidence score** for each detected object.

**Procedure:**
1. Sort all predicted boxes by confidence.
2. Pick the box with highest score; discard any box whose IoU with it exceeds threshold.
3. Repeat until all boxes are processed.

**Common IoU threshold:** $\text{IoU} \geq 0.5$ (overlap threshold for suppression).

*(Examiner's Note: 2 marks for procedure + 1 mark for threshold.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the IoU formula** in part (a) and lose 2 marks.
> - In YOLO loss questions, students often **forget the $\sqrt{w}$ and $\sqrt{h}$ terms** — remember, square-root is applied to width and height to penalize large-box errors less harshly.
> - Always state the **dataset name** (GTSRB/BelgiumTS) when discussing traffic sign benchmarks. Vague answers like "a dataset of signs" fetch only partial credit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`**

**Q: (a) Explain the complete pipeline of a deep-learning-based Traffic Sign Detection system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the CNN backbone and detection head in detail. (7 Marks)**

**(b) The IoU between a predicted bounding box and the ground truth is to be computed. Given $B_{pred} = (20, 20, 80, 80)$ and $B_{gt} = (40, 40, 100, 100)$, calculate the Intersection over Union. Based on the standard threshold, determine if the detection is a True Positive or False Positive. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The deep-learning-based TSD pipeline comprises the following six stages (refer to Section 4.1 for the diagram):

1. **Image Acquisition:** A vehicle-mounted camera captures RGB frames at 30 FPS, typically at HD resolution.
2. **Preprocessing:** Frame is resized to $416 \times 416$ (YOLO input), normalized via $x_{norm} = (x/255 - 0.5)/0.5$, and color-augmented using HSV jitter.
3. **CNN Backbone:** A deep feature extractor (e.g., ResNet-50, Darknet-53) processes the image. Early layers detect edges and color blobs; mid-layers detect shapes (octagons, triangles); deep layers detect full semantic sign patterns. The output is a feature map of shape $13 \times 13 \times 1024$.
4. **Detection Head:** Predicts, for each grid cell, 5 values per anchor box $(x, y, w, h, \text{confidence})$ plus $C$ class probabilities. Total output: $S \times S \times B \times (5 + C)$.
5. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** Filters overlapping predictions, keeping only the most confident box.
6. **Actuator Decision:** The detected sign is mapped to a driving action via a rule-based or reinforcement-learning policy module.

**Model Solution to (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Intersection coordinates} \quad & x_{1}^{int} = \max(20, 40) = 40, \quad x_{2}^{int} = \min(80, 100) = 80 \\
& y_{1}^{int} = \max(20, 40) = 40, \quad y_{2}^{int} = \min(80, 100) = 80 \\[6pt]
\text{Intersection Area} \quad & A_{int} = (80 - 40)(80 - 40) = 40 \times 40 = 1600 \text{ px}^2 \\[6pt]
\text{Ground-Truth Area} \quad & A_{gt} = (100 - 40)(100 - 40) = 60 \times 60 = 3600 \text{ px}^2 \\[6pt]
\text{Predicted Area} \quad & A_{pred} = (80 - 20)(80 - 20) = 60 \times 60 = 3600 \text{ px}^2 \\[6pt]
\text{Union Area} \quad & A_{union} = 3600 + 3600 - 1600 = 5600 \text{ px}^2 \\[6pt]
\text{IoU} \quad & = \frac{1600}{5600} = \frac{4}{14} = \frac{2}{7} \approx 0.286
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\text{IoU} = 0.286 < 0.5$ (the standard PASCAL VOC threshold), the detection is classified as a **False Positive**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Q: (a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of the YOLO (You Only Look Once) object detector. Discuss how the input image is divided into a grid and how bounding box predictions are made. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A traffic sign classifier outputs the following logits for a test image: $z = [2.5, 1.0, 0.3]$ corresponding to classes [Stop, Yield, SpeedLimit-60]. Compute the Softmax probabilities. If the ground-truth label is "Stop", compute the cross-entropy loss. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

YOLO reframes object detection as a **single regression problem**, going from image pixels directly to bounding-box coordinates and class probabilities in one forward pass.

**Architecture (refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram):**
- **Input Layer:** Image resized to $416 \times 416 \times 3$.
- **Convolutional Backbone:** 24 conv layers (YOLOv1) + 2 FC layers extract hierarchical features.
- **Grid Division:** The image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid (typically $13 \times 13$ or $7 \times 7$). If the center of an object falls in a grid cell, that cell is responsible for detecting it.
- **Per-Cell Prediction:** Each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes, each with 5 values $(x, y, w, h, \text{confidence})$, plus $C$ class probabilities.
- **Final Output Tensor:** $S \times S \times (B \times 5 + C)$.
- **NMS Post-Processing:** Removes duplicate detections.

**Key Advantage:** YOLO runs at **45–155 FPS** (real-time), making it ideal for robotic applications such as autonomous vehicles.

**Model Solution to (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute exponentials} \quad & e^{2.5} \approx 12.182, \quad e^{1.0} \approx 2.718, \quad e^{0.3} \approx 1.350 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Compute denominator (partition function)} \quad & Z = 12.182 + 2.718 + 1.350 = 16.250 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Softmax probabilities} \quad & P(\text{Stop}) = \frac{12.182}{16.250} \approx 0.7497 \\
& P(\text{Yield}) = \frac{2.718}{16.250} \approx 0.1673 \\
& P(\text{SpeedLimit-60}) = \frac{1.350}{16.250} \approx 0.0831 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Verify probabilities sum to 1} \quad & 0.7497 + 0.1673 + 0.0831 = 1.0001 \approx 1.00 \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5: Cross-Entropy Loss} \quad & L_{CE} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) \\
& \text{Since ground truth is Stop (one-hot } y = [1, 0, 0]) \\
& L_{CE} = -[1 \cdot \log(0.7497) + 0 + 0] = -\log(0.7497) \\
& L_{CE} \approx 0.288 \text{ nats}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The classifier is **~75% confident** the sign is a Stop sign, yielding a low loss of 0.288 nats. If the model had predicted 0.33 for all classes (uniform), the loss would be $\log(3) \approx 1.099$ nats — significantly higher.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3: Traffic Sign Detection):**
>
> - ✅ Traffic Sign Detection = **Localization (Bounding Box)** + **Classification (Class Label)**.
> - ✅ Canonical pipeline: **Image → Preprocess → CNN Backbone → Detection Head → NMS → Decision**.
> - ✅ Standard benchmark dataset: **GTSRB (43 classes, 50,000+ images)**.
> - ✅ **IoU ≥ 0.5** is the standard threshold for a True Positive.
> - ✅ **YOLO** = single-shot, real-time (~45+ FPS); **Faster R-CNN** = two-stage, higher accuracy but slower.
> - ✅ **Softmax** converts logits to probabilities; **Cross-Entropy** is the loss function.
> - ✅ YOLO loss has three components: **localization loss + confidence loss + classification loss**.
> - ✅ The **$13 \times 13$ YOLO grid** divides the image; each cell is responsible for objects whose center lies within it.
> - ✅ **HSV color space** is preferred over RGB for sign color recognition (illumination-robust).
> - ✅ **Data augmentation** (rotation, translation, brightness jitter) is *mandatory* for robust traffic sign classifiers.
> - ✅ **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** eliminates redundant overlapping boxes.
> - ✅ Real-time requirement for autonomous driving: **≥ 30 FPS**.
> - ✅ The cross-entropy gradient w.r.t. logits has the elegant closed form: **$\partial L / \partial z_k = \hat{y}_k - y_k$**.
> - ✅ Always cite **mAP (mean Average Precision)** as the gold-standard detection metric; cite **accuracy** only for pure classification tasks.
> - ✅ The CNN backbone hierarchy: **Edges (Layer 1) → Shapes (Layer 5) → Semantics (Layer 15+)**.
> - ✅ **CLAHE** is used for local contrast enhancement in low-light or shadowed scenes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pedestrian Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pedestrian Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pedestrian Detection** is a specialized branch of object detection within computer vision and robotic perception, focused on automatically identifying and localizing human figures (pedestrians) in static images or continuous video streams. In the context of robotics (KTU PEBRT755), it forms a critical perception layer that enables autonomous systems — such as self-driving cars, service robots, and surveillance drones — to perceive, predict, and react to human presence in their operational environment.

Mathematically, pedestrian detection is formulated as a **binary classification** problem combined with a **bounding-box regression** task. Given an input image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$, the system must produce:

$$\{(b_i, c_i, s_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$$

where:
- $b_i = (x_i, y_i, w_i, h_i)$ is the bounding box (centre coordinates, width, height),
- $c_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the class label (1 = pedestrian),
- $s_i \in [0, 1]$ is the confidence score,
- $N$ is the number of detected pedestrian instances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pedestrian detection is the *gateway* to higher-level robotic tasks like path planning, collision avoidance, intent prediction, and human–robot interaction. It is **not** just a vision problem — it directly drives the safety-critical decision loop of any mobile robot operating near humans.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a busy, crowded railway crossing at dusk. The light is fading, people are wearing varied clothing (sarees, suits, backpacks, umbrellas), some are partially hidden behind others, and a few are moving erratically. Despite all this chaos, your brain *instantly* separates "humans" from "poles, signboards, and trees." 

**Pedestrian detection algorithms do the same job, but mathematically.** They extract distinctive *gradient patterns* (edges, contours, silhouettes) that are statistically common to the human form — a round-ish head, a vertical torso, two legs, swinging arms — and use trained classifiers to ask at every sliding window of the image: *"Is there a pedestrian here, and if so, how confident am I?"*

A real-world analogy: it works like a **metal detector at an airport**. You sweep a coil (sliding window) across a surface (image), and a beep (confidence score) tells you whether something metallic (pedestrian) is buried underneath.

### 1.3 Core Metrics in Pedestrian Detection (Bold per KTU Convention)

- **Precision:** Fraction of detections that are true pedestrians.
- **Recall (Sensitivity):** Fraction of actual pedestrians that are detected.
- **Miss Rate (MR):** $MR = 1 - Recall$ — the *primary benchmark* in pedestrian detection literature (e.g., **Caltech Pedestrian Benchmark**).
- **False Positives Per Image (FPPI):** Industry-standard axis paired with Miss Rate.
- **Intersection over Union (IoU):** $IoU = \frac{\vert B_{pred} \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_{pred} \cup B_{gt} \vert}$. A detection is **valid** only if $IoU \geq 0.5$.
- **Frames Per Second (FPS):** Real-time threshold for robotics is **≥ 15 FPS**; autonomous driving typically requires **≥ 30 FPS**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote the **Caltech Pedestrian Dataset** when discussing benchmarks. It contains ~250,000 frames with ~350,000 annotated pedestrian bounding boxes across ~10 hours of 30 Hz video, captured from a vehicle-mounted camera.

### 1.4 Evolution Timeline (KTU Board-Favourite)

| Era | Dominant Paradigm | Landmark Method | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2000–2005 | Hand-crafted features | Viola–Jones | Haar features + AdaBoost cascade |
| 2005–2013 | Gradient features | **HOG + SVM** (Dalal & Triggs, 2005) | Histogram of Oriented Gradients |
| 2008–2014 | Part-based models | **DPM** (Felzenszwalb et al., 2010) | Deformable Parts Model |
| 2013–2017 | Deep CNN features | R-CNN, Fast/Faster R-CNN | Region proposals + CNN |
| 2016–Present | End-to-End Deep Learning | **YOLO, SSD, CenterNet** | Single-shot real-time detection |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Pedestrian Detection System

A canonical pedestrian detection system (whether classical or deep) follows a **5-stage pipeline**:

1. **Image Acquisition & Pre-processing** — Resize, normalise, colour-space conversion (RGB → grayscale or HSV).
2. **Region Proposal / Sliding Window Generation** — Generate candidate locations where pedestrians *might* exist.
3. **Feature Extraction** — Convert raw pixels into a compact, discriminative descriptor (e.g., HOG, LBP, CNN features).
4. **Classification** — Binary classifier (pedestrian / non-pedestrian) with confidence score.
5. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** — Eliminate duplicate, overlapping detections.

### 2.2 The HOG + SVM Pipeline (Dalal–Triggs 2005) — *The KTU Favourite*

This is the **most heavily tested classical method** in KTU examinations. The "why" behind each step:

#### Step A: Gradient Computation
Compute horizontal $G_x$ and vertical $G_y$ gradients using 1-D centred masks:
$$G_x = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad G_y = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T$$

Magnitude and orientation:
$$\vert G \vert = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}, \quad \theta = \arctan\left(\frac{G_y}{G_x}\right)$$

> **Why?** Gradients are *invariant* to illumination changes — the silhouette of a person remains stable whether shot in sunlight or shadow.

#### Step B: Cell & Block Histograms
- Divide the detection window (typically **64 × 128 pixels**) into cells of size **8 × 8**.
- Each cell votes its pixel gradients into **9 orientation bins** (0°–180°, unsigned).
- Group cells into **blocks of 2 × 2** cells.
- Concatenate all block descriptors → final HOG feature vector of length **3780** (for 64×128 window).

#### Step C: Block Normalisation
To improve robustness to local illumination and contrast, each block is L2-normalised:
$$v_{norm} = \frac{v}{\sqrt{\vert\vert v \vert\vert_2^2 + \epsilon^2}}$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small constant to prevent division by zero.

#### Step D: Linear SVM Classification
A linear Support Vector Machine finds the optimal hyperplane:
$$f(x) = w^T x + b$$

Decision rule:
$$\text{Class}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{Pedestrian} & \text{if } f(x) > 0 \\ \text{Background} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.3 Deformable Parts Model (DPM) — Extension Worth Knowing

DPM models a pedestrian as a **root filter** (whole body) plus several **part filters** (head, arms, legs) with allowable spatial deformations. The score is:

$$S(x) = S_{root}(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{P} \max_{y_i} \left[ S_{part_i}(x, y_i) - d_i \cdot \phi_i(x, y_i) \right] - b$$

where:
- $S_{root}, S_{part_i}$ are filter response maps,
- $d_i$ is the deformation cost,
- $\phi_i$ measures displacement of part $i$ from its ideal location,
- $b$ is a bias term.

> **Why this matters in KTU:** DPM was the **VOC 2007, 2008, 2009** winner and represents the bridge between hand-crafted features and modern deep learning.

### 2.4 Deep Learning Era: YOLO-Style Single-Shot Detection

Modern robotic platforms use **single-shot detectors**. For an input image divided into an $S \times S$ grid, each grid cell predicts:

$$Y = [p_c, b_x, b_y, b_w, b_h, C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n]$$

where $p_c$ is the objectness confidence, $(b_x, b_y, b_w, b_h)$ is the bounding box, and $C_i$ are class probabilities. The final loss combines:

$$\mathcal{L} = \lambda_{coord} \mathcal{L}_{box} + \mathcal{L}_{conf} + \mathcal{L}_{cls}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gradient magnitude | $\vert G \vert = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | Foundation of HOG |
| 2 | Gradient orientation | $\theta = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$ | Foundation of HOG |
| 3 | HOG vector length (64×128) | $7 \times 15 \times 4 \times 9 = 3780$ | Asked in numericals |
| 4 | L2-normalisation | $v_{norm} = v / \sqrt{\vert\vert v \vert\vert_2^2 + \epsilon^2}$ | Illumination robustness |
| 5 | SVM decision function | $f(x) = w^T x + b$ | Classifier core |
| 6 | DPM score | $S(x) = S_{root} + \sum \max[\ldots] - b$ | Part-based detection |
| 7 | IoU | $IoU = A_{int} / A_{union}$ | Evaluation metric |
| 8 | Miss Rate | $MR = FN / (FN + TP)$ | Primary benchmark |
| 9 | Precision | $P = TP / (TP + FP)$ | Evaluation metric |
| 10 | Recall | $R = TP / (TP + FN)$ | Evaluation metric |
| 11 | NMS suppression rule | Keep box $i$ if $IoU(M, b_i) < N_t$ | De-duplication |
| 12 | YOLO grid output | $S \times S \times (5 + C)$ tensor | Modern detector |
| 13 | Multi-box loss | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{loc} + \mathcal{L}_{conf}$ | Training objective |
| 14 | Average Precision (AP) | $\int_0^1 P(R) \, dR$ | mAP across classes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on Notation:** In LaTeX, use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the pipe symbol `|`) when writing absolute value or magnitude inside markdown tables. The pipe character breaks the table parser.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Pedestrian Detection is Critical |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** (Tesla, Waymo) | Pre-collision braking; ethical AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking) |
| **Service Robots** (Pepper, Tug) | Safe navigation in malls, hospitals, airports |
| **Smart Surveillance** | Crowd density estimation, anomaly detection, fall detection in elderly care |
| **Agricultural Robotics** | Detecting humans in farm zones to halt autonomous tractors |
| **Industrial Cobots** | Speed-and-separation monitoring per ISO/TS 15066 |
| **Assistive Robotics** | Wheelchairs, guide robots for the visually impaired |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Computing the HOG Feature Vector Length

This is a **numerical problem that appears almost every KTU cycle** under Module 3. Let us derive it rigorously.

**Given:**
- Detection window: $W = 64$ pixels (width), $H = 128$ pixels (height).
- Cell size: $c \times c = 8 \times 8$ pixels.
- Block size: $2 \times 2$ cells.
- Number of orientation bins: $B = 9$.

**Step 1 — Number of cells along width:**
$$N_{cells,x} = \frac{W}{c} = \frac{64}{8} = 8 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 2 — Number of cells along height:**
$$N_{cells,y} = \frac{H}{c} = \frac{128}{8} = 16 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 3 — Number of blocks along width (with stride 1 cell):**
$$N_{blocks,x} = N_{cells,x} - (\text{block}_x - 1) = 8 - 1 = 7 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 4 — Number of blocks along height:**
$$N_{blocks,y} = N_{cells,y} - (\text{block}_y - 1) = 16 - 1 = 15 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 5 — Total number of blocks:**
$$N_{blocks} = N_{blocks,x} \times N_{blocks,y} = 7 \times 15 = 105 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 6 — Cells per block:**
$$N_{cells,block} = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 7 — Total number of cells in detection window:**
$$N_{cells,total} = N_{cells,x} \times N_{cells,y} = 8 \times 16 = 128 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 8 — Descriptor length per block:**
$$L_{block} = N_{cells,block} \times B = 4 \times 9 = 36 \text{ dimensions}$$

**Step 9 — Final HOG feature vector length:**
$$L_{HOG} = N_{blocks} \times L_{block} = 105 \times 36 = 3780$$

$$\boxed{L_{HOG} = 3780 \text{ dimensions}}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: SVM Margin Maximisation

Given a linearly separable training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{m}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the SVM seeks the hyperplane $w^T x + b = 0$ that maximises the margin $\frac{2}{\vert\vert w \vert\vert}$.

**Step 1 — Constraint formulation:**
For all training samples:
$$y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 \quad \forall i$$

**Step 2 — Optimisation problem (primal):**
$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 \quad \text{subject to } y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$$

**Step 3 — Lagrangian:**
$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \vert\vert w \vert\vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (w^T x_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Partial derivatives (KKT conditions):**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \Rightarrow w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 5 — Dual problem:**
$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$$

subject to $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.

**Step 6 — Decision function:**
$$f(x) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i (x_i \cdot x) + b \right)$$

where $SV$ denotes support vectors (those $\alpha_i > 0$).

> **Why this matters:** In pedestrian detection, only the *support vectors* (borderline samples) define the classifier. The "interior" pedestrian and non-pedestrian samples are irrelevant. This is why SVMs generalise beautifully on HOG features.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Computing IoU

**Given:**
- Predicted box: $B_p = (x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2) = (10, 10, 60, 100)$
- Ground truth box: $B_{gt} = (20, 30, 80, 90)$

**Step 1 — Compute intersection coordinates:**
$$x_{int,\min} = \max(10, 20) = 20$$
$$y_{int,\min} = \max(10, 30) = 30$$
$$x_{int,\max} = \min(60, 80) = 60$$
$$y_{int,\max} = \min(100, 90) = 90$$

**Step 2 — Intersection area:**
$$A_{int} = (60 - 20) \times (90 - 30) = 40 \times 60 = 2400 \text{ pixels}^2$$

**Step 3 — Area of predicted box:**
$$A_p = (60 - 10) \times (100 - 10) = 50 \times 90 = 4500$$

**Step 4 — Area of ground truth box:**
$$A_{gt} = (80 - 20) \times (90 - 30) = 60 \times 60 = 3600$$

**Step 5 — Union area:**
$$A_{union} = A_p + A_{gt} - A_{int} = 4500 + 3600 - 2400 = 5700$$

**Step 6 — IoU:**
$$IoU = \frac{A_{int}}{A_{union}} = \frac{2400}{5700} = 0.4211$$

**Step 7 — Verdict:**
$$0.4211 < 0.5 \Rightarrow \text{Detection is REJECTED}$$

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) Score

Given multiple overlapping boxes for the same pedestrian, NMS keeps only the *most confident* one.

**Algorithm (Mathematical Form):**

Let $\mathcal{B} = \{b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n\}$ be candidate boxes with scores $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$.

1. Initialise the keep-list $\mathcal{K} = \emptyset$ and working set $\mathcal{W} = \mathcal{B}$.

2. Find $b_m = \arg\max_{b_i \in \mathcal{W}} s_i$.

3. Move $b_m$ from $\mathcal{W}$ to $\mathcal{K}$.

4. For every remaining $b_j \in \mathcal{W}$, if $IoU(b_m, b_j) > N_t$, remove $b_j$ from $\mathcal{W}$ (where $N_t \approx 0.5$).

5. Repeat from Step 2 until $\mathcal{W} = \emptyset$.

6. Return $\mathcal{K}$.

> **Why this works:** A single pedestrian should ideally generate *one* tight bounding box. NMS enforces this by eliminating the lower-scored, redundant neighbours.

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Classical vs. Deep Pedestrian Detection

| Parameter | HOG + SVM (Classical) | DPM | CNN-based (YOLO/Faster R-CNN) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feature Type** | Hand-crafted gradients | Hand-crafted + part filters | Learned hierarchical |
| **Training Time** | Minutes–Hours | Hours | Days–Weeks (on GPU) |
| **Inference Speed** | ~1–5 FPS (CPU) | ~0.5–2 FPS (CPU) | 30–150 FPS (GPU) |
| **Accuracy (MR on Caltech)** | ~68% | ~51% | ~7–10% |
| **Memory Footprint** | < 50 MB | ~100 MB | 100 MB – 250 MB |
| **Interpretability** | High | Medium | Low (black box) |
| **Hardware for Robotics** | CPU, edge devices | CPU | GPU / NPU required |
| **Best Use Case (KTU)** | Teaching, low-power robots | Concept-learning | Modern autonomous cars |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Classical HOG + SVM Pedestrian Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Image I HxWx3] --> A1[Grayscale Conversion]
    A1 --> A2[Gradient Filter Gx Gy]
    A2 --> A3[Cell Histogram 8x8 9 bins]
    A3 --> A4[Block Descriptor 2x2 cells]
    A4 --> A5[Block Normalization L2]
    A5 --> A6[Concatenate HOG Vector 3780 dim]
    A6 --> A7[Linear SVM Classifier]
    A7 --> A8{Decision Score Positive}
    A8 -- Yes --> A9[Pedestrian Bounding Box]
    A8 -- No --> A10[Reject Window]
    A9 --> A11[Non Maximum Suppression NMS]
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12[Final Pedestrian Detections]
```

### 4.2 Modern YOLO-Based Pedestrian Detection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Input Image 416x416] --> B1[CNN Backbone ResNet Darknet]
    B1 --> B2[Feature Map 13x13x1024]
    B2 --> B3[Grid Cell Predictions]
    B3 --> B4[Objectness Score pc]
    B3 --> B5[Bounding Box bx by bw bh]
    B3 --> B6[Class Probability Pedestrian]

    B5 --> B7
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B8[NMS Filtering]
    B8 --> B9[Final Detected Pedestrians]
```

### 4.3 DPM (Deformable Parts Model) Functional Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Input Window 64x128] --> C1[HOG Feature Pyramid]
    C1 --> C2[Root Filter Response Map]
    C1 --> C3[Part Filter 1 Head]
    C1 --> C4[Part Filter 2 Torso]
    C1 --> C5[Part Filter 3 Legs]
    C2 --> C6[Combined Score Function S of x]
    C3 --> C6
    C4 --> C6
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7[Max Score Hypothesis]
    C7 --> C8{Threshold T Greater than 0}

```

### 4.4 Evaluation & Benchmarking Workflow

```mermaid

    D0[Detector Outputs] --> D1[Apply IoU Threshold 0.5]

    D2 -- No --> D4[False Positive FP]
    D0 --> D5[Ground Truth Boxes]
    D5 --> D6[Unmatched GT]

```

    E1 --> E2[Tracking Kalman Filter]
