```mermaid
    E4 --> E5{Action Decision}
    E5 -- High Risk --> E6[Emergency Stop]
    E5 -- Medium Risk --> E7[Slow Down Replan Path]
    E5 -- Low Risk --> E8[Continue Mission]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define pedestrian detection. List any two challenges faced by pedestrian detection systems in real-world robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

Pedestrian detection is a computer vision task that aims to identify and localise human figures in images or video frames by producing bounding boxes and class confidence scores. It is a binary classification plus regression problem.

Two major challenges:
1. **Occlusion** — pedestrians are frequently partially hidden by other objects or other pedestrians, breaking the silhouette.
2. **Scale variation** — pedestrians may appear at vastly different sizes depending on their distance from the camera, requiring multi-scale processing.
3. *(Acceptable also: illumination variation, pose variation, cluttered background, real-time constraints.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**

**Q2. What is the Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) descriptor? Why is it preferred over raw pixel values for pedestrian detection?**

**Model Answer:**

HOG is a feature descriptor that counts occurrences of gradient orientation in localised portions of an image. For a 64×128 detection window with 8×8 cells and 2×2 blocks, the resulting HOG vector has 3780 dimensions.

It is preferred over raw pixel values because:
1. **Illumination invariance** — gradients capture edge structure, not absolute intensity, making it robust to lighting changes.
2. **Local spatial encoding** — block-based histograms preserve the geometric distribution of gradients, which encodes the human silhouette.
3. **Dimensionality reduction** — raw 64×128×3 = 24,576 values are compressed into 3780 informative features.
4. **Discriminative power** — empirical results (Dalal & Triggs, 2005) showed HOG + SVM drastically outperforms pixel-based classifiers on pedestrian benchmarks.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the classical pipeline of pedestrian detection using HOG features and an SVM classifier. Discuss the role of each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The HOG + SVM pipeline consists of the following stages:

1. **Image Pre-processing:** The input image is converted to grayscale and optionally gamma-normalised to reduce the effect of lighting variations.

2. **Gradient Computation:** Horizontal $G_x$ and vertical $G_y$ gradients are computed using 1-D masks. The magnitude $\vert G \vert = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ and orientation $\theta = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$ are computed for every pixel.

3. **Cell Histogram:** The image is divided into 8×8 cells. For each cell, the gradient orientations of its 64 pixels are binned into 9 unsigned orientation bins (0°–180°). Each pixel votes with a weight equal to its gradient magnitude.

4. **Block Descriptor & Normalisation:** Adjacent 2×2 cells form a block. The 4 × 9 = 36-dimensional descriptor of each block is L2-normalised for illumination robustness.

5. **Concatenation:** All 105 block descriptors (7×15) are concatenated into a 3780-dimensional HOG feature vector.

6. **SVM Classification:** A pre-trained linear SVM computes $f(x) = w^T x + b$. If $f(x) > 0$, the window is classified as a pedestrian.

7. **Sliding Window & Multi-scale:** The above process is repeated across the image at multiple positions and scales.

8. **NMS:** Duplicate detections are merged using Non-Maximum Suppression with IoU threshold 0.5.

*(Neat block diagram to be drawn showing: Image → Pre-process → Gradient → Cell → Block → Normalise → HOG Vector → SVM → Detection.)*

**Q.A.(b)** Compute the length of the HOG feature vector for a detection window of size 128 × 64 with cells of 8 × 8, blocks of 2 × 2 cells, and 9 orientation bins. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $W = 128$, $H = 64$, cell = $8 \times 8$, block = $2 \times 2$ cells, bins $B = 9$.

$$\text{Step 1: Cells along width} = \frac{128}{8} = 16$$
$$\text{Step 2: Cells along height} = \frac{64}{8} = 8$$
$$\text{Step 3: Blocks along width} = 16 - 1 = 15$$
$$\text{Step 4: Blocks along height} = 8 - 1 = 7$$
$$\text{Step 5: Total blocks} = 15 \times 7 = 105$$
$$\text{Step 6: Cells per block} = 2 \times 2 = 4$$
$$\text{Step 7: Descriptor per block} = 4 \times 9 = 36$$
$$\text{Step 8: Final HOG length} = 105 \times 36 = 3780$$

$$\boxed{L_{HOG} = 3780}$$

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B.(a)** Explain the Deformable Parts Model (DPM) for pedestrian detection. How does it improve upon the rigid HOG + SVM approach? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Deformable Parts Model (Felzenszwalb et al., 2010) represents a pedestrian as a **root filter** (whole body) plus several higher-resolution **part filters** (head, shoulders, arms, legs). Each part filter is allowed spatial displacement from its ideal anchor location, penalised by a deformation cost.

The DPM score for a hypothesis $x$ is:

$$S(x) = S_{root}(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{P} \max_{y_i} \left[ S_{part_i}(x, y_i) - d_i \cdot \phi_i(x, y_i) \right] - b$$

**Improvements over rigid HOG + SVM:**

1. **Handles articulation** — humans are non-rigid; arms and legs move. DPM explicitly models this via part displacement.
2. **Higher resolution for parts** — parts are computed at 2× resolution, capturing finer details (e.g., face) that the root filter misses.
3. **Better occlusion handling** — if a leg is occluded, the head and torso filters can still contribute, allowing partial detection.
4. **Latent SVM training** — the part locations are *latent variables* discovered during training, removing the need for manual part annotation.
5. **Improved accuracy** — DPM won PASCAL VOC 2007–2009 and significantly reduced Miss Rate on Caltech Pedestrian compared to rigid HOG + SVM.

**Q.B.(b)** With a diagram, describe the working of the YOLO single-shot detector for pedestrian detection in autonomous vehicles. Mention any two advantages over region-based detectors. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**YOLO Working:**

1. The input image is resized to $448 \times 448$ (YOLOv1) or $416 \times 416$ (later versions).
2. A single CNN (e.g., Darknet-19, Darknet-53) processes the image in **one forward pass**.
3. The image is conceptually divided into an $S \times S$ grid (e.g., $13 \times 13$).
4. Each grid cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes, an objectness score $p_c$, and class probabilities $C_i$.
5. The output tensor is $S \times S \times (B \times 5 + C)$ — for pedestrian-only: $13 \times 13 \times (5 \times 5 + 1) = 13 \times 13 \times 30$.
6. NMS eliminates redundant overlapping boxes; remaining boxes are final pedestrian detections.

**Diagram (description):** *[Draw the YOLO pipeline as Input → CNN Backbone → Grid Division → Per-cell Predictions → NMS → Output Boxes.]*

**Two advantages over region-based detectors (R-CNN family):**
1. **Speed** — YOLO runs at 45–150 FPS, suitable for real-time autonomous driving. R-CNN variants are limited to 5–10 FPS.
2. **End-to-end training** — the entire model is trained jointly on a single loss function, optimising localisation and classification simultaneously. R-CNN trains region proposals, CNN features, and SVM classifier in three separate stages.
3. **Global context** — YOLO sees the entire image during both training and inference, reducing false positives on background patches.
4. **Lower latency** — single forward pass enables ultra-low-latency perception, critical for collision-avoidance robotics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students **commonly lose marks** on pedestrian detection questions in the following ways:
>
> 1. **Confusing HOG vector length** — The most common error is forgetting that *block overlap* reduces the number of independent blocks (it is $N_{blocks,x} = N_{cells,x} - 1$, not $N_{cells,x}$). This single mistake loses 3 marks.
> 2. **Writing "HOG = 64 × 128 = 8192"** — Never treat HOG as raw pixels. Always show the 105 × 36 derivation.
> 3. **Forgetting L2-normalisation** — Examiners specifically test whether you know *why* HOG works (illumination robustness via normalisation). Omitting this loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Miss Rate and Precision** — Miss Rate is $1 - Recall$ (lower is better), plotted on the y-axis. Precision is $TP / (TP+FP)$. Confusing these will be heavily penalised.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — In Q.A.(a) and Q.B.(b), a block diagram is **mandatory**. Examiners allocate at least 1–2 marks for the diagram. Hand-drawn ASCII / neat boxes are acceptable.
> 6. **Citing non-existent references** — Do not invent author names or years. Dalal & Triggs (2005), Felzenszwalb et al. (2010), Redmon et al. (2016) are the *only* safe citations.
> 7. **Confusing "objectness" with "class probability"** in YOLO — $p_c$ is *objectness* (is there any object?), and $C_i$ is *class probability* (is it a pedestrian?). Conflating them costs marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pedestrian Detection (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Pedestrian detection = binary classification + bounding-box regression for humans in images/video.
- **Core Pipeline:** Image → Pre-process → Sliding Window → Feature Extraction → Classifier → NMS → Output.
- **HOG (Dalal & Triggs 2005):** Hand-crafted gradient histogram; 8×8 cells, 2×2 blocks, 9 bins, 64×128 window → **3780-dim vector**.
- **SVM:** Linear hyperplane classifier; only support vectors matter; loss = hinge loss.
- **DPM (Felzenszwalb 2010):** Root + parts model with deformation cost; handles articulation and partial occlusion.
- **YOLO (Redmon 2016):** Single-shot CNN detector; grid-based prediction; real-time (45+ FPS).
- **Faster R-CNN:** Region Proposal Network (RPN) + detector; two-stage but high accuracy.
- **Key Metrics:** Precision, Recall, Miss Rate, FPPI, IoU, mAP, FPS.
- **IoU ≥ 0.5** is the standard threshold for a *valid* detection.
- **Real-time threshold** for robotics: **≥ 15 FPS**; autonomous driving: **≥ 30 FPS**.
- **Caltech Pedestrian Dataset** is the standard benchmark — quote its Miss Rate.
- **Challenges:** Occlusion, scale variation, pose variation, lighting, real-time constraints, adversarial attacks.
- **Engineering Applications:** Autonomous vehicles, service robots, surveillance, cobots, assistive robotics.
- **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:** Skipping block-overlap step in HOG math, omitting L2-normalisation rationale, confusing Miss Rate with Precision, missing the block diagram, citing fake references.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotics - Robotic Perception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robotics - Robotic Perception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *Robotic Perception* is the interdisciplinary sub-field of robotics concerned with enabling a robot to **sense, interpret, and reason** about its physical environment using data acquired from heterogeneous sensor modalities (vision, depth, range, tactile, proprioceptive, and inertial sensors), producing a structured symbolic or geometric representation that downstream planning and control modules can consume for autonomous decision-making.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the *Perception–Planning–Action* loop forms the cognitive backbone of any intelligent robotic agent. Perception is the *upstream* gateway — garbage in, garbage out — meaning the entire autonomy stack is only as robust as its perception front-end.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into an unfamiliar room. The moment you remove the blindfold, your eyes flood your brain with raw pixels — colors, edges, shadows. You do **not** instantly "know" the room. Your visual cortex first segments the scene into objects, estimates depth (how far the table is), recognises that the moving blob is your pet dog, and stitches all of this into a *mental map*. **Robotic Perception is exactly this cognitive translation pipeline, but implemented in silicon and mathematics.** The camera is the retina; the CNN is the visual cortex; SLAM is the hippocampus; and the occupancy grid is the mental map.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric — End-to-End Latency Budget:** A perception pipeline for an autonomous mobile robot must complete (sense → fuse → recognise → localise) within **≤ 100 ms** to be considered real-time, with the **Mean Average Precision (mAP)** on object detection being the standard performance benchmark in the **range 0.6 – 0.9**.

### The Five Pillars of Robotic Perception

| Pillar | Primary Sensor | Output Representation |
|---|---|---|
| **Vision Perception** | RGB / RGB-D Cameras | Pixels, feature maps, bounding boxes |
| **Range Perception** | LiDAR, Radar, Ultrasonic | 3D Point Clouds, Occupancy Grids |
| **Tactile & Force Perception** | Tactile sensors, F/T sensors | Pressure maps, wrench vectors |
| **Proprioceptive Perception** | Encoders, IMUs | Pose, velocity, acceleration |
| **Multi-Modal Fusion** | All of the above | Unified belief state of the world |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Hook:** A single-sentence viva-ready definition: *"Robotic Perception is the inverse problem of recovering semantic and geometric state of the world from noisy, partial, multi-rate sensor observations."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Perception Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

1. **Sensing (Data Acquisition):** Physical transducers convert environmental energy (photons, acoustic waves, EM pulses) into electrical signals. Sampling rate, dynamic range, and field-of-view (FoV) are critical specs.
2. **Pre-Processing:** Noise filtering (Gaussian, Median), distortion correction (lens radial/tangential), and time synchronisation across sensors.
3. **Feature Extraction:** Identification of salient primitives — edges (Canny), corners (Harris), blobs (SIFT/SURF/ORB), or learned features via CNN backbones (ResNet, EfficientNet).
4. **Sensor Fusion:** Combination of heterogeneous data streams using probabilistic filters (Kalman, EKF, UKF) or learned fusion (transformer-based).
5. **World Modelling:** Construction of metric (occupancy grid), topological (graph), or semantic (object-labelled) representations.
6. **Estimation & Inference:** Bayesian inference, particle filters, or deep neural networks to estimate the *true* world state from noisy observations.
7. **Output Hand-off:** The structured belief state is published (often via ROS topics) to the planning module.

### 2.2 The Pinhole Camera Model — The Foundation of Vision Perception

A 3D world point $\mathbf{X}_w = (X, Y, Z, 1)^T$ in homogeneous coordinates is projected onto the 2D image plane through the **intrinsic matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ and the **extrinsic matrix** $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$.

The complete projection equation is:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \, \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where the intrinsic matrix is decomposed as:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $f_x, f_y$ — Focal lengths in pixel units (typically **300 – 1500 pixels**).
- $c_x, c_y$ — Principal point coordinates (image centre, ideally $(W/2, H/2)$).
- $\gamma$ — Skew coefficient (**0** for modern rectified cameras).
- $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$ — Rotation of camera w.r.t. world frame.
- $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ — Translation of camera origin w.r.t. world frame.
- $s$ — Arbitrary scale factor (homogeneous coordinate).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pinhole Projection | $s\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]\mathbf{X}$ | Maps 3D world to 2D image | pixels |
| Lens Distortion (Radial) | $x_d = x(1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4 + k_3 r^6)$ | Barrel/pincushion correction | unitless |
| Depth from Stereo | $Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | Triangulation using disparity $d$ | metres |
| LiDAR Range Equation | $P_r = P_t \frac{G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Received power for range $R$ | Watts |
| Kalman Predict | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}\hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + \mathbf{B}u_k$ | A priori state estimate | mixed |
| Kalman Update | $\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}\mathbf{H}^T(\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}\mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$ | Optimal gain (Kalman filter) | mixed |
| IoU (Detection Metric) | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Bounding box overlap | $[0,1]$ |
| mAP | $\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{AP}_i$ | Mean Average Precision over classes | $[0,1]$ |
| Occupancy Update | $l_t = l_{t-1} + \log\frac{p(m\vert z_t)}{1 - p(m\vert z_t)}$ | Log-odds Bayesian grid update | nats |
| Optical Flow (Lucas–Kanade) | $\mathbf{v} = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{b}$ | Local pixel velocity | px/frame |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` not `\|` or `|` inside KTU exam answer sheets rendered in markdown** to avoid breaking column alignment. This is a common OCR / digital valuation pitfall.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Multi-modal perception fuses cameras + radar + LiDAR at 30 Hz to enable highway-speed autonomy.
- **Industrial Manipulators (KUKA, ABB):** Vision-guided pick-and-place with sub-millimetre accuracy.
- **Medical Robotics (Da Vinci):** Force & tactile perception for haptic feedback to surgeons.
- **Humanoid Robots (Boston Dynamics Atlas, Tesla Optimus):** Whole-body proprioception + vision for dynamic balancing.
- **Agri-Robotics:** Weed-vs-crop semantic segmentation using deep CNNs on multispectral imagery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Depth from Stereo Vision

We derive the relationship between disparity and depth for a rectified stereo camera pair. This is a **board-favourite derivation** for KTU 14-mark questions.

**Given:** Two cameras with identical focal length $f$, separated by a baseline $B$, both rectified such that their optical axes are parallel. A 3D world point $\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)$ projects to pixel coordinates $(u_L, v)$ in the left image and $(u_R, v)$ in the right image (same vertical coordinate because of rectification).

**Step 1 — Similar Triangles (Left Camera):**
The projection of $\mathbf{P}$ on the left image plane satisfies:

$$\frac{u_L - c_x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Similar Triangles (Right Camera):**
Define the right camera origin to be at $X = -B$ along the baseline. The projection equation is:

$$\frac{u_R - c_x}{f} = \frac{X + B}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract Right from Left:**
Subtracting Step 2 from Step 1, the $X/Z$ terms cancel cleanly:

$$\frac{(u_L - u_R)}{f} = \frac{X}{Z} - \frac{X + B}{Z} = -\frac{B}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Define Disparity:**
The disparity $d$ is defined as the horizontal pixel difference between the two projections:

$$d = u_L - u_R$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Depth Z:**
Substituting $d$ into the result of Step 3:

$$\frac{d}{f} = \frac{B}{Z}$$

Therefore, the depth of the point is:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}}$$

**Step 6 — Sensitivity Analysis (KTU Favourite Follow-up):**
The derivative of depth w.r.t. disparity tells us how error in disparity propagates to error in depth:

$$\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} = -\frac{f \cdot B}{d^2} = -\frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B}$$

This is the **inverse-square law of stereo error propagation** — depth error grows quadratically with distance, which is why long-range stereo requires wider baselines or active illumination (structured light / ToF).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sensor Modalities

| Property | Monocular Camera | Stereo Camera | LiDAR | Radar | IMU |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | 0.5 – 100 m | 0.5 – 30 m | 0.1 – 200 m | 0.1 – 250 m | N/A (self) |
| **Depth Accuracy** | Poor (scale ambiguous) | High (sub-cm @ close) | Very High (±2 cm) | Moderate (±10 cm) | Drift: ~1°/hr |
| **Works in Fog/Rain** | Poor | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| **Night Operation** | Poor (needs IR) | Poor | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| **Cost (USD)** | $20 – $500 | $100 – $2000 | $1000 – $75000 | $200 – $2000 | $10 – $500 |
| **Output Type** | RGB pixels | Disparity map | Point cloud | Range-Doppler | Accel/Ang vel |
| **Field of View** | 60° – 120° | 60° – 120° | 360° (spinning) | 20° – 150° | N/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Any 14-mark question on "Compare sensor modalities" **must** include both a quantitative table and a qualitative discussion of *sensor fusion* rationale. Failing to mention fusion costs a minimum of 3 marks.

### 3.3 Kalman Filter — The Mathematical Heart of Sensor Fusion

For a linear system with state $\mathbf{x}_k$, the discrete Kalman filter proceeds in two phases:

**Prediction Step (Time Update):**
$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$$
$$\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$$

**Update Step (Measurement Update):**
$$\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k (\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1})$$
$$\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}$$

Where $\mathbf{Q}_k$ is process noise covariance and $\mathbf{R}_k$ is measurement noise covariance. The **Kalman Gain** $\mathbf{K}_k$ automatically balances trust between prediction and measurement — high $\mathbf{R}_k$ (noisy sensor) → low gain → trust model more.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Robotic Perception Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Environment Stimuli] --> B[Sensor Array]
    B --> B1[RGB Camera]
    B --> B2[Depth Camera]
    B --> B3[LiDAR]
    B --> B4[IMU]
    B --> B5[Tactile]
    B1 --> C[Pre-Processing Unit]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> D[Feature Extraction Layer]
    D --> D1[Classical CV]
    D --> D2[Deep CNN Backbone]
    D1 --> E[Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[World State Estimator]
    F --> F1[Pose and Localization]
    F --> F2[Semantic Map]
    F --> F3[Object Tracking]
    F1 --> G[Belief State Publisher]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Planning and Decision Module]
    H --> I[Motor Control]
    I --> J[Robot Actuators]
    J -. Feedback Loop .-> A
```

### 4.2 Pinhole Camera Projection Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[World Point X Y Z] --> B[Extrinsic Transform R t]
    B --> C[Camera Frame Xc Yc Zc]
    C --> D[Intrinsic Matrix K]
    D --> E[Perspective Divide]
    E --> F[Image Pixel u v]
    F --> G[Lens Distortion Correction]
    G --> H[Rectified Image Coordinates]
```

### 4.3 Sensor Fusion Hierarchy (JDL Data Fusion Model adapted for Robotics)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Level 0 Source Preprocessing] --> L1[Level 1 Object Refinement]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2 Situation Refinement]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Threat and Risk Refinement]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Process Refinement]
    L4 -. Improves .-> L0
    L4 -. Improves .-> L1
    L4 -. Improves .-> L2
    L4 -. Improves .-> L3
```

### 4.4 SLAM Perception Loop (Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Robot Motion Command] --> B[Odometry Prediction]
    B --> C[Prior Pose Estimate]
    C --> D[Sensor Observation]
    D --> E[Feature Matching and Data Association]
    E --> F[Update Pose and Map]
    F --> G[Updated Global Map]
    G --> H[Loop Closure Detection]
    H -. Corrects Drift .-> F
    H --> I[Optimised Pose Graph]
    I --> J[Published Map and Pose]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid `flowchart TB` is used here for the perception pipeline (most KTU-typical rendering). For SLAM, the loop closure back-edge is critical — examiners often deduct marks for missing this feedback arrow.

### 4.5 Comparison Schematic — Classical vs. Learning-Based Perception

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLASS[Classical Perception Pipeline]
        A1[Raw Image] --> A2[Hand-Crafted Features SIFT HOG] --> A3[Classical Classifier SVM] --> A4[Object Label]
    end
    subgraph LEARN[Deep Learning Perception Pipeline]
        B1[Raw Image] --> B2[Convolutional Backbone ResNet] --> B3[End to End Learned Features] --> B4[Object Label and Bounding Box]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define Robotic Perception. List any four key sensors used in a perception system.*

**Model Answer:**
Robotic Perception is the capability of a robot to interpret sensory data from its environment to extract meaningful information such as object identity, location, and motion, enabling autonomous behaviour. It bridges raw sensor signals and high-level decision making.

**Four Key Sensors:**
1. **RGB / RGB-D Cameras** — for visual and depth information.
2. **LiDAR** — for accurate 3D range measurements.
3. **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** — for acceleration and angular velocity.
4. **Tactile / Force-Torque Sensors** — for contact and manipulation feedback.

*(Examiner expects sensor + one-line purpose. 3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 × 4 for each sensor with purpose.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors with two examples each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Sense robot's *internal* state | Sense robot's *external* environment |
| **Examples** | Wheel encoders, IMU, joint potentiometers | Camera, LiDAR, ultrasonic, microphone |
| **Frame of Reference** | Robot's own body frame | World / object frame |
| **Use Case** | Odometry, balance, motor control | Navigation, recognition, mapping |

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **pinhole camera model** and derive the **camera projection matrix** relating 3D world coordinates to 2D image coordinates. Mention the role of intrinsic and extrinsic parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two rectified stereo cameras with focal length $f = 800$ pixels and baseline $B = 0.12$ m observe a point with disparity $d = 40$ pixels. Compute the depth $Z$ of the point. If the disparity measurement has an uncertainty of $\pm 0.5$ pixel, estimate the depth uncertainty $\Delta Z$ at this range. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Pinhole Camera Model:**

The pinhole camera approximates the lens as an infinitesimal aperture through which light rays from a 3D scene pass and project onto an image plane, inverting the image.

**Setup:** Let the camera optical centre be at the origin, with the $Z$-axis along the optical axis. A 3D point $\mathbf{X}_c = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ in camera coordinates projects to image-plane coordinates $(x, y)$ via similar triangles:

$$x = f \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \quad y = f \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

To convert to pixel coordinates $(u, v)$, we apply the affine transformation from metres to pixels and translate by the principal point $(c_x, c_y)$:

$$u = f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \quad v = f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

In homogeneous form, with scale factor $s = Z_c$:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}$$

**Intrinsic Parameters:** Encapsulated in $\mathbf{K}$. These describe the camera's internal geometry (focal length, principal point, skew). They are constant for a given camera and obtained via calibration (e.g., Zhang's method using a chessboard).

**Extrinsic Parameters:** The rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$ that transform a point from the world frame to the camera frame:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Combined Equation:**

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The composite $3 \times 4$ matrix $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is the **Camera Projection Matrix** (or Camera Matrix). It has 11 degrees of freedom: 5 intrinsic + 3 rotation + 3 translation (the 12th parameter is a global scale).

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):** Optical centre → image plane, with principal axis, focal length $f$, and a 3D point projecting through the pinhole.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $f = 800$ px, $B = 0.12$ m, $d = 40$ px.

**Step 1 — Compute depth $Z$:**
Apply the stereo depth formula:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{800 \times 0.12}{40} = \frac{96}{40} = 2.4 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute depth sensitivity to disparity:**
Differentiate $Z = fB/d$ with respect to $d$:

$$\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} = -\frac{fB}{d^2} = -\frac{Z^2}{fB} = -\frac{(2.4)^2}{800 \times 0.12} = -\frac{5.76}{96} = -0.06 \text{ m/px}$$

**Step 3 — Propagate disparity uncertainty to depth uncertainty:**
For a small uncertainty $\Delta d = \pm 0.5$ px:

$$\Delta Z = \left\vert \frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} \right\vert \cdot \Delta d = 0.06 \times 0.5 = 0.03 \text{ m} = \pm 3 \text{ cm}$$

**Final Answer:** The point is at depth $\mathbf{Z = 2.4 \text{ m}}$ with uncertainty $\mathbf{\Delta Z = \pm 3 \text{ cm}}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **Kalman Filter** algorithm for sensor fusion. Derive the Kalman Gain expression and explain its role in balancing prediction and measurement updates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the **SLAM (Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping)** problem. Discuss the role of **loop closure** in correcting accumulated drift. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Kalman Filter:**

The Kalman Filter is an optimal recursive Bayesian estimator for linear dynamical systems with Gaussian noise. It maintains a belief state $(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k, \mathbf{P}_k)$ — the mean and covariance — over the true state.

**State-Space Model:**
- Process model: $\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k + \mathbf{w}_k$, with $\mathbf{w}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q}_k)$
- Measurement model: $\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k$, with $\mathbf{v}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_k)$

**Prediction Step:**
$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1\vert k-1} + \mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{u}_k$$
$$\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} = \mathbf{F}_k \mathbf{P}_{k-1\vert k-1} \mathbf{F}_k^T + \mathbf{Q}_k$$

**Update Step — Derivation of Kalman Gain:**
The posterior covariance minimised w.r.t. $\mathbf{K}_k$ is:

$$\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1}$$

The trace is minimised by setting $\frac{\partial \text{tr}(\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k})}{\partial \mathbf{K}_k} = 0$:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{K}_k = \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R}_k)^{-1}}$$

**Role of $\mathbf{K}_k$:**
- If $\mathbf{R}_k \to 0$ (perfect sensor): $\mathbf{K}_k \to \mathbf{I}$, measurement fully trusted.
- If $\mathbf{P}_{k\vert k-1} \to 0$ (perfect model): $\mathbf{K}_k \to \mathbf{0}$, measurement ignored.
- The Kalman Gain **automatically weights** the innovation $\mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k\vert k-1}$ based on relative noise.

**Part (b) — SLAM:**

**Block Diagram (described in words for exam):**
Robot motion → Odometry → **Prediction of pose & map** → Sensor observation → **Data association** (matching observed features to map features) → **Update pose & map** → Maintained map and pose estimate → **Loop closure detection** → Pose graph optimisation → Corrected map.

**Loop Closure Discussion:**
Over time, dead-reckoning errors (wheel slip, IMU drift) accumulate, causing the robot to believe it is at a slightly different pose than its true location. When the robot **revisits** a previously mapped area, loop closure detection (e.g., via bag-of-words place recognition like **DBoW2** or deep features like **NetVLAD**) identifies the revisit. The pose graph is then globally optimised (e.g., using **g2o** or **GTSAM** solvers based on Levenberg–Marquardt) to **redistribute the accumulated error** across all poses, dramatically reducing drift.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** homogeneous coordinates with screen coordinates — they are *not* the same.
> 2. In numerical problems, **always state the units** in the final answer (e.g., $Z = 2.4$ m, *not* just 2.4).
> 3. For Kalman filter derivations, **memorise the matrix dimensions**: $\mathbf{K}_k$ is $n \times m$, $\mathbf{P}$ is $n \times n$, $\mathbf{H}$ is $m \times n$.
> 4. In SLAM diagrams, **loop closure MUST be a back-edge** to a previous node — not just a forward arrow. Marks are specifically allotted for this.
> 5. If the question says "explain", a 7-mark sub-part needs **at least 4–5 sentences + diagram**, not just a bullet list.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Robotic Perception** = the *upstream* of the Sense → Think → Act loop; it converts raw sensor data into a usable world model.
- ✅ The **Pinhole Camera Model** uses intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ (internal geometry) and extrinsic $[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]$ (pose) to project 3D → 2D.
- ✅ **Stereo depth formula**: $Z = \frac{fB}{d}$ — depth error grows as $Z^2$ (inverse-square law of stereo).
- ✅ **Five sensor pillars**: Vision, Range, Tactile, Proprioceptive, Multi-Modal Fusion.
- ✅ **Kalman Filter** = optimal linear-Gaussian estimator; two steps: Predict & Update. The **Kalman Gain** auto-balances trust in model vs. measurement.
- ✅ **SLAM** = simultaneous pose estimation and map building; **loop closure** is essential for global consistency.
- ✅ **Performance metrics**: IoU, mAP, RMSE, end-to-end latency (target ≤ 100 ms), depth accuracy in cm.
- ✅ **Multi-modal fusion** is the *only* way to achieve all-weather, all-lighting robust perception for real robots.
- ✅ **Classical CV** (SIFT, HOG) is interpretable; **Deep Learning** (CNN, Transformer) is more accurate but data-hungry and a "black box."
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers: **Always include a diagram, derivation, and a numerical example** — examiners allocate marks roughly **3-4-3-4** across these components.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization and Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Localization and Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Localization — Formal Definition

**Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot estimates its own pose (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) relative to a given coordinate frame, using sensor measurements and control inputs. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, it is treated as a *state estimation* problem, where the state of the robot is inferred from a probabilistic combination of sensor data, motion commands, and a prior belief.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** Localization is the problem of determining the pose of a robot relative to a known map, or determining the robot's pose while simultaneously building that map.

## 1.2 Mapping — Formal Definition

**Mapping** is the process of constructing a spatial representation of the environment from the sequence of sensor observations collected as the robot moves. The output may be a 2D occupancy grid, a 3D point cloud, a topological graph, or a landmark-based feature map.

## 1.3 SLAM — The Coupled Problem

When localization and mapping happen **simultaneously** (without a prior map), the problem becomes **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)**. It is famously described as a "chicken-and-egg" problem: an accurate map is needed for localization, yet an accurate pose estimate is needed to build the map.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics in KTU-style questions:**
> - **Accuracy:** Pose error in meters / radians
> - **Convergence Time:** Time taken for belief to stabilize
> - **Computational Complexity:** Often **$O(N \cdot M)$** for grid maps with $N$ cells and $M$ particles
> - **Robustness:** Performance under sensor noise and dynamic environments

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "Walking Blindfolded in a New House"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and placed inside an unfamiliar house. You can feel walls with your right hand, count your footsteps, and hear echoes. Localization is like remembering *where you are right now* based on how many steps you took and the walls you just touched. Mapping is like sketching the house's floor plan on a notepad **as** you move. SLAM is the harder case — you are doing *both* at the same time, with no notepad from a previous visit.

This analogy captures the three key challenges KTU examiners love to test: **uncertainty accumulation (drift)**, **data association** (which wall is which?), and **the loop-closure problem** (recognizing a place you have been before).

## 1.5 Three Classical Localization Problems

| Problem Variant | Initial Knowledge | Difficulty | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | Robot starts at known pose, tracks small drift | Easy | Local estimation |
| **Global Localization** | Robot starts anywhere, must find its pose | Medium | Global search |
| **Kidnapped Robot** | Robot is teleported mid-run; must recover | Hard | Recovery & re-localization |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Probabilistic Foundation — Bayes Filter

The entire localization pipeline rests on recursive **Bayesian filtering**. The robot maintains a *belief* $\text{bel}(x_t)$ over its pose $x_t$ at time $t$, updated through two steps:

### Step 1 — Prediction (Motion Update)
The belief is propagated forward using the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$, where $u_t$ is the control input.

### Step 2 — Correction (Measurement Update)
The predicted belief is multiplied by the measurement likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$, where $z_t$ is the sensor reading and $m$ is the map.

### Why It Works
The Bayes filter is *optimal* in the sense that it propagates the *full* posterior distribution, not just a point estimate. It is also recursive — older measurements do not need to be stored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whenever a question says "Explain the Bayes filter for localization," the examiner expects a diagram of the **predict–correct loop** and the two key equations (prediction + update).

## 2.2 Types of Maps — Theoretical Comparison

| Map Type | Representation | Storage | Best For | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Metric (Occupancy Grid)** | 2D / 3D grid of cells | $O(N)$ cells | Dense indoor nav. | Memory hungry |
| **Feature / Landmark** | List of $(x, y, \text{id})$ tuples | $O(K)$ | Sparse outdoor env. | Needs feature extract. |
| **Topological** | Graph $G = (V, E)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Hallway / corridor nav. | Hard to compute distance |
| **Hybrid (Grid + Topology)** | Combined | $O(N + \vert V \vert)$ | Large-scale maps | Complex fusion |

> Use `\vert` for absolute value / cardinality to keep markdown tables clean.

## 2.3 Landmark-Based Observation Model

For a landmark at position $(x_l, y_l)$ observed from robot pose $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$, the **range** and **bearing** measurements are:

$$r = \sqrt{(x_l - x_t)^2 + (y_l - y_t)^2}$$

$$\phi = \text{atan2}(y_l - y_t, x_l - x_t) - \theta_t$$

The signature $(r, \phi)$ is what the robot uses for **data association** — matching current observations to previously seen landmarks.

## 2.4 Occupancy Grid Map Update (Log-Odds Form)

Let $m_i$ denote a grid cell, and $l(m_i) = \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}$ be its log-odds. The update rule is:

$$l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(m_i, z_t, x_t) - l_0$$

where $l_0 = \log \frac{p(m_i = 0.5)}{1 - p(m_i = 0.5)} = 0$ is the prior log-odds. This additive form is **fast** to compute, which is why KTU questions often ask for the log-odds derivation.

## 2.5 Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization)

For non-Gaussian beliefs (e.g., global localization), we approximate the posterior with $M$ weighted samples (particles) $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$:

- **Sampling:** Draw $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$
- **Weighting:** $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$
- **Resampling:** Discard low-weight particles, duplicate high-weight ones.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU ESE — Memorize the Structure, Not Just the Symbols.**

| Equation | Meaning | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Bayes filter recursion | Localization theory |
| $w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | Particle weight update | MCL, global localization |
| $l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - l_0$ | Log-odds grid update | Occupancy grid mapping |
| $x_t = x_{t-1} + \Delta x \cos \theta_{t-1} - \Delta y \sin \theta_{t-1}$ | Odometry $x$ update | Motion model |
| $y_t = y_{t-1} + \Delta x \sin \theta_{t-1} + \Delta y \cos \theta_{t-1}$ | Odometry $y$ update | Motion model |
| $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \Delta \theta$ | Odometry heading update | Motion model |
| $r = \sqrt{(x_l - x_t)^2 + (y_l - y_t)^2}$ | Landmark range | Observation model |
| $\phi = \text{atan2}(\cdot) - \theta_t$ | Landmark bearing | Observation model |
| $\text{Effective Particles} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | $N_{\text{eff}}$ — resampling trigger | Particle filter |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Real-time lane-level localization using LiDAR + HD maps.
- **Warehouse Robotics (AMR):** Multi-floor SLAM in Amazon-style fulfilment centres.
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots:** Wall-following + bumper-based occupancy grid.
- **Search & Rescue (UAV):** GPS-denied SLAM in collapsed buildings.
- **AR/VR:** Inside-out tracking on Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro — same mathematical backbone.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter Recursion (Localization)

We start from the **posterior** we want to estimate:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Row 1 — Apply the Bayes rule** (with normalizing constant $\eta$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) &= \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 2 — Markov assumption on measurements** (the current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_t$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) &= p(z_t \mid x_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 3 — Law of total probability** over the previous pose $x_{t-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) &= \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 4 — Markov assumption on motion** (the current pose depends only on the previous pose and current control $u_t$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) &= p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 5 — Final Bayes filter recursion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) &= \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **textbook equation** the KTU examiner expects in any "derive the localization update" question.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Particle Filter Weight Update (MCL)

**Starting point:** The belief is represented as a sum of weighted Dirac deltas:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bel}(x_t) &\approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \cdot \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 1 — Substitute the Bayes filter posterior:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_t^{[i]} &\propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \cdot p(x_t^{[i]} \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t) \cdot w_{t-1}^{[i]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 2 — Since we sampled** $x_t^{[i]}$ from the proposal $q = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$, the motion term cancels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_t^{[i]} &\propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \cdot w_{t-1}^{[i]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 3 — Normalize so weights sum to unity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_t^{[i]} &\leftarrow \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_t^{[j]}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the famous **importance sampling** step that powers Monte Carlo Localization.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Occupancy Grid Log-Odds Update

**Step 1 — Start from the log-odds definition for cell $m_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l(m_i) &= \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes rule** and expand the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_t(m_i) &= l_{t-1}(m_i) + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - \log \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the last term as the prior** $l_0$. For a uniform prior $p(m_i) = 0.5$, $l_0 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_t(m_i) &= l_{t-1}(m_i) + l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - l_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to occupancy probability** if needed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(m_i) &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l(m_i)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Algorithm | Belief Type | Computational Cost | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EKF Localization** | Gaussian | $O(K^2)$ per step | Closed-form, fast | Linearization errors |
| **Particle Filter (MCL)** | Non-parametric | $O(M \cdot K)$ | Handles multi-modal | Particle depletion |
| **Grid-based (Histogram)** | Discrete grid | $O(N)$ | Global localization ready | Coarse resolution |
| **AMCL (Adaptive MCL)** | Non-parametric | $O(M \cdot K)$, $M$ adaptive | Solves kidnapped problem | KLD-sampling tuning |

> Use `\vert` style fences to keep table parsers happy. $K$ is the number of landmarks, $M$ the particle count, and $N$ the number of grid cells.

## 3.5 Numerical Sanity Check — Bayes Filter Update

Suppose a robot has a uniform prior over 3 cells: $p(x) = [0.33, 0.33, 0.33]$. A sensor reports landmark match with likelihoods $p(z \mid x) = [0.1, 0.7, 0.2]$.

**Step 1 — Multiply prior and likelihood element-wise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[0.33 \times 0.1, \; 0.33 \times 0.7, \; 0.33 \times 0.2] &= [0.033, \; 0.231, \; 0.066]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the normalizer $\eta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{1}{0.033 + 0.231 + 0.066} = \frac{1}{0.330} \approx 3.030
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bel}(x) &= [0.100, \; 0.700, \; 0.200]
\end{aligned}
$$

The belief has correctly concentrated on cell 2 — the most likely pose given the measurement.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SLAM System — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Input Zt] --> B[Data Association]
    C[Control Input Ut] --> D[Motion Model]
    D --> E[Pose Prediction]
    E --> B
    B --> F[Measurement Model]
    F --> G[Bayes Filter Update]
    G --> H[Updated Belief Bel Xt]
    H --> I[Map Update Module]
    I --> J[Updated Map M]
    J --> F
    H --> K[Pose Estimate Output]
    J --> L[Map Output]
    K --> M[Robot Controller]
    L --> M
    M --> C
```

## 4.2 Localization Sub-System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Odometry Encoder] --> P1[Motion Model P Xt Xt-1 Ut]
    S2[LiDAR Camera] --> C1[Measurement Model P Zt Xt M]
    S3[IMU] --> P1
    P1 --> BF[Bayes Filter Core]
    C1 --> BF
    M1[Stored Map M] --> C1
    BF --> OUT[Pose Estimate Xt Yt Theta t]
    BF --> FEED[Feedback to Predict Step]
    FEED --> BF
```

## 4.3 Mapping Pipeline — Occupancy Grid Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Robot Pose Xt and Measurement Zt] --> INV[Inverse Sensor Model]
    INV --> LOG[Log Odds Update L mi]
    LOG --> ACC[Accumulate to Grid Cell mi]
    ACC --> THR[Threshold for Occupancy Decision]
    THR --> OUT2[Final Occupancy Grid Map]
    PRIOR[Uniform Prior L0 0] --> LOG
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Which Algorithm to Pick

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is a prior map available?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need global localization?}
    Q1 -- No --> SLAM[Use SLAM Algorithm EKF SLAM FastSLAM Graph SLAM]
    Q2 -- No --> EKF[Use EKF Localization]
    Q2 -- Yes --> MCL[Use Monte Carlo Localization]
    SLAM --> FEAT{Environment sparse or dense?}
    EKF --> OK[Output Pose]
    MCL --> OK
    FEAT -- Sparse --> FSLAM[FastSLAM with landmarks]
    FEAT -- Dense --> GS[Graph SLAM with pose graph]
    FSLAM --> OK
    GS --> OK
```

## 4.5 Localization Variants — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TYPE[Three Localization Variants]
        A1[Position Tracking]
        A2[Global Localization]
        A3[Kidnapped Robot Problem]
    end
    A1 --> M1[Small Gaussian belief]
    A2 --> M2[Multi-modal belief]
    A3 --> M3[Re-initialized belief]
    M1 --> OUTA[Pose Output]
    M2 --> OUTA
    M3 --> OUTA
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between localization, mapping, and SLAM. *(KTU University Exam — May 2024 style)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Localization | Mapping | SLAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Map + sensor data | Sensor data + robot pose | Only sensor data + controls |
| **Output** | Robot pose $(x, y, \theta)$ | Spatial map $m$ | Both pose and map |
| **Coupling** | Map assumed known | Pose assumed known | Coupled, joint estimation |
| **Difficulty** | Moderate | Moderate | Hardest (chicken-and-egg) |
| **Example** | GPS in a city | Floor plan from scans | Robot exploring Mars terrain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always draw a one-line relationship: *Localization = Sensor + Map → Pose*, *Mapping = Sensor + Pose → Map*, *SLAM = Sensor → (Pose, Map)*.

### Q2. What is the kidnapped robot problem? How does Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization (AMCL) address it? *(KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 style)*

**Model Answer:**
- The **kidnapped robot problem** is a sudden, discrete jump of the robot to a new location without warning. The robot's belief is suddenly **wrong** and must recover.
- **AMCL** addresses it by injecting **random particles** into the filter whenever the average measurement likelihood falls below a threshold — essentially saying, "my belief is bad, let me re-seed globally."
- It uses **KLD-sampling** to adapt the number of particles $M$ dynamically, increasing $M$ when the belief is uncertain.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks *(KTU University Exam — July 2024 style)*

#### (a) Derive the Bayes filter recursion for robot localization. Clearly state all assumptions used. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the goal** clearly:
We wish to estimate the posterior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ over the robot pose $x_t$ given all measurements $z_{1:t}$ and all control inputs $u_{1:t}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) &= \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Markov assumption 1 (measurement):**
Given $x_t$, the current measurement $z_t$ is conditionally independent of all past measurements and controls:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) &= p(z_t \mid x_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Law of total probability** over $x_{t-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) &= \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Markov assumption 2 (motion):** applied as above. Combine:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) &= \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The recursion alternates between **prediction** (the integral) and **correction** (multiplication by $p(z_t \mid x_t)$). This is the canonical formula for KTU's 7-mark derivation.

#### (b) Explain Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) with a clear algorithm. How does it handle the global localization problem? (7 Marks)

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Draw $M$ particles from a uniform distribution over the pose space.
2. **Prediction:** For each particle, sample a new pose using the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$.
3. **Weighting:** Assign each particle a weight $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$.
4. **Resampling:** Draw $M$ new particles with replacement, proportional to weights. This step is *crucial* — it eliminates the low-weight "waste" particles.
5. **Estimate:** The pose estimate is the weighted mean of the surviving particles.

**Why MCL handles global localization:**
Because the particles start as a **uniform cloud** spread across the entire environment, MCL maintains a **multi-modal belief** — it can track multiple plausible pose hypotheses simultaneously. As the robot moves and observes landmarks, all but one of the modes die out due to repeated resampling. This is impossible with EKF, which assumes a unimodal Gaussian.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always mention **particle depletion** as a drawback and how AMCL fixes it via random particle injection.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks *(KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 style)*

#### (a) What is an occupancy grid map? Derive the log-odds update rule for building such a map. (7 Marks)

**Definition:**
An **occupancy grid map** divides the environment into a 2D (or 3D) grid of cells, where each cell $m_i$ stores the probability $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ that the cell is **occupied**.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define the log-odds** for each cell $m_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_t(m_i) &= \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes rule** for the posterior $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) &= \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the log-odds ratio** for time $t$ vs $t-1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_t(m_i) &= l_{t-1}(m_i) + \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} - l_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude** with the additive update (fast, in-place):

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_t(m_i) &= l_{t-1}(m_i) + \Delta l(m_i, z_t, x_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta l = l(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - l_0$ is a constant lookup from the **inverse sensor model**.

#### (b) Compare feature-based maps, topological maps, and occupancy grid maps. When is each preferred? (7 Marks)

| Feature | Feature-Based Map | Topological Map | Occupancy Grid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | List of landmarks | Graph of nodes and edges | 2D / 3D grid of cells |
| **Storage** | $O(K)$, $K$ landmarks | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(N)$ cells |
| **Resolution** | Sparse, landmark-only | Place-based | Fine, cell-level |
| **Computation** | Fast matching | Efficient graph search | Heavy for large maps |
| **Preferred When** | Sparse outdoor env., GPS-poor | Hallways, multi-floor | Indoor dense env., obstacle avoidance |
| **Example** | Autonomous car on highway | Museum guide robot | Roomba-style vacuum |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse **topological** with **semantic** maps (semantic maps include object labels).
> - Do **NOT** say "feature maps are always better" — examiners want trade-offs.
> - When asked for a *derivation*, **every algebraic step** must be visible. Skipping the log-odds derivation is a guaranteed 3-mark loss.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam:**

- **Localization** estimates pose $(x, y, \theta)$; **Mapping** builds the environment; **SLAM** does both jointly.
- The **Bayes filter** has two stages: **Prediction** (motion model) and **Correction** (measurement model).
- The Bayes filter formula: $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$.
- **MCL** (Particle Filter) approximates the posterior with weighted samples; it can handle **multi-modal** beliefs and **global localization**.
- **AMCL** adds **random particle injection** to handle the **kidnapped robot** problem.
- **Occupancy grids** use the **log-odds** update: additive, fast, in-place.
- **Three localization variants:** Position Tracking (easy), Global Localization (medium), Kidnapped Robot (hard).
- **EKF** is fast but unimodal (Gaussian); **MCL** is slower but multi-modal; choose based on uncertainty.
- **Landmark observation** = $(r, \phi)$: range and bearing; **data association** matches observations to stored landmarks.
- **Odometry model** uses $(x, y, \theta)$ composition with $\cos$ and $\sin$ of the previous heading.
- **Loop closure** is what makes SLAM consistent over long trajectories — recognize a previously visited place.
- **KTU high-frequency keywords** to memorize verbatim: *Markov assumption, total probability, importance sampling, resampling, log-odds, inverse sensor model, KLD-sampling*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning in Robot Perception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning in Robot Perception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Perception** is the interdisciplinary field concerned with enabling a robotic system to acquire, process, interpret, and reason about sensory data originating from its physical environment, transforming raw sensor measurements (images, point clouds, sound waves, tactile readings) into structured, semantically meaningful representations that can be acted upon by the robot's planning and control subsystems.

**Machine Learning (ML) in Robot Perception** refers to the paradigm where robots are *not* explicitly programmed with hand-crafted rules for interpreting sensors, but instead *learn* the mapping from raw sensory inputs to useful perceptual outputs (e.g., object labels, depth maps, pose estimates) directly from large volumes of annotated or self-supervised data, using statistical learning algorithms such as **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)**, **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)**, and **Transformer architectures**.

The mathematical abstraction can be formally stated as learning a parameterized function:

$$f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$$

where $\mathcal{X}$ is the high-dimensional sensor input space (e.g., $\mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ for RGB images), $\mathcal{Y}$ is the perceptual output space (labels, bounding boxes, depth), and $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d$ are the learnable parameters of the model (weights and biases), optimized by minimizing a task-specific loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Robot as Infant" Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Infant Learning Analogy:** Imagine teaching a baby to recognize a "cup." You do not write the baby a rulebook: *"If the object has a cylindrical base, a concave opening, and a handle on the right side, then it is a cup."* Instead, you repeatedly show the baby cups and non-cups, and over time, the baby's brain (a biological neural network) statistically learns the visual features that distinguish cups. **Machine Learning in Robot Perception works in exactly the same way.** A robot is shown thousands of labeled images, and its artificial neural network (a mathematical model of the brain) gradually tunes its internal parameters to recognize objects, segments scenes, and estimate geometry—without a human ever writing a single "if-then" perception rule.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Robot Perception

| Pillar | Core Question Answered | Typical Sensor | Classical Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Vision (2D/3D)** | *"What is in front of me, and where is it?"* | RGB Camera, Stereo Camera | Bounding boxes, class labels, depth maps |
| **Localization & Mapping** | *"Where am I, and what does the world look like?"* | LiDAR, Camera, IMU, Wheel Odometry | Occupancy grids, pose trajectories |
| **Multi-Modal Fusion** | *"Can combining senses improve confidence?"* | Camera + LiDAR + Radar + Microphone | Robust scene graphs, semantic maps |

## 1.4 Why ML Became Dominant in Modern Perception

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Pre-ML Era Bottleneck:** Traditional computer vision relied on **hand-crafted features** such as SIFT, HOG, and SURF. These features are brittle—they fail under lighting variation, occlusion, scale changes, and intra-class diversity. **ML-based perception** replaced this fragile pipeline with **learned feature hierarchies**, where low-level edges and textures, mid-level parts, and high-level semantic concepts are all discovered automatically from data. This is the foundational reason ML dominates modern robot perception.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical ML-Based Perception Pipeline (Operational Breakdown)

The execution of ML-driven robot perception can be decomposed into a **five-stage logical pipeline**. Understanding these stages is critical for both KTU theory questions and design-oriented problems.

* **Stage 1 — Sensor Data Acquisition:** The robot captures raw multimodal data. For vision, this involves an imaging sensor producing a digital image $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$, where $H$ is height, $W$ is width, and $C$ is the number of channels (typically **3** for RGB, **1** for grayscale, or **4** for RGB-D). For LiDAR, the output is an unordered set of 3D points $\mathcal{P} = \{p_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
* **Stage 2 — Preprocessing & Normalization:** Raw data is normalized to a standard scale. Pixel intensities are rescaled to $[0, 1]$, point clouds are downsampled, and noise is filtered. This ensures the model receives inputs from a stable statistical distribution.
* **Stage 3 — Feature Extraction (via Deep Neural Networks):** A deep network (e.g., a ResNet-50 backbone) transforms the raw input into a **feature representation** $z = f_\theta(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$, a dense vector in a learned semantic space where similar concepts cluster together.
* **Stage 4 — Task-Specific Head:** The feature vector $z$ is fed into a specialized "head" that produces the final output—e.g., a softmax classifier for recognition, a regression head for bounding boxes, or a transposed convolution decoder for segmentation masks.
* **Stage 5 — Post-Processing & Decision:** Outputs are refined using geometric constraints (e.g., Non-Maximum Suppression for object detection, RANSAC for pose estimation) before being passed to the robot's motion planner.

## 2.2 Core ML Perception Tasks

| Task | Definition | Output Type | Key ML Model Family |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Image Classification** | Assign a single class label to an entire image. | Discrete label $y \in \{1, \dots, K\}$ | CNN (VGG, ResNet, EfficientNet) |
| **Object Detection** | Localize objects with bounding boxes AND classify them. | $\{(x, y, w, h, c, s)\}$ tuples | YOLO, Faster R-CNN, DETR |
| **Semantic Segmentation** | Assign a class label to *every pixel*. | $H \times W$ label map | U-Net, DeepLab, SegFormer |
| **Depth Estimation** | Predict per-pixel distance from camera. | $H \times W$ depth map | Monodepth, MiDaS, DPT |
| **Pose Estimation** | Estimate 6-DoF pose of an object/camera. | Rotation $R \in SO(3)$, Translation $t \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ | PoseCNN, PnP-Net |
| **SLAM** | Jointly estimate map and trajectory. | Trajectory $\mathcal{T}$, Map $\mathcal{M}$ | ORB-SLAM, NeRF-SLAM |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you must know for solving KTU ESE problems on this topic. **Memorize the equations, their purpose, and their standard units.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Purpose | Standard Notation | Units / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\hat{y} = \text{softmax}(Wz + b)$ | Multi-class classification output | $W$: weight matrix, $z$: features | Probabilities $\in [0, 1]$ |
| 2 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Cross-Entropy Loss (classification) | $y_i$: ground-truth one-hot, $\hat{y}_i$: prediction | Dimensionless, $\geq 0$ |
| 3 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -[y \log \hat{p} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})]$ | Binary Cross-Entropy (objectness) | $y \in \{0,1\}$, $\hat{p} \in (0,1)$ | Dimensionless, $\geq 0$ |
| 4 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error (regression) | $y_i$: truth, $\hat{y}_i$: prediction | Squared units of $y$ |
| 5 | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Intersection-over-Union (segmentation/detection) | $A$: prediction, $B$: ground truth | Scalar $\in [0, 1]$ |
| 6 | $\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{AP}_k$ | Mean Average Precision (detection metric) | $K$: number of classes | Scalar $\in [0, 1]$ |
| 7 | $f_\theta(x) = \sigma(W_L \ast \sigma(\cdots \sigma(W_1 \ast x + b_1)\cdots) + b_L)$ | Convolutional Neural Network forward pass | $\ast$: convolution, $\sigma$: ReLU | Feature maps |
| 8 | $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta^{(t)})$ | Gradient Descent Update Rule | $\eta$: learning rate | $\eta > 0$, typically $\sim 10^{-3}$ |
| 9 | $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} \approx \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_i(\theta)$ | Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) estimate | $m$: mini-batch size | Batch size $m \in [32, 512]$ |
| 10 | $y = \text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Rectified Linear Unit activation | $x$: pre-activation | $y \geq 0$ |
| 11 | $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | Softmax activation (multi-class) | $z_i$: logit for class $i$ | $\sum_i \sigma(z_i) = 1$ |
| 12 | $P_{\text{world}} = T_{\text{camera}}^{\text{world}} \cdot P_{\text{camera}}$ | Camera-to-World coordinate transform | $T \in SE(3)$ | 3D position $\in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Autonomous Vehicles:** Tesla FSD, Waymo, and Cruise stacks rely on CNN-based perception to detect pedestrians, vehicles, and lane markings in real time (latency $\leq \mathbf{50 \text{ ms}}$).
* **Industrial Robotics:** Bin-picking robots use 6-DoF pose estimation networks to grasp randomly oriented parts from cluttered bins.
* **Medical Robotics:** Da Vinci surgical systems use semantic segmentation to highlight anatomical structures in endoscopic video.
* **Service & Domestic Robots:** Vacuum robots (Roomba) use lightweight CNNs for room segmentation; humanoid robots (Tesla Optimus, Figure 01) use transformer-based perception for object manipulation.
* **Space Exploration:** NASA's Mars rovers (Perseverance) use ML-based autonomous navigation (AutoNav) to detect hazards and plan paths without Earth-based teleoperation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Cross-Entropy Loss for Multi-Class Classification

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Define the Modeling Problem
We are given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where each input $x^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and each label $y^{(i)} \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}$ denotes one of $K$ possible classes.

The model produces a probability distribution $\hat{p}(y = k \mid x) = \hat{y}_k$, with the constraint $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \hat{y}_k = 1$.

### Step 2 — Assume a Generative Model for the Data
We assume that the labels $y$ are generated by a **Categorical distribution** conditional on the input $x$, parameterized by the model's predicted probabilities $\hat{y}_k$. The probability of observing a single sample's label is:

$$P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \theta) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \hat{y}_k^{\mathbb{1}[y^{(i)} = k]}$$

*Reasoning:* Only the term corresponding to the true class contributes to the product, since $\mathbb{1}[\cdot] = 1$ only when the condition is true, and $\hat{y}_k^0 = 1$ for all other classes.

### Step 3 — Write the Likelihood of the Full Dataset
Assuming samples are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.), the total likelihood is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \prod_{k=1}^{K} \hat{y}_k^{(i)\;\mathbb{1}[y^{(i)} = k]}$$

### Step 4 — Take the Logarithm (Log-Likelihood)
Logarithms preserve monotonicity and convert products into sums, which is critical for gradient-based optimization:

$$\ell(\theta) = \log \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}[y^{(i)} = k] \log \hat{y}_k^{(i)}$$

### Step 5 — Convert Maximization to Minimization
Machine learning convention minimizes a *loss*. We negate the log-likelihood and average over $N$ to obtain a per-sample loss:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}[y^{(i)} = k] \log \hat{y}_k^{(i)}$$

### Step 6 — Express Using One-Hot Encoding
In practice, the ground-truth label $y^{(i)}$ is encoded as a one-hot vector $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}^{K}$ where exactly one entry is **1** and the rest are **0**. Thus $\mathbb{1}[y^{(i)} = k] = y_k^{(i)}$, and we get the canonical form:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k}$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Sanity Check
If the model perfectly predicts ($\hat{y}_k = 1$ for the correct class, $0$ for others), then $\log(1) = 0$, so the loss is **0** (best possible). If the model is maximally wrong ($\hat{y}_k = 0$ for the correct class), the loss approaches $+\infty$ (worst possible). This matches our intuition.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: The Gradient of MSE Loss

### Step 1 — Define the Loss
For a regression problem with prediction $\hat{y} = w^T x + b$ and ground truth $y$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$$

### Step 2 — Expand the Square
For a single sample, expand $(y - \hat{y})^2 = (y - w^T x - b)^2$.

### Step 3 — Compute Partial Derivative with Respect to $w$
Apply the chain rule $\frac{\partial}{\partial w}(y - w^Tx - b)^2 = 2(y - w^Tx - b) \cdot (-x)$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = -\frac{2}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)} (y^{(i)} - w^T x^{(i)} - b)$$

### Step 4 — Compute Partial Derivative with Respect to $b$
By similar application of the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\frac{2}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - w^T x^{(i)} - b)$$

### Step 5 — Set Gradients to Zero (Closed-Form Optimum)
For a *linear* model, setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0$ yields $\hat{y} = \bar{y}$ (predictions are mean-centered). Substituting into $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 0$ yields the **Normal Equation**:

$$\boxed{w^{\star} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$

This is the closed-form optimal weight vector, computable without iterative gradient descent.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Loss Functions for Perception Tasks

| Loss Function | Mathematical Form | Best Suited For | Why It Works | Pitfall in Robot Perception |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cross-Entropy** | $-\sum y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ | Classification, Segmentation | Strong gradient when prediction is wrong | Sensitive to class imbalance (e.g., rare obstacles) |
| **Mean Squared Error** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (y-\hat{y})^2$ | Depth regression, pose regression | Penalizes large errors quadratically | Assumes Gaussian noise; degrades for outlier sensor readings |
| **Huber Loss** | $\begin{cases} 0.5(y-\hat{y})^2 & \text{if } \vert y-\hat{y} \vert \leq \delta \\ \delta \cdot (\vert y-\hat{y} \vert - 0.5\delta) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Robust depth/pose estimation | Quadratic near zero, linear in tails—robust to outliers | Requires tuning the $\delta$ hyperparameter |
| **Focal Loss** | $-\alpha(1-\hat{y})^\gamma \log \hat{y}$ | Object detection (rare objects) | Down-weights easy examples, focuses on hard ones | Computationally heavier; two extra hyperparameters |
| **Dice Loss** | $1 - \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Semantic segmentation | Directly optimizes IoU; handles class imbalance | Gradient can be unstable for small regions |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"Which loss is appropriate for X task?"* in a 7-mark question, *always* justify your choice in terms of the task's output type, the data distribution, and the error sensitivity. A bare formula is worth only 2 marks; a justified choice is worth the full 7.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ML-Based Robot Perception Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *end-to-end* flow of data, transformations, and decision points in a modern ML-driven robot perception system. Note that node IDs are purely alphanumeric to ensure clean Mermaid compilation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Sensor Array] --> B[Raw Data Acquisition]
    B --> B1[RGB Camera Stream]
    B --> B2[LiDAR Point Cloud]
    B --> B3[Depth Map / RGB-D]
    B --> B4[IMU and Odometry]

    B1 --> C1[Preprocessing: Resize, Normalize, Denoise]
    B2 --> C2[Preprocessing: Voxel Downsampling, Ground Removal]
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C5[Preprocessing: Bias Correction, Integration]

    C1 --> D1[CNN Backbone ResNet-50]
    C2 --> D2[PointNet Voxel Net]
    C1 --> D3[Transformer Encoder ViT]
    C5 --> D5[Kalman Filter Pose Estimator]

    D1 --> E1[Task Specific Head 1: Object Detection YOLO]
    D1 --> E2[Task Specific Head 2: Semantic Segmentation U-Net]
    D2 --> E3[Task Specific Head 3: 3D Bounding Box Regression]
    D3 --> E4[Task Specific Head 4: Depth Estimation]
    D5 --> E6[Sensor Fusion Layer]

    E1 --> F1[Perception Output: Object List with Poses]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F2[Perception Output: Dense Scene Geometry]
    E6 --> F3[Perception Output: Robot State Estimate]

    F1 --> G[Scene Graph Construction]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G

    G --> H[Robot Decision Module: Planning and Control]

    H --> I[Robot Action: Move, Grasp, Speak]
    I --> J[New Sensor Data: Closed Loop Perception]
    J --> A
```

## 4.2 Architecture of a Convolutional Neural Network for Visual Perception

The following block diagram maps the canonical CNN architecture used for image-based perception. It traces the dimensional transformation of feature maps as data flows through convolutional, activation, and pooling layers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Image 224x224x3] --> A2[Conv Layer 1: 7x7 kernel, 64 filters, stride 2]
    A2 --> A3[ReLU Activation]
    A3 --> A4[Max Pooling 3x3 stride 2]
    A4 --> A5[Residual Block 1: 3 conv layers, 256 filters]
    A5 --> A6[Residual Block 2: 4 conv layers, 512 filters]
    A6 --> A7[Residual Block 3: 6 conv layers, 1024 filters]
    A7 --> A8[Residual Block 4: 3 conv layers, 2048 filters]
    A8 --> A9[Global Average Pooling]
    A9 --> A10[Fully Connected Layer 1000 units]
    A10 --> A11[Softmax Output: Class Probabilities]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the **spatial resolution decreases** (224 → 112 → 56 → 28 → 14 → 7 → 1) while the **channel depth increases** (3 → 64 → 256 → 512 → 1024 → 2048). This is the universal "hourglass" trade-off in CNN design: as spatial precision is lost, semantic richness is gained. Examiners love asking about this trade-off.

## 4.3 Training vs. Inference Loop in ML Perception

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Labeled Dataset D] --> P2[Sample Mini-Batch of size m]
    P2 --> P3[Forward Pass: Compute Predictions y_hat]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Loss L]
    P4 --> P5[Backward Pass: Compute Gradients]
    P5 --> P6[Optimizer SGD or Adam: Update Parameters theta]
    P6 --> P7{Has Convergence Been Reached?}
    P7 -- No --> P2
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Trained Model f_theta star]
    P8 --> P9[Deploy on Robot for Inference]
    P9 --> P10[Real-time Predictions on Unseen Data]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** The **training phase** involves labeled data, a loss function, and gradient-based optimization—this is computationally expensive and happens *offline* on GPUs. The **inference phase** involves feeding new, unseen sensor data through the trained model to obtain predictions—this is computationally lightweight and must run *in real time* on the robot's onboard hardware. Many exam questions test whether students can correctly distinguish these two phases.
