# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
Define *Robot Perception*. List any two sensors commonly used for perception in mobile robots and state the type of data each sensor provides.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Robot perception is the process by which a robotic system acquires, processes, and interprets sensory data from its environment to extract meaningful, structured information that can be used for decision-making, planning, and control.

Two commonly used sensors:
1. **RGB Camera** → Provides 2D color images (data type: $\mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ pixel intensity matrices).
2. **LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)** → Provides 3D point clouds (data type: unordered set of $(x, y, z)$ coordinates).

*(Alternate valid sensors: Depth camera, IMU, ultrasonic sensor, tactile sensor.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTu University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
What is the role of the **softmax function** in a multi-class image classification neural network?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The softmax function converts a vector of raw, unnormalized scores called **logits** $z = (z_1, z_2, \dots, z_K)$ into a probability distribution over $K$ classes. Its mathematical form is:

$$\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

Properties: every output is in $(0, 1)$, and the outputs sum to 1. It is used in the final layer of a classification network so that the output is interpretable as class probabilities, enabling the use of cross-entropy loss for training.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [Choice 1]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

(a) Explain the **architecture of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** used for visual perception in robots. Draw a labeled block diagram and describe the role of each component layer. *(7 Marks)*

(b) With a suitable example, derive the **Cross-Entropy loss function** for a multi-class classification task from first principles using the principle of Maximum Likelihood Estimation. *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a): Architecture of a CNN (7 Marks)**

A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is a specialized deep learning architecture designed to process grid-structured data such as images. For robot visual perception, the standard CNN pipeline consists of the following layers:

1. **Input Layer:** Accepts the raw RGB image tensor of shape $H \times W \times 3$ (e.g., $224 \times 224 \times 3$). Pixel values are normalized to $[0, 1]$ for stable training.

2. **Convolutional Layer:** Applies a set of learnable filters (kernels) that slide across the input image, computing dot products to produce **feature maps**. Each kernel acts as a detector for a specific low-level pattern (edges, corners, textures). Formally, the convolution operation is:

$$y_{i,j,k} = \sum_{u,v,c} W_{u,v,c,k} \cdot x_{i+u, j+v, c} + b_k$$

3. **Activation Layer (ReLU):** Introduces non-linearity by applying $y = \max(0, x)$ element-wise. Without non-linearity, the entire network would collapse into a single linear transformation.

4. **Pooling Layer (Max Pooling):** Down-samples feature maps to reduce spatial dimensions and computational cost, while retaining the most salient activations. Typical configuration: $2 \times 2$ max-pool with stride 2, halving resolution.

5. **Fully Connected Layer:** Flattens the final feature map and connects every neuron to every class score. This is the "reasoning" stage that combines all extracted features.

6. **Softmax Output Layer:** Converts logits to class probabilities, enabling the cross-entropy loss computation during training.

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram above for the canonical flow.)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b): Derivation of Cross-Entropy Loss (7 Marks)**

Refer to the **exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The student must reproduce all 7 steps, with the final boxed result:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$$

For full marks (7/7), the derivation must include:
* Assumption of categorical generative model (1 mark)
* Construction of likelihood function (1 mark)
* Log-likelihood derivation (1 mark)
* Negation and averaging to form loss (1 mark)
* One-hot encoding substitution (1 mark)
* Sanity check / interpretation of the result (1 mark)
* Final boxed equation (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Choice 2]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

(a) Discuss the differences between **semantic segmentation, object detection, and instance segmentation**. Illustrate with examples relevant to robot perception. *(7 Marks)*

(b) Explain the **Intersection-over-Union (IoU)** metric. Why is it the standard evaluation metric for object detection and segmentation in robot perception systems? *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a): Comparison of Three Tasks (7 Marks)**

| Feature | Object Detection | Semantic Segmentation | Instance Segmentation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Output Granularity** | Bounding boxes around objects | Class label for every pixel | Class + instance label for every pixel |
| **Distinguishes Instances?** | No (only "there is a cup") | No (only "these pixels are cup-class") | Yes ("cup #1 vs cup #2") |
| **Typical Model** | YOLO, Faster R-CNN | U-Net, DeepLab | Mask R-CNN |
| **Robot Perception Use Case** | Detect and localize obstacles in a warehouse | Identify drivable road surface for a self-driving car | Distinguish individual objects in a bin-picking task |
| **Output Tensor** | $\{(x, y, w, h, c, \text{score})\}$ list | $H \times W$ integer label map | $H \times W$ integer instance map |

**Example:** In an autonomous warehouse robot:
* **Object detection** tells the robot *"there are 3 boxes ahead, with bounding boxes at these coordinates."*
* **Semantic segmentation** tells the robot *"this entire region is 'floor', this is 'shelf', this is 'box-class'."*
* **Instance segmentation** tells the robot *"there are 3 distinct boxes; this pixel belongs to box #1, this to box #2, and this to box #3."*

For grasping, instance segmentation is critical because the robot must plan a unique grasp pose for each individual object.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b): Intersection-over-Union Metric (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** Intersection-over-Union (IoU), also called the **Jaccard Index**, is defined as the ratio of the area (or volume) of overlap between the predicted region $A$ and the ground-truth region $B$ to the area of their union:

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**
1. Compute the intersection area $\vert A \cap B \vert$: the pixels/voxels that belong to BOTH the prediction and the ground truth.
2. Compute the union area $\vert A \cup B \vert$: the pixels/voxels that belong to either.
3. Take the ratio, which lies in $[0, 1]$.

**Why IoU is the standard for robot perception:**
* **Scale Invariance:** It is independent of the absolute size of objects. A small distant object and a large nearby object are penalized proportionally, which matches human perception of segmentation quality.
* **Geometrically Meaningful:** It directly measures the *overlap* between predicted and true regions, which is exactly what matters for safe robot navigation and manipulation. A bounding box that is geometrically correct is useful; one that overlaps poorly is dangerous.
* **Threshold-Based Decision Making:** In practice, a prediction is considered "correct" if $\text{IoU} \geq 0.5$ (the **PASCAL VOC** threshold) or $\text{IoU} \geq 0.75$ (the stricter **COCO** threshold). This enables clean precision-recall trade-offs.
* **Foundation for mAP:** The mean Average Precision (mAP) metric used in object detection benchmarks is computed by integrating the precision-recall curve at multiple IoU thresholds, making IoU the bedrock of detection evaluation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing IoU with Dice Coefficient:** The Dice coefficient is $\frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$. While related, they are NOT equal. Students who substitute one for the other lose 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the Union:** A common mistake is to write $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ (using sum instead of union). This is mathematically wrong and will cost 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring edge cases:** If $A = B = \emptyset$ (empty), IoU is undefined. Students should mention this boundary condition for full credit.
> 4. **Mixing training loss with evaluation metric:** Cross-entropy is for *training*; IoU is for *evaluation*. Conflating them is a classic conceptual error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Preparation**

* **Core Definitions:** Robot Perception = sensor-to-semantics; ML in Perception = learning the mapping $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from data.
* **Pipeline Stages:** Acquisition → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction (Deep Network) → Task Head → Post-Processing → Decision.
* **Six Core Tasks:** Classification, Detection, Segmentation, Depth Estimation, Pose Estimation, SLAM — know the output type and example model for each.
* **Activation Functions:** ReLU for hidden layers, Softmax for multi-class output, Sigmoid for binary output.
* **Loss Functions:** Cross-Entropy for classification (derived from MLE under categorical assumption), MSE for regression, Huber for robust regression, Focal for imbalanced detection, Dice for segmentation.
* **Evaluation Metrics:** IoU for segmentation and detection quality; mAP for detection benchmarks (COCO, PASCAL VOC); Accuracy for classification; RMSE for depth regression.
* **Optimization:** SGD with $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$; Adam as adaptive variant; learning rate $\eta \sim 10^{-3}$ is typical.
* **CNN Architecture Hourglass:** Spatial resolution ↓, channel depth ↑ as you go deeper; global average pooling replaces fully connected layers in modern architectures.
* **Sensors:** Camera (RGB, RGB-D, stereo), LiDAR (3D point cloud), IMU (acceleration + angular velocity), wheel odometry (relative pose).
* **SLAM:** Joint estimation of robot trajectory $\mathcal{T}$ and environment map $\mathcal{M}$; ORB-SLAM is a classical example; NeRF-SLAM and deep SLAM are modern ML-based variants.
* **Key Trade-offs:** Speed vs. accuracy (lightweight MobileNet vs. heavy ResNet-152); real-time inference constraints ($\leq 50$ ms latency for autonomous driving); training data requirements (millions of labeled images).
* **Common Pitfalls to Avoid in Exams:** Mixing training and evaluation metrics; confusing IoU and Dice; forgetting to justify loss function choice; using $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) inside markdown table syntax (use `\vert` or `\mid`).
* **2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Expect at least one 7-mark question on either the CNN architecture diagram OR a derivation of the loss function. Practice drawing the Mermaid-style block diagram from memory.
* **Real-World Anchors to Mention:** Tesla FSD (autonomous driving), Mask R-CNN (instance segmentation for bin-picking), U-Net (medical robot perception), ORB-SLAM (mobile robot localization), BERT/ViT (transformer-based perception).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Domains, Case Study - Use of AI in Typical Pick and Place Task
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Domains, Case Study - Use of AI in Typical Pick and Place Task

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application Domains of AI in Robotics** refer to the broad spectrum of real-world sectors and operational scenarios where Artificial Intelligence algorithms—including Machine Learning (ML), Deep Learning (DL), Reinforcement Learning (RL), and Computer Vision (CV)—are integrated with robotic actuators, sensors, and embedded controllers to achieve autonomous perception, cognition, planning, and manipulation tasks.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus framework, these domains span **Industrial Automation, Service Robotics, Medical & Surgical Robotics, Autonomous Transportation, Precision Agriculture, Defense & Surveillance, Space Exploration, and Human–Robot Collaboration (HRC)**.

A **Pick-and-Place Task** is formally defined as a canonical robotic manipulation primitive in which a robot end-effector must (i) perceive an object in an unstructured environment, (ii) plan a stable grasp, (iii) execute a collision-free trajectory, and (iv) place the object at a target configuration. When augmented with AI, this task transitions from rigid, pre-programmed automation to **adaptive, vision-guided, learning-based manipulation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Kitchen Assistant**
> Imagine teaching a *new apprentice* in a busy restaurant kitchen. Initially, the apprentice needs explicit instructions: *"Pick the ripe tomato from the third shelf, rotate your wrist by 45°, and place it gently in the bowl."* Over time, by watching thousands of cooking videos, the apprentice *learns* which tomatoes are ripe, which grip avoids crushing, and which path avoids the hot stove. AI-enabled pick-and-place robots are the **robotic equivalent of this apprentice**—they learn from data (videos, simulations, trial-and-error) rather than from rigid scripts.

The key shift in AI-based pick-and-place is the movement from:
- **Hard-coded rules** → **Learned policies** (via Neural Networks, RL)
- **Fixed environments** → **Unstructured, dynamic scenes** (via Computer Vision)
- **Open-loop execution** → **Closed-loop visual servoing** (via real-time feedback)

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants (Bolded for KTU Board Examinations)

- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** typically **6 DoF** for a serial manipulator in 3D space.
- **Repeatability (RP):** industry standard metric, typically **±0.02 mm** for precision arms.
- **Payload (P):** mass capacity at end-effector, range **0.5 kg – 1500 kg** for industrial arms.
- **Cycle Time (T_c):** time for one complete pick-and-place cycle, benchmarked at **0.3 s** for high-speed SCARA arms.
- **Inference Latency (t_inf):** AI model response time, ideally **< 30 ms** for real-time control at 30 FPS.
- **Intersection over Union (IoU):** object detection accuracy metric, threshold **≥ 0.5** for valid detection.
- **Discount Factor (γ):** RL parameter, range **[0, 1)**, typically **0.99** for long-horizon tasks.
- **Learning Rate (α):** RL/optimization parameter, range **[10⁻⁴, 10⁻¹]**, typically **10⁻³**.

### 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Focus (Module 4, KTU 2024):**
> Students must be able to *(i)* identify and explain major application domains of AI-driven robotics, *(ii)* describe the perception–planning–action loop, and *(iii)* analyze a typical pick-and-place case study integrating **CNNs, RL, classical control, and sensor fusion**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Application Domains of AI in Robotics

The integration of AI into robotics has produced transformative impact across multiple sectors. Below is the structured analytical breakdown as per KTU expectations.

#### 2.1.1 Industrial Robotics & Smart Manufacturing
- **Why:** Mass customization, high-mix-low-volume production, and Industry 4.0 / 5.0 paradigms demand flexible automation.
- **How:** AI enables **bin-picking, defect inspection (visual quality control), predictive maintenance, and human–robot collaborative assembly** (e.g., KUKA LBR iiwa, FANUC CRX cobots).
- **Key AI Techniques:** CNN-based defect classifiers, RL-based force control, digital twins.

#### 2.1.2 Service and Domestic Robotics
- **Why:** Aging populations and labor shortages drive demand for assistive robots.
- **How:** Autonomous navigation (SLAM), object retrieval, person-following, and dialogue systems.
- **Examples:** SoftBank Pepper, Amazon Astro, iRobot Roomba (with AI-driven room mapping).

#### 2.1.3 Medical and Surgical Robotics
- **Why:** Sub-millimeter precision, tremor filtering, and minimally invasive procedures.
- **How:** AI assists in **surgical suturing, tumor localization (via medical imaging), and rehabilitation exoskeletons**.
- **Examples:** da Vinci Surgical System, Mako robotic-arm assisted surgery.

#### 2.1.4 Autonomous Vehicles and Drones
- **Why:** Safety-critical navigation in dynamic, partially observable environments.
- **How:** Sensor fusion (LiDAR + Camera + Radar), end-to-end learning (NVIDIA PilotNet), behavior cloning.
- **Examples:** Waymo, Tesla Autopilot, Skydio drones.

#### 2.1.5 Precision Agriculture
- **Why:** Labor scarcity, yield optimization, and selective harvesting.
- **How:** Vision-based ripeness detection (YOLO, U-Net), weed–crop classification, autonomous tractors.
- **Examples:** John Deere See & Spray, Agrobot strawberry harvester.

#### 2.1.6 Defense, Space, and Underwater Robotics
- **Why:** Hazardous, remote, or hostile environments.
- **How:** Multi-agent RL, terrain traversability analysis, autonomous sample collection.
- **Examples:** Boston Dynamics Spot (military), NASA Perseverance rover, AUVs for ocean mapping.

### 2.2 Anatomy of an AI-Based Pick-and-Place Task

A typical AI-driven pick-and-place system operates as a **closed-loop pipeline** consisting of five core stages:

1. **Perception (Sense):** RGB-D cameras, LiDAR, tactile sensors acquire raw data.
2. **Object Detection & Pose Estimation (Interpret):** Deep learning models (YOLOv8, PoseCNN) localize the object and estimate its 6-DoF pose.
3. **Grasp Planning (Decide):** GraspNet / Dex-Net propose antipodal grasp candidates; quality is scored via wrench-space analysis.
4. **Motion Planning (Act):** RRT*, PRM, or learned policies generate collision-free trajectories.
5. **Control & Feedback (Refine):** Visual servoing (IBVS, PBVS) and impedance control close the loop at **1 kHz** while vision runs at **30 Hz**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Equation / Formula** | **Variables & Meaning** | **Typical Units / Values** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Kinematics (DH) | $T_{i}^{i-1} = R_z(\theta_i) \cdot T_z(d_i) \cdot T_x(a_i) \cdot R_x(\alpha_i)$ | $\theta_i$: joint angle; $d_i$: link offset; $a_i$: link length; $\alpha_i$: link twist | Angles in **rad**, lengths in **mm** |
| Intersection over Union (IoU) | $IoU = \dfrac{A_{pred} \cap A_{gt}}{A_{pred} \cup A_{gt}}$ | $A_{pred}$: predicted box; $A_{gt}$: ground-truth box | Dimensionless, $\in [0,1]$; threshold **0.5** |
| Cross-Entropy Loss (Classification) | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | $y_c$: true label; $\hat{y}_c$: predicted probability | NLL score, lower is better |
| Grasp Quality (Ferrari–Canny) | $Q = w_1 C + w_2 V + w_3 R + w_4 S$ | C: closure; V: visibility; R: robustness; S: safety; $w_i$: weights | Dimensionless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| Q-Learning Update Rule | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]$ | $Q(s,a)$: action-value; $\alpha$: learning rate; $\gamma$: discount factor; $r$: reward | $\alpha \in [0,1]$; $\gamma \in [0,1)$ |
| Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$ | $r_{t+k+1}$: future reward; $\gamma$: discount factor | Scalar value |
| Bellman Optimality | $Q^{*}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s',a') \mid s,a \right]$ | Defines optimal action-value | Scalar |
| Image-Based Visual Servoing (IBVS) | $\dot{q} = -\lambda \hat{L}^{+} (s - s^{*})$ | $\hat{L}^{+}$: pseudo-inverse interaction matrix; $s, s^{*}$: features | Velocity in **rad/s** |
| Proportional Control | $u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$ | $K_p$: gain; $e(t)$: error | Output signal |
| PID Control | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$: gains | Output signal |
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $y_i, \hat{y}_i$: true and predicted values | Squared error units |
| Softmax Activation | $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | $z_i$: logits; $K$: number of classes | Probability $\in [0,1]$ |
| Convolution Operation | $(f * g)[i,j] = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} f[m,n] \cdot g[i-m, j-n]$ | $f$: input; $g$: kernel | Feature map |
| ReLU Activation | $\phi(x) = \max(0, x)$ | $x$: input | Non-negative scalar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The IoU metric is the **gold standard** for evaluating pick-and-place object detection models (YOLOv8, Faster R-CNN). The Q-learning update is the **foundational learning law** that enables a robot to improve its pick-and-place policy through trial-and-error in simulation (e.g., NVIDIA Isaac Sim, MuJoCo). Grasp quality Q is critical in **Dex-Net** and **GraspNet** for ranking thousands of candidate grasps in real time.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Tesla Optimus** and **Figure 01** humanoid robots use vision-transformer-based pick-and-place pipelines.
- **Amazon Sparrow** uses deep learning for warehouse pick-and-place of unstructured items.
- **Fanuc iRVision** combines classical image processing with neural networks for bin-picking in automotive plants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Depth Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is explicitly shown. No steps are skipped.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Q-Learning Update Rule from the Bellman Equation

The Q-learning update is derived from the **Bellman Optimality Equation**, which expresses the optimal action-value recursively.

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of the optimal action-value function. The optimal Q-function satisfies:

$$
Q^{*}(s,a) = \mathbb{E} \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s_{t+1}, a') \;\Big|\; s_t = s, a_t = a \right]
$$

**Step 2:** Take the expectation. Because the immediate reward $r_{t+1}$ is observed and the future state $s_{t+1}$ is sampled from the transition dynamics $P(s_{t+1} \mid s, a)$:

$$
Q^{*}(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} P(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Convert from exact expectation to a **stochastic sample-based estimate**. In a robotic pick-and-place task, we observe a single transition $(s, a, r, s')$ per trial:

$$
Q^{*}(s,a) \approx r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the current estimate $Q(s,a)$ contains a **Temporal Difference (TD) error**:

$$
\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)
$$

**Step 5:** Apply a learning rate $\alpha$ to blend the new information with the old estimate (incremental mean update):

$$
Q_{new}(s,a) = Q_{old}(s,a) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t
$$

**Step 6:** Expand $\delta_t$ to obtain the **canonical Q-learning update**:

$$
Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a) \right]
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:** Step 1 states that the optimal value equals the immediate reward plus the discounted best future value. Step 2 expands the expectation over all possible next states and rewards. Step 3 is a *Monte Carlo* approximation since computing the full expectation is intractable in real robotics. Step 4 isolates the *error signal* (TD error) that drives learning. Step 5 uses a convex combination with weight $\alpha$ to ensure convergence. Step 6 is the final iterative update used inside any Deep Q-Network (DQN) training loop for a pick-and-place policy.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Intersection over Union (IoU) for Object Detection

**Step 1:** Define the predicted bounding box $B_p$ and ground-truth box $B_g$ as axis-aligned rectangles:

$$
B_p = (x_p^{(1)}, y_p^{(1)}, x_p^{(2)}, y_p^{(2)})
$$

$$
B_g = (x_g^{(1)}, y_g^{(1)}, x_g^{(2)}, y_g^{(2)})
$$

where superscript $(1)$ and $(2)$ denote the top-left and bottom-right corners respectively.

**Step 2:** Compute the **coordinates of the intersection rectangle** by taking the maximum of the top-left corners and the minimum of the bottom-right corners:

$$
x_{inter}^{(1)} = \max(x_p^{(1)}, x_g^{(1)}), \quad y_{inter}^{(1)} = \max(y_p^{(1)}, y_g^{(1)})
$$

$$
x_{inter}^{(2)} = \min(x_p^{(2)}, x_g^{(2)}), \quad y_{inter}^{(2)} = \min(y_p^{(2)}, y_g^{(2)})
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **intersection area** $A_{inter}$. If the width or height is negative, the boxes do not overlap and $A_{inter} = 0$:

$$
w_{inter} = \max(0,\; x_{inter}^{(2)} - x_{inter}^{(1)})
$$

$$
h_{inter} = \max(0,\; y_{inter}^{(2)} - y_{inter}^{(1)})
$$

$$
A_{inter} = w_{inter} \cdot h_{inter}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the area of each box independently:

$$
A_p = (x_p^{(2)} - x_p^{(1)}) \cdot (y_p^{(2)} - y_p^{(1)})
$$

$$
A_g = (x_g^{(2)} - x_g^{(1)}) \cdot (y_g^{(2)} - y_g^{(1)})
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the **union area** using the inclusion-exclusion principle:

$$
A_{union} = A_p + A_g - A_{inter}
$$

**Step 6:** Form the **IoU ratio**:

$$
IoU = \dfrac{A_{inter}}{A_{union}}
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:** Step 1 formalizes bounding boxes. Step 2 is the geometric rule for overlap. Step 3 handles the degenerate case. Step 4 measures each box individually. Step 5 avoids double-counting the intersection. Step 6 yields a normalized score in $[0,1]$. A threshold of **0.5** is the standard KTU-recommended value for declaring a *true positive* in pick-and-place object localization.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Grasp Quality Metric (Ferrari–Canny Wrench Closure)

**Step 1:** Define the contact point $c_i$ between a robotic finger and the object. The contact produces a **friction cone** of admissible contact forces.

**Step 2:** The set of forces and torques that the grasp can resist is the **grasp wrench space (GWS)**. Each contact $i$ contributes wrenches in the form:

$$
w_i = [f_i; \; c_i \times f_i], \quad \text{where } f_i \in FC_i
$$

where $FC_i$ is the friction cone of contact $i$ and $c_i$ is the contact point position.

**Step 3:** The **convex hull** of all $w_i$ defines the GWS. A grasp is *force-closure* if the origin lies strictly inside this convex hull.

**Step 4:** Ferrari and Canny defined the **grasp quality** as the radius of the largest ball centered at the origin that fits inside GWS:

$$
Q = \min_{w \in \partial GWS} \Vert w \Vert_2
$$

where $\partial GWS$ is the boundary of the grasp wrench space.

**Step 5:** Decompose into a weighted sum of normalized sub-metrics (engineering approximation used in modern grasp networks):

$$
Q = w_1 C + w_2 V + w_3 R + w_4 S
$$

where $C$ = closure measure, $V$ = visibility (no joint limits), $R$ = robustness to perturbations, $S$ = safety margin, and $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$.

**Conversion Logic Explanation:** Step 1 grounds the analysis in physics. Step 2 combines force and moment (wrench = 6D vector). Step 3 is the formal force-closure condition. Step 4 quantifies the margin against external disturbances. Step 5 gives a tractable, learnable metric for neural networks.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Convolution Operation in a CNN Feature Extractor

**Step 1:** Input image is represented as a 2D discrete function $f[m, n]$.

**Step 2:** A learnable kernel (filter) is $g[m, n]$ of size $k \times k$.

**Step 3:** The convolution is defined as the discrete cross-correlation (in deep learning convention, the kernel is not flipped):

$$
(f * g)[i, j] = \sum_{m=0}^{k-1} \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} f[i+m, j+n] \cdot g[m, n]
$$

**Step 4:** Add bias and apply non-linearity:

$$
h[i,j] = \phi \left( (f * g)[i,j] + b \right)
$$

where $\phi$ is typically ReLU.

**Step 5:** Stride $s$ and padding $p$ adjust the output spatial dimension:

$$
H_{out} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{H_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:** Step 1–2 set up the inputs. Step 3 is the local feature extraction. Step 4 adds the bias term and activation. Step 5 governs dimensional transitions between layers in a CNN such as ResNet-50 backbone of a YOLO detector.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use Mermaid syntax with alphanumeric node IDs (letter prefix) and plain uppercase labels (no markdown inside quotes). These are **block-level functional architecture flows** depicting AI-robot interaction pipelines.

### 4.1 Master Pipeline: AI-Based Pick-and-Place Task

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[RGB D CAMERA INPUT] --> A2[OBJECT DETECTION YOLO V8]
    A2 --> A3[6D POSE ESTIMATION POSECNN]
    A3 --> A4[GRASP CANDIDATE GENERATION GRASPNET]
    A4 --> A5[GRASP QUALITY SCORING FERRARI CANNY]
    A5 --> A6[MOTION PLANNER RRT STAR]
    A6 --> A7[INVERSE KINEMATICS SOLVER]
    A7 --> A8[ROBOT ARM EXECUTION 6DOF]
    A8 --> A9[VISUAL SERVOING FEEDBACK IBVS]
    A9 --> A10[REWARD SIGNAL COMPUTATION]
    A10 --> A11[RL POLICY UPDATE DQN]
    A11 --> A4
    A9 --> A2
```

**Interpretation of Diagram 4.1:** The flowchart shows a closed-loop perception–planning–action pipeline. The RGB-D camera (A1) feeds an object detector (A2), which outputs class labels and 2D bounding boxes. Pose estimation (A3) converts these to 6D poses. Grasp candidates (A4) are generated and ranked (A5). The motion planner (A6) computes a collision-free path. Inverse kinematics (A7) convert it to joint trajectories. The robot (A8) executes; visual servoing (A9) compares the current and desired image features. A reward signal (A10) drives RL policy updates (A11) that refine the grasp network (A4) over time. The dashed line from A9 back to A2 indicates continuous re-perception.

### 4.2 Application Domain Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    B0[AI IN ROBOTICS MASTER DOMAIN]
    B0 --> B1[INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING]
    B0 --> B2[SERVICE AND DOMESTIC]
    B0 --> B3[MEDICAL AND SURGICAL]
    B0 --> B4[AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES AND DRONES]
    B0 --> B5[AGRICULTURE AND HARVESTING]
    B0 --> B6[DEFENSE AND SPACE]
    B0 --> B7[HUMAN ROBOT COLLABORATION]
    B1 --> B1A[BIN PICKING]
    B1 --> B1B[DEFECT INSPECTION]
    B1 --> B1C[COBOT ASSEMBLY]
    B2 --> B2A[CLEANING ROBOTS]
    B2 --> B2B[ASSISTIVE CARE]
    B3 --> B3A[SURGICAL SUTURING]
    B3 --> B3B[REHABILITATION]
    B4 --> B4A[SELF DRIVING CARS]
    B4 --> B4B[AERIAL DRONES]
    B5 --> B5A[SELECTIVE HARVESTING]
    B5 --> B5B[WEED DETECTION]
    B6 --> B6A[ROVER EXPLORATION]
    B6 --> B6B[SWARM DRONES]
    B7 --> B7A[COLLABORATIVE ASSEMBLY]
    B7 --> B7B[SAFE HRI]
```

**Interpretation of Diagram 4.2:** This taxonomy breaks the master domain (B0) into seven principal sectors, each with two sub-applications. Industrial (B1) emphasizes pick-and-place, inspection, and human–robot collaboration. Service (B2) covers household and elderly care. Medical (B3) includes precision surgery and rehabilitation exoskeletons. Autonomous systems (B4) include ground and aerial vehicles. Agriculture (B5) focuses on selective harvesting and crop monitoring. Defense/Space (B6) covers exploration rovers and drone swarms. Human–Robot Collaboration (B7) addresses safety and shared workspaces.

### 4.3 Closed-Loop RL Architecture for Pick-and-Place

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[ENVIRONMENT ROBOT PLUS OBJECTS] --> C2[STATE OBSERVATION RGB D IMAGE]
    C2 --> C3[DEEP Q NETWORK DQN]
    C3 --> C4[ACTION SELECTION EPSILON GREEDY]
    C4 --> C5[ROBOT JOINT COMMANDS]
    C5 --> C1
    C1 --> C6[REWARD FUNCTION SHAPED]
    C6 --> C7[REPLAY BUFFER]
    C7 --> C8[BATCH SAMPLING]
    C8 --> C9[TARGET NETWORK Q DOUBLE]
    C9 --> C3
```

**Interpretation of Diagram 4.3:** The agent (robot) interacts with the environment (C1) by observing states (C2) and querying the Deep Q-Network (C3). Actions are chosen via $\varepsilon$-greedy exploration (C4) and executed as joint commands (C5). The reward function (C6) shapes learning (e.g., +1 for successful grasp, −0.1 per timestep). Transitions are stored in a replay buffer (C7) and sampled in mini-batches (C8) to update a separate target network (C9), which stabilizes training.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define the term "Application Domain" in the context of AI-enabled robotics. List any four major application domains with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An *application domain* of AI in robotics is a defined real-world sector where intelligent algorithms are integrated with physical robotic systems to perform autonomous tasks, replacing or augmenting human labor. The four major domains with examples are:

1. **Industrial Manufacturing** — AI-based bin-picking robots (e.g., FANUC iRVision).
2. **Medical / Surgical Robotics** — AI-assisted tumor localization and da Vinci surgical suturing.
3. **Autonomous Vehicles** — Self-driving cars using sensor fusion (e.g., Waymo).
4. **Precision Agriculture** — Vision-based selective harvesting robots (e.g., Agrobot).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for four domains with examples. Distribution: 0.5 per correct domain.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

*Briefly explain the role of "Computer Vision" and "Reinforcement Learning" in a typical AI-based pick-and-place task.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Computer Vision (1.5 marks):** Enables the robot to *perceive* its workspace. RGB-D cameras capture images; CNN-based models (YOLO, PoseCNN) detect objects, classify them, and estimate 6D poses, providing spatial coordinates for grasp planning.

- **Reinforcement Learning (1.5 marks):** Enables the robot to *learn* an optimal pick-and-place policy through trial-and-error. Using Q-learning or Deep Q-Networks, the agent receives rewards (e.g., +1 for successful grasp, −1 for drop) and refines its action-value function to maximize cumulative reward.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Extended-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the major application domains of AI in robotics, highlighting at least five domains with the AI techniques used and one industrial example per domain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the complete pipeline of an AI-based pick-and-place task. Explain the role of each module. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Application Domains (7 marks):**

1. **Industrial Robotics & Smart Manufacturing (1.5 marks):**
   - *AI Techniques:* CNN-based defect detection, RL-based force/impedance control, digital twin simulation.
   - *Example:* FANUC iRVision uses deep learning for high-speed bin-picking in automotive assembly.

2. **Medical & Surgical Robotics (1.5 marks):**
   - *AI Techniques:* U-Net for tumor segmentation in MRI/CT, RL for suture planning, haptic feedback.
   - *Example:* da Vinci Surgical System uses AI for instrument tracking and tremor cancellation.

3. **Autonomous Vehicles & Drones (1.5 marks):**
   - *AI Techniques:* Sensor fusion (LiDAR + Camera), end-to-end CNNs (PilotNet), behavior cloning.
   - *Example:* Waymo's self-driving fleet uses learned policies for urban navigation.

4. **Service & Domestic Robotics (1.0 mark):**
   - *AI Techniques:* Visual SLAM, NLP for voice assistants, RL for navigation.
   - *Example:* Amazon Astro uses computer vision for home monitoring.

5. **Agriculture (1.0 mark):**
   - *AI Techniques:* YOLO for fruit detection, semantic segmentation for weed identification.
   - *Example:* John Deere See & Spray selectively applies herbicide via AI vision.

6. **Defense & Space (0.5 mark for brief mention):**
   - *Example:* NASA Perseverance uses AI-based terrain classification for autonomous navigation on Mars.

---

**(b) AI-Based Pick-and-Place Pipeline (7 marks):**

The pipeline consists of six functional blocks (refer to Diagram 4.1):

1. **Perception Module (1.5 marks):** RGB-D camera and (optionally) tactile sensors acquire environmental data. Output: color image + depth map.

2. **Object Detection & Pose Estimation (1.5 marks):** YOLOv8 detects the object's bounding box; PoseCNN estimates the 6D pose (3D position + 3D orientation). Output: object class, $(x,y,z,\text{roll},\text{pitch},\text{yaw})$.

3. **Grasp Planning (1.5 marks):** GraspNet (or Dex-Net) generates thousands of antipodal grasp candidates. Each is scored using the Ferrari–Canny quality metric Q. The top-ranked grasp is selected.

4. **Motion Planning (1.0 mark):** RRT* or PRM computes a collision-free trajectory from the current end-effector pose to the pre-grasp pose. Sampling-based methods are preferred for high-dimensional configuration spaces.

5. **Inverse Kinematics & Control (1.0 mark):** IK solver (analytical or numerical) converts the Cartesian path into joint trajectories. A PID or impedance controller drives the actuators at 1 kHz.

6. **Visual Servoing & Feedback (0.5 mark):** IBVS adjusts the end-effector in real time by minimizing the error between current and desired image features, compensating for pose-estimation errors and object motion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain how Deep Learning (CNN) and Reinforcement Learning (DQN) are integrated to enable a pick-and-place robot to learn from scratch in a simulated environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the key challenges of deploying AI in real-world pick-and-place operations and the emerging future trends. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Deep Learning + RL Integration (7 marks):**

1. **CNN as Visual Encoder (2 marks):** The CNN (e.g., ResNet-50) processes RGB images from the simulated environment and extracts a low-dimensional feature vector. This vector serves as the state representation $s_t$ for the RL agent. Transfer learning from ImageNet pre-trained weights accelerates convergence.

2. **DQN as Decision-Maker (2 marks):** The Deep Q-Network takes the CNN feature vector as input and outputs Q-values for each discrete action (e.g., move left, move right, open gripper, close gripper). The action $a_t$ is selected via $\varepsilon$-greedy:

$$
a_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_a Q(s_t, a) & \text{with probability } 1-\varepsilon \\ \text{random action} & \text{with probability } \varepsilon \end{cases}
$$

3. **Reward Shaping (1.5 marks):** Sparse rewards are augmented with shaped rewards:
   - +1.0 for successful grasp and place
   - +0.1 for decreasing distance to object
   - −0.01 per timestep (to encourage efficiency)
   - −1.0 for dropping the object

4. **Training Loop (1.5 marks):** Transitions $(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})$ are stored in a replay buffer. Mini-batches are sampled to compute the Q-learning target using a separate target network (for stability). Gradients are back-propagated through both the DQN and the CNN. After ~1 M episodes in simulation, the policy is transferred to the real robot (sim-to-real transfer via domain randomization).

---

**(b) Challenges and Future Trends (7 marks):**

**Challenges:**

1. **Data Scarcity and Annotation Cost (1.5 marks):** Real-world labeled manipulation data is expensive. *Mitigation:* Sim-to-real transfer, synthetic data via Isaac Sim, self-supervised learning.

2. **Sim-to-Real Gap (1.5 marks):** Policies trained in simulation often fail in reality due to differences in physics, lighting, and friction. *Mitigation:* Domain randomization, domain adaptation, real-world fine-tuning.

3. **Real-Time Latency (1.0 mark):** AI inference must complete within the control loop deadline (often <10 ms). *Mitigation:* Model pruning, quantization (INT8), edge AI accelerators (NVIDIA Jetson, Intel Movidius).

4. **Safety and Robustness (1.0 mark):** Failures in AI predictions can cause collisions or damage. *Mitigation:* Collision checking, force limiting, safety-rated monitored stop (ISO/TS 15066).

5. **Generalization to Novel Objects (1.0 mark):** Most models struggle with unseen object geometries. *Mitigation:* Foundation models for robotics (RT-2, PaLM-E), large-scale pretraining.

6. **Ethical and Workforce Concerns (0.5 mark):** Job displacement and accountability in autonomous decisions. *Mitigation:* Regulatory frameworks, human-in-the-loop oversight.

**Future Trends (0.5 mark for brief overview):**
- Vision-Language-Action (VLA) foundation models (e.g., Google RT-2, Tesla Optimus).
- Diffusion-based grasp planning.
- Self-supervised representation learning for tactile and proprioceptive signals.
- Multi-agent robotic coordination in warehouses.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Vague Definitions (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing *"AI is used in robots"* instead of *"AI algorithms (CNN, RL) integrated with sensors and actuators for autonomous perception, planning, and control."* Examiners award marks for *technical precision*.
>
> 2. **Skipping the Diagram (–2 marks):** In pick-and-place questions, a *neatly labeled block diagram* is **MANDATORY** for full marks. Omitting it or using vague boxes like "perception → planning → action" without naming CNN, IK, PID, etc., leads to heavy deduction.
>
> 3. **Confusing Supervised and Reinforcement Learning (–1 mark):** Writing *"we train CNN with rewards"* is a *fatal conceptual error*. Use: *"CNN is trained via supervised learning on labeled images; DQN is trained via RL on trial-and-error interaction."*
>
> 4. **Forgetting Real-Time Constraints (–1 mark):** AI in robotics is *latency-critical*. Always mention inference time and control-loop frequency.
>
> 5. **Not Linking Theory to Example (–1 mark):** A domain without an industrial example is incomplete. Use FANUC, da Vinci, Waymo, etc.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the Sim-to-Real Gap:** When discussing RL-based learning, you MUST mention that training occurs in simulation and that domain randomization is used for transfer to real hardware.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Application Domains (8 sectors):** Industrial, Service, Medical, Autonomous Vehicles, Agriculture, Defense/Space, HRC, Underwater.
- **Pick-and-Place = Perception + Cognition + Planning + Control + Feedback** (5-stage closed loop).
- **Key AI Techniques:** CNN (perception), PoseCNN (6D pose), GraspNet/Dex-Net (grasp), DQN/PPO (control policy), PID (low-level motor control), IBVS (visual servoing).
- **Must-Know Formulas (memorize):**
  - IoU = $A_{inter} / A_{union}$ (threshold 0.5).
  - Q-update = $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
  - Grasp quality = $w_1 C + w_2 V + w_3 R + w_4 S$.
  - Bellman optimality = $Q^* = r + \gamma \max Q^*$.
  - IBVS velocity = $-\lambda \hat{L}^+ (s - s^*)$.
  - IoU, CE loss, MSE, Softmax, ReLU, Convolution.
- **Standard Metrics to Quote:** 6 DoF, ±0.02 mm repeatability, <30 ms inference, IoU ≥ 0.5, γ ≈ 0.99, α ≈ 10⁻³.
- **Industrial Examples (must memorize):** FANUC iRVision, da Vinci, Waymo, John Deere See & Spray, Amazon Sparrow, Boston Dynamics Spot, Tesla Optimus, NASA Perseverance.
- **Real-Time Constraint:** Vision runs at 30 Hz, control at 1 kHz — *always mention this*.
- **Sim-to-Real Transfer:** Training happens in Isaac Sim / MuJoCo; domain randomization bridges the gap.
- **Safety Standard:** ISO/TS 15066 (cobot safety), ISO 10218 (industrial robots).
- **Examiner's Mantra:** *Define → Diagram → Example → Formula → Real-time constraint.*
- **Avoid These Words:** "Smart," "advanced," "modern" — use *specific* terms: "CNN-based," "RL-trained," "6-DoF."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization of a Differential Drive Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT755 - AI & MACHINE LEARNING FOR ROBOTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Localization of a Differential Drive Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Localization** of a differential drive robot is the process of estimating the robot's pose (position and orientation) within a known environment using on-board sensors, proprioceptive measurements (such as wheel encoder odometry), and exteroceptive measurements (such as LIDAR, sonar, cameras, or GPS). For a planar differential drive robot operating on a 2D surface, the pose is fully described by the state vector:

$$
\mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $(x_t, y_t)$ denotes the robot's Cartesian position in the global (world) reference frame and $\theta_t$ denotes its heading angle with respect to the global x-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Localization is treated as a **state estimation problem** in probabilistic robotics. The robot's pose is treated as a probability distribution rather than a deterministic value because of inherent sensor noise, actuator uncertainty, and environmental disturbances.

The differential drive robot is a non-holonomic mobile base with two independently driven wheels mounted on a common axis. The control inputs are typically the angular velocities of the left and right wheels $(v_L, v_R)$, or equivalently, the linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ of the robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Robot Localization**
> Robot localization answers the question: *"Where am I?"* It is the computational procedure that fuses motion data (dead reckoning / odometry) and perception data (landmark, range, or GPS measurements) to produce a statistically optimal estimate of the robot's pose and an associated uncertainty measure.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you wake up blindfolded in an unfamiliar building. You have two sources of information:
- **Internal sensing (proprioception):** You can count your footsteps and feel the direction you are walking. Over time, however, small errors in step counting accumulate and you drift away from your true position. This is exactly like **wheel odometry** in a robot.
- **External sensing (exteroception):** You can hear echoes, feel walls, or recognize landmarks (a statue, a painting). Each time you sense a known landmark, you correct your estimated position. This is exactly like a robot using a **LIDAR scan** or **camera image** to match against a stored map.

**Localization** is the brain that fuses these two streams of information. The dead reckoning gives smooth, continuous updates but drifts; the external sensing gives absolute corrections but is sparse and noisy. The mathematical framework (Kalman Filter, Particle Filter) optimally balances them — much like a sailor using both a compass (drifts) and a GPS fix (noisy but absolute).

### 1.3 Why Localization is Hard

> [!WARNING]
> **The Three Hates of Robotics (Sebastian Thrun's Lemma):**
> 1. Robots are **noisy** — wheels slip, encoders miss counts.
> 2. Environments are **partially observable** — sensors cannot see through walls.
> 3. Robot models are **approximations** — the real physics never perfectly matches the equations.

These three facts force us to treat localization as a **probabilistic inference problem** rather than a deterministic one.

### 1.4 Classification of Localization Problems

| Type | Initial Knowledge | Difficulty | Common Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | Robot's initial pose is known | Moderate (small uncertainty) | EKF, UKF |
| **Global Localization** | Initial pose unknown | High | Particle Filter, Monte Carlo Localization |
| **Kidnapped Robot** | Robot is teleported to unknown location | Highest | MCL with random restart |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Differential Drive Kinematic Model

A differential drive robot has two wheels of radius $r$ separated by an axle of length $L$ (also called the wheel-base or track). Each wheel is driven by an independent motor. The instantaneous motion is governed entirely by the velocities of the two wheels.

**Step 1 — Control Inputs:**
- $v_L$ : linear velocity of the left wheel (m/s)
- $v_R$ : linear velocity of the right wheel (m/s)

**Step 2 — Compute the Robot's Linear and Angular Velocities:**
- The robot's instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) lies on the axle. The mean of the two wheel velocities gives the forward velocity, while their difference (scaled by axle length) gives the rotation rate.

**Step 3 — Compute the Pose Rate of Change:**
- The robot's heading $\theta$ dictates the direction in which $v$ acts in the world frame.

### 2.2 Three Coordinate Frames in Localization

> [!NOTE]
> **Frame Hierarchy in Mobile Robotics:**
> 1. **World / Global Frame** $(X_W, Y_W)$ — fixed map coordinates.
> 2. **Robot Frame** $(X_R, Y_R)$ — attached to the robot's center; $X_R$ points forward.
> 3. **Sensor Frame** $(X_S, Y_S)$ — attached to each individual sensor (LIDAR, camera).

Localization ultimately aims to recover the **transformation** between the world frame and the robot frame at every time step.

### 2.3 Probabilistic Formulation (Bayes Filter)

The robot's belief about its pose is a posterior probability density:

$$
bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})
$$

where $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$ are all measurements up to time $t$ and $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$ are all control inputs up to time $t$. The recursive Bayes filter has two stages:

- **Prediction step (motion update):** propagates belief using the control input and motion model $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$.
- **Correction step (measurement update):** sharpens belief using the sensor model $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t)$.

### 2.4 The Two Principal Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Localization:** assumes Gaussian belief and linearized motion/measurement models. Best for *position tracking* where uncertainty is small.
> **Monte Carlo Localization / Particle Filter (MCL):** represents belief by a set of weighted samples. Best for *global localization* and can represent multi-modal distributions.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | Forward linear velocity of robot | m/s |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | Angular velocity of robot | rad/s |
| 3 | $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$ | World-frame x-velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$ | World-frame y-velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Heading rate | rad/s |
| 6 | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$ | Mean wheel displacement | m |
| 7 | $\Delta \theta = \dfrac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L}$ | Change in heading | rad |
| 8 | $x' = x + \Delta s \cos\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | Odometry x-update | m |
| 9 | $y' = y + \Delta s \sin\!\left(\theta + \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | Odometry y-update | m |
| 10 | $\theta' = \theta + \Delta\theta$ | Odometry heading-update | rad |
| 11 | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ | EKF mean prediction | Pose vector |
| 12 | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + V_t M_t V_t^T$ | EKF covariance prediction | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| 13 | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | $3 \times m$ matrix |
| 14 | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$ | EKF mean correction | Pose vector |
| 15 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\,\bar{\Sigma}_t$ | EKF covariance correction | $3 \times 3$ matrix |
| 16 | $w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | Particle importance weight | Normalized |
| 17 | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | Effective sample size | Threshold ≈ $N/2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In the formulas above, $\Sigma$ denotes the **3×3 pose covariance matrix**, $G_t$ and $V_t$ are the **Jacobians** of the motion model with respect to state and control, $H_t$ is the **Jacobian** of the measurement model, and $Q_t$ and $M_t$ are the measurement and control noise covariances respectively.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Differential drive localization is the workhorse of:
- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Amazon, FedEx, and Kiva systems use differential drive bases with Monte Carlo localization on a pre-built map.
- **Indoor Service Robots:** Cleaning robots (iRobot Roomba) and hospitality robots use EKF/MCL on 2D LIDAR scans.
- **Autonomous Wheelchairs and Mobile Manipulators:** Must precisely localize before interacting with humans.
- **Last-Mile Delivery Robots:** Starship, Nuro use differential drive bases with GPS + IMU + LIDAR fusion.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a Differential Drive Robot

**Problem statement:** Given wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ (rad/s) and wheel radius $r$, derive the robot's linear velocity $v$, angular velocity $\omega$, and the equations of motion for the pose $(x, y, \theta)$.

**Step 1 — Relate wheel angular velocity to wheel linear velocity.**
Each wheel rolls without slipping. The linear velocity at the contact point of the left wheel is:

$$
v_L = r \cdot \omega_L
$$

Similarly for the right wheel:

$$
v_R = r \cdot \omega_R
$$

*Logic:* The arc length covered per second by a wheel rotating at $\omega$ with radius $r$ equals $r\omega$.

**Step 2 — Sum the wheel velocities to get the body's forward velocity.**
The body of the robot moves forward at the average of the two wheel velocities (the wheels pull the body in the same direction along the axle):

$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}
$$

*Logic:* The two wheel contact points move at $v_L$ and $v_R$ in the same direction; the body, fixed at the midpoint of the axle, moves at the average.

**Step 3 — Difference the wheel velocities to get angular velocity.**
The right and left wheels are separated by distance $L$. The differential velocity creates a rotation about a point on the axle. The arc-length difference per unit time divided by the axle length gives the angular rate:

$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

*Logic:* Imagine the axle as a rigid bar. If the right end moves forward by $v_R$ and the left by $v_L$, the bar rotates at rate $\omega = \Delta v / L$.

**Step 4 — Express the pose dynamics in the world frame.**
The robot's body frame has its x-axis aligned with the forward direction. The forward velocity $v$ in the world frame is rotated by the heading $\theta$:

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

*Logic:* The vector $(v, 0)$ in the robot frame becomes $(v\cos\theta, v\sin\theta)$ in the world frame after rotation by angle $\theta$.

**Step 5 — Continuous-time state-space form.**
Combining the above, the kinematic model in matrix form is:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & 0 \\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **unicycle model**, the canonical motion model for a differential drive robot.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Odometry Update (Discrete-Time Integration)

Wheel encoders report incremental wheel rotations $\Delta \phi_L$ and $\Delta \phi_R$ over a sampling interval $\Delta t$. From these we compute the increments of the wheel contact points:

$$
\Delta s_L = r \cdot \Delta \phi_L, \quad \Delta s_R = r \cdot \Delta \phi_R
$$

**Step 1 — Mean traveled distance and change in heading.**

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}, \quad \Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Update the pose (mid-point integration).**
A more accurate integration than the simple Euler step is to use the heading at the **midpoint** of the arc:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The robot travels an arc of length $\Delta s$ and rotates by $\Delta\theta$. The tangent to the arc at the midpoint makes an angle $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$ with the world x-axis. Integrating along this tangent gives the displacement.

**Step 3 — Wrap heading to $(-\pi, \pi]$** to maintain numerical stability:

$$
\theta_{t+1} \gets \mathrm{atan2}\!\left(\sin\theta_{t+1},\, \cos\theta_{t+1}\right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Always state that odometry suffers from **unbounded drift** because errors in $\Delta s_L$ and $\Delta s_R$ accumulate. The covariance of the pose grows without limit if no external corrections are applied.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: EKF Localization — Prediction Step

Let the state be $\mu_{t-1} = [x, y, \theta]^T$ with covariance $\Sigma_{t-1}$. The control input is $u_t = [v, \omega]^T$ with covariance $M_t$ (the control noise matrix).

**Step 1 — Predicted mean.** Apply the motion model $g(\mu, u)$:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = \mu_{t-1} +
\begin{bmatrix}
\Delta t \cdot v \cos\theta_{t-1} \\
\Delta t \cdot v \sin\theta_{t-1} \\
\Delta t \cdot \omega
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobians.** The motion model is nonlinear in $\theta$, so we linearize it.

Jacobian of $g$ w.r.t. the state $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \theta]^T$:

$$
G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial \mathbf{x}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -\Delta t \cdot v \sin\theta_{t-1} \\
0 & 1 & \;\;\Delta t \cdot v \cos\theta_{t-1} \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

*Logic:* Differentiate each row of $g$ w.r.t. each component of $\mathbf{x}$. Only the trigonometric terms depend on $\theta$, producing the sine/cosine entries in the third column.

Jacobian of $g$ w.r.t. the control $\mathbf{u} = [v, \omega]^T$:

$$
V_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial \mathbf{u}} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1} & 0 \\
\Delta t \sin\theta_{t-1} & 0 \\
0 & \Delta t
\end{bmatrix}
$$

*Logic:* Differentiate the motion update w.r.t. $v$ (produces the $\cos$ and $\sin$ terms in column 1) and w.r.t. $\omega$ (produces $\Delta t$ in the third row, column 2).

**Step 3 — Predicted covariance.**

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^T + V_t \, M_t \, V_t^T
$$

*Logic:* The first term propagates the old state uncertainty through the linearized motion; the second term adds new uncertainty from noisy controls.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: EKF Localization — Correction Step

Suppose a sensor returns a measurement $z_t$ and the measurement model is $z = h(\mathbf{x}) + \text{noise}$, with measurement noise covariance $Q_t$.

**Step 1 — Jacobian of the measurement model** (linearization at the predicted mean):

$$
H_t = \left.\frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|_{\bar{\mu}_t}
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation (measurement residual).**

$$
\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)
$$

*Logic:* Difference between the actual measurement and the measurement predicted from the predicted pose.

**Step 3 — Innovation covariance.**

$$
S_t = H_t \, \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T + Q_t
$$

**Step 4 — Kalman gain.** The gain is chosen to minimize the posterior covariance:

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Updated mean and covariance.**

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \nu_t
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\,\bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

*Logic:* The mean is shifted in the direction of the innovation, weighted by the Kalman gain. The covariance is reduced in the directions where the measurement is informative, but can never go below the noise floor imposed by $Q_t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Result:** The Kalman gain $K_t$ is large when measurement noise is small (we trust the sensor) and small when state uncertainty is large (we have not propagated enough motion to constrain the pose).

---

### 3.5 Numerical Example Worked Out

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (typical KTU problem):**
> A differential drive robot with wheel-base $L = 0.5\,\text{m}$ and wheel radius $r = 0.1\,\text{m}$ has wheel encoders reporting $\Delta\phi_L = 0.50\,\text{rad}$ and $\Delta\phi_R = 0.70\,\text{rad}$ over one time step. The current pose is $(x, y, \theta) = (1.0\,\text{m},\, 2.0\,\text{m},\, 30°)$. Compute the new pose.

**Step 1 — Wheel displacements.**

$$
\Delta s_L = r \Delta\phi_L = 0.1 \times 0.50 = 0.05\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\Delta s_R = r \Delta\phi_R = 0.1 \times 0.70 = 0.07\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Mean distance and heading change.**

$$
\Delta s = \frac{0.05 + 0.07}{2} = 0.06\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{0.07 - 0.05}{0.5} = 0.04\,\text{rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Midpoint heading.**

$$
\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} = 30° + \frac{0.04 \times 180/\pi}{2} \approx 30° + 1.146° = 31.146°
$$

**Step 4 — Apply pose update.**

$$
x' = 1.0 + 0.06 \cos(31.146°) = 1.0 + 0.06 \times 0.856 = 1.0513\,\text{m}
$$

$$
y' = 2.0 + 0.06 \sin(31.146°) = 2.0 + 0.06 \times 0.517 = 2.0310\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\theta' = 30° + 0.04 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 30° + 2.292° = 32.292°
$$

**Final pose:** $(1.0513\,\text{m},\ 2.0310\,\text{m},\ 32.292°)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Differential Drive Robot Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    leftWheel["Left Wheel r 0.1m vL m/s"]
    rightWheel["Right Wheel r 0.1m vR m/s"]
    axle["Axle L 0.5m"]
    body["Robot Body Center of Mass"]
    encoderL["Encoder Left"]
    encoderR["Encoder Right"]
    lidar["LIDAR 2D Scan"]
    imu["IMU 9 axis"]
    ctrl["Onboard Computer ROS2"]

    leftWheel --- axle
    rightWheel --- axle
    axle --- body
    encoderL --- leftWheel
    encoderR --- rightWheel
    lidar --- body
    imu --- body
    encoderL --> ctrl
    encoderR --> ctrl
    lidar --> ctrl
    imu --> ctrl
    ctrl -.Velocity Commands.-> leftWheel
    ctrl -.Velocity Commands.-> rightWheel
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — EKF Localization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Initial Pose mu0 Sigma0"] --> B["Read Encoders dPhiL dPhiR"]
    B --> C["Compute Delta s and Delta theta"]
    C --> D["Motion Model g mu u"]
    D --> E["Linearize Compute Gt and Vt"]
    E --> F["Predicted Mean mu bar t"]
    E --> G["Predicted Covariance Sigma bar t"]
    H["LIDAR or Beacon Measurement zt"] --> I["Measurement Model h x"]
    I --> J["Compute Innovation vt"]
    F --> J
    G --> K["Innovation Covariance St"]
    I --> K
    K --> L["Kalman Gain Kt"]
    G --> L
    L --> M["Update Mean mu t"]
    J --> M
    L --> N["Update Covariance Sigma t"]
    M --> O["Publish Pose to Navigation Stack"]
    N --> O
    O --> A
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Particle Set at t minus 1"] --> B["Prediction: Sample from Motion Model"]
    B --> C["Predicted Particle Set"]
    H["Sensor Scan zt"] --> I["Beam or Likelihood Field Model"]
    I --> J["Compute Weight wt for Each Particle"]
    C --> J
    J --> K["Weighted Particle Set"]
    K --> L{"Neff less than N over 2"}
    L -- Yes --> M["Resample with Replacement"]
    L -- No --> N["Keep Particles"]
    M --> O["Output Pose Estimate Best Particle or Weighted Mean"]
    N --> O
    O --> A
```

### 4.4 Coordinate Frame Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W["World Frame XW YW Fixed Global"]
    R["Robot Frame XR YR Origin at Robot Center XR forward"]
    S["Sensor Frame XS YS Origin at Sensor Mount"]
    P["Pose Transform TWR = x y theta"]

    W -- P --> R
    R -- TRS --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The pose $T_{WR}$ is a 2D rigid-body transform from world to robot frame. A point $p^R$ in the robot frame is transformed to the world frame by $p^W = R(\theta)\,p^R + t$ where $R(\theta)$ is the 2D rotation matrix and $t = (x, y)^T$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define localization of a mobile robot. Differentiate between position tracking and global localization.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Localization is the process of estimating the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ within a known environment using sensor data. **Position tracking** assumes the initial pose is approximately known and the problem is to refine it over time; uncertainty is small and unimodal, so an EKF is typically sufficient. **Global localization** assumes the initial pose is completely unknown; the belief may be multi-modal, requiring methods like Monte Carlo Localization (particle filters) that can maintain multiple hypotheses simultaneously. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for differentiation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Write the kinematic model equations of a differential drive robot and explain the role of the wheel-base $L$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The kinematic model is given by:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

where $v = (v_L + v_R)/2$ and $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$. The wheel-base $L$ is the distance between the two drive wheels. It acts as the **denominator** in the angular velocity equation, meaning a longer wheel-base produces smaller $\omega$ for the same wheel-velocity difference, and vice versa. Geometrically, the wheel-base defines the radius of curvature of the robot's instantaneous circular motion. *(3 marks — 1 for equations, 2 for role of L)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematics of a differential drive robot relating wheel angular velocities $(\omega_L, \omega_R)$ to the robot's linear and angular velocities $(v, \omega)$. Express the result in state-space form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a differential drive robot with $L = 0.4\,\text{m}$ and $r = 0.05\,\text{m}$, the encoders report $\Delta\phi_L = 1.2\,\text{rad}$ and $\Delta\phi_R = 1.8\,\text{rad}$ during a sampling interval. If the current pose is $(x, y, \theta) = (0, 0, 0°)$, compute the new pose using the midpoint integration method. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Forward Kinematics Derivation (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Relate wheel linear and angular velocities: $v_L = r\omega_L$, $v_R = r\omega_R$.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Forward velocity is the average $v = (v_L + v_R)/2 = r(\omega_L + \omega_R)/2$. Angular velocity arises from the velocity difference across the axle $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** The body-frame forward velocity $v$ must be rotated by the heading $\theta$ to yield world-frame velocities: $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** State-space form:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & 0 \\
\sin\theta & 0 \\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Wheel displacements: $\Delta s_L = 0.05 \times 1.2 = 0.06\,\text{m}$, $\Delta s_R = 0.05 \times 1.8 = 0.09\,\text{m}$.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Mean distance and heading change: $\Delta s = (0.06 + 0.09)/2 = 0.075\,\text{m}$, $\Delta\theta = (0.09 - 0.06)/0.4 = 0.075\,\text{rad}$.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Midpoint heading: $\theta + \Delta\theta/2 = 0 + 0.0375\,\text{rad} = 2.149°$.
- **Step 4 (3 marks):** Pose update:
  - $x' = 0 + 0.075 \cos(0.0375) = 0.075 \times 0.99930 = 0.07495\,\text{m}$
  - $y' = 0 + 0.075 \sin(0.0375) = 0.075 \times 0.03749 = 0.00281\,\text{m}$
  - $\theta' = 0 + 0.075\,\text{rad} = 4.297°$

**Final pose:** $(0.0749\,\text{m},\ 0.0028\,\text{m},\ 4.30°)$.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) based localization algorithm for a differential drive robot. Write the prediction and update equations clearly and state the role of the Jacobians $G_t$, $V_t$, and $H_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot uses a 2D LIDAR to detect a known landmark at world position $(x_L, y_L) = (5, 5)\,\text{m}$. The measurement model is $z = h(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x - 5)^2 + (y - 5)^2} \\ \mathrm{atan2}(y - 5,\, x - 5) - \theta \end{bmatrix}$, i.e., range and bearing to the landmark. Derive the measurement Jacobian $H_t$ at the predicted pose $\bar{\mu}_t = (4, 5, 0)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EKF Localization (7 marks):**

The EKF maintains a Gaussian belief $bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$. It alternates between **prediction** (using the motion model) and **correction** (using the measurement model).

- **Prediction (3 marks):**
  - $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ — predicted mean from the motion model.
  - $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + V_t M_t V_t^T$ — predicted covariance.
  - $G_t = \partial g / \partial \mathbf{x}$ linearizes the motion model w.r.t. the state; $V_t = \partial g / \partial \mathbf{u}$ linearizes w.r.t. the control; $M_t$ is control noise covariance.
- **Correction (3 marks):**
  - $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ — innovation.
  - $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ — Kalman gain; $H_t = \partial h / \partial \mathbf{x}$ linearizes the measurement model.
  - $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$ and $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\bar{\Sigma}_t$.
- **Role summary (1 mark):** $G_t, V_t$ propagate state/control uncertainty; $H_t$ projects state uncertainty into the measurement space so that the residual $\nu_t$ can be properly weighted.

**(b) Measurement Jacobian (7 marks):**

Let $\Delta x = x - 5$ and $\Delta y = y - 5$ and $r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$. The two components of $h$ are $h_1 = r$ and $h_2 = \mathrm{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$.

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Partial derivatives of $h_1 = r$:
  - $\partial r / \partial x = \Delta x / r$, $\partial r / \partial y = \Delta y / r$, $\partial r / \partial \theta = 0$.
- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Partial derivatives of $h_2 = \phi - \theta$ where $\phi = \mathrm{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x)$:
  - $\partial \phi / \partial x = -\Delta y / r^2$, $\partial \phi / \partial y = \Delta x / r^2$, $\partial \phi / \partial \theta = 0$.
  - So $\partial h_2 / \partial x = -\Delta y / r^2$, $\partial h_2 / \partial y = \Delta x / r^2$, $\partial h_2 / \partial \theta = -1$.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Evaluate at $\bar{\mu}_t = (4, 5, 0)$: $\Delta x = -1$, $\Delta y = 0$, $r = 1$, $r^2 = 1$.
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute:

$$
H_t = \begin{bmatrix}
-1 / 1 & 0 / 1 & 0 \\
-0 / 1 & -1 / 1 & -1
\end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
-1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & -1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the final measurement Jacobian matrix at the specified pose.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting units** on $L$, $r$, $v$, $\omega$ — costs **0.5 to 1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing $v$ (robot velocity) with $v_L$ or $v_R$ (wheel velocities)** — examiners deduct heavily for this conceptual blur.
> 3. **Omitting the Jacobian derivations** in EKF questions — even if the final equation is correct, partial-credit is lost without the linearization steps.
> 4. **Not wrapping the heading angle** to $(-\pi, \pi]$ after the update — this is a common numerical pitfall that examiners check.
> 5. **Mixing up $G_t$ and $H_t$**: $G_t$ is the motion-model Jacobian (state), $H_t$ is the measurement-model Jacobian. Swapping them is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 6. **Skipping the control noise matrix $M_t$ in the covariance prediction** — leads to an under-estimation of uncertainty and an incomplete answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Localization of a Differential Drive Robot:**

- **Robot state:** $(x, y, \theta)$ in the world frame — 3 degrees of freedom on a plane.
- **Two wheels, one axle:** Differential drive is **non-holonomic** — it cannot move sideways.
- **Forward kinematics:** $v = (v_L + v_R)/2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$ — memorize these.
- **Unicycle model:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ — canonical motion model.
- **Odometry drift:** Encoder-based pose estimation **always drifts**; uncertainty grows without bound.
- **Midpoint integration** is more accurate than simple Euler for arc trajectories.
- **EKF Localization:** Gaussian assumption; works only for unimodal, small-uncertainty beliefs.
- **EKF Prediction equations:** $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ and $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + V_t M_t V_t^T$.
- **EKF Correction equations:** $K_t$, $\mu_t$, $\Sigma_t$ — memorize the full triplet.
- **Jacobians:** $G_t$ (motion w.r.t. state), $V_t$ (motion w.r.t. control), $H_t$ (measurement w.r.t. state).
- **Particle Filter / MCL:** for global localization and the kidnapped-robot problem; handles multi-modal beliefs.
- **Resampling:** Triggered when $N_{eff} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$ falls below a threshold (typically $N/2$).
- **Sensor models:** Beam-range model (accurate, slow) vs. likelihood field model (fast, approximate).
- **Wrap angle to $(-\pi, \pi]$** after every update — prevents numerical issues.
- **Map requirement:** All localization algorithms assume a **known map** (or a SLAM front-end building one).
- **Real-world sensors used:** Wheel encoders, IMU, 2D LIDAR, monocular/stereo cameras, GPS, UWB beacons, AprilTags.
